topics.title: Food Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Analysis** is the discipline of analytical chemistry applied to the qualitative and quantitative determination of the chemical composition, physical properties, microbiological status, and structural characteristics of food materials, food ingredients, and finished food products, employing validated physico-chemical, biochemical, and instrumental techniques governed by national and international regulatory standards.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 B.Tech (Food Technology) Outcome-Based Education framework**, Food Analysis encompasses:

- **Qualitative Analysis** – identification of the *type* of constituent (e.g., is this a reducing sugar or a non-reducing sugar?)
- **Quantitative Analysis** – determination of the *amount* of constituent (e.g., how much vitamin C is present per 100 g of sample?)
- **Structural Analysis** – elucidation of molecular architecture (e.g., fatty acid profile, protein conformation)
- **Functional Analysis** – measurement of technological properties (e.g., emulsifying capacity, foaming stability)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Food Analysis like a "Medical Check-up for Food."**

Just as a doctor examines a patient through blood tests, X-rays, and scans to determine health status, a food analyst examines food through chemical assays, spectroscopic scans, and chromatographic separations to determine its quality, safety, and nutritional value.

| Medical Diagnosis Analogy | Food Analysis Counterpart |
|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Patient blood sample | Food homogenate / extract |
| Complete Blood Count (CBC) | Proximate analysis (moisture, protein, fat, ash, carbohydrate) |
| Blood glucose test | Sugar estimation by Lane-Eynon / DNS method |
| MRI / CT Scan | NMR / FTIR spectroscopy |
| Biopsy report | HPLC / GC-MS chromatogram |
| Doctor's prescription | AOAC / FSSAI / Codex standard compliance report |

## 1.3 Objectives of Food Analysis

The **prime objectives** that any food analyst must satisfy are:

1. **Quality Assurance (QA)** – Confirming that food conforms to declared specifications (nutritional label verification).
2. **Safety Assessment** – Detecting contaminants, toxins, pesticide residues, microbial pathogens, and adulterants.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** – Meeting the legal limits prescribed under the **Food Safety and Standards Act (FSSAI, 2006)**, **Codex Alimentarius**, **BIS**, and **ISO 17025** standards.
4. **Shelf-life & Stability Studies** – Establishing expiry dates via accelerated and real-time stability testing.
5. **Process Control** – In-line / at-line monitoring during manufacturing to ensure batch-to-batch consistency.
6. **Research & Development (R&D)** – Supporting the development of novel food products, fortification strategies, and processing techniques.
7. **Authentication & Adulteration Detection** – Verifying the authenticity of premium products (e.g., basmati rice purity, honey adulteration with sugar syrup, olive oil adulteration with cheaper oils).
8. **Trade & Export Compliance** – Generating analytical certificates for international food trade.

## 1.4 Scope of Food Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The scope of food analysis is exceptionally broad because food itself is a *complex multi-phase, multi-component matrix* containing thousands of chemical species distributed across **solid, liquid, and gaseous phases**.

The major analytical domains within food analysis include:

- **Proximate Composition Analysis** – moisture, ash, crude protein, crude fat (ether extract), crude fibre, and carbohydrate by difference.
- **Mineral & Trace Element Analysis** – macro-minerals (Ca, P, K, Na, Mg) and micro-minerals (Fe, Zn, Cu, Se, I) by AAS, ICP-OES, ICP-MS.
- **Vitamin Analysis** – fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) and water-soluble (B-complex, C) vitamins by HPLC, UPLC, fluorimetry.
- **Pigments & Colorants Analysis** – carotenoids, chlorophylls, anthocyanins, synthetic dyes by spectrophotometry and HPLC.
- **Lipid Profiling** – fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs) by GC-FID, peroxide value, iodine value, saponification value, free fatty acids.
- **Protein & Amino Acid Analysis** – Kjeldahl nitrogen, amino acid profiling by HPLC-PITC/OPA derivatization, peptide mapping.
- **Carbohydrate Analysis** – total sugars, reducing sugars, starch, dietary fibre, non-starch polysaccharides.
- **Contaminant Analysis** – pesticide residues, mycotoxins (aflatoxin, ochratoxin), heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, As), veterinary drug residues.
- **Microbiological Analysis** – total plate count, coliforms, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, yeast & mould by cultural and molecular methods.
- **Sensory & Rheological Analysis** – texture profile analysis (TPA), viscosity, colorimetry (CIE Lab system).

## 1.5 Classification of Analytical Methods

The two umbrella classifications that students must internalize for KTU examinations are:

### A. Classical (Wet) Methods
- Based on gravimetric, volumetric, and titrimetric principles.
- Examples: Kjeldahl nitrogen estimation, Soxhlet fat extraction, Lane-Eynon titration of reducing sugars, Mohr's method for chloride.
- **Advantages:** Inexpensive, robust, do not require sophisticated instrumentation.
- **Disadvantages:** Time-consuming, low sensitivity (mg range), require large sample sizes, generate chemical waste.

### B. Instrumental Methods
- Based on the measurement of a **physical property** of the analyte (light absorption, fluorescence, electrode potential, mass-to-charge ratio, etc.).
- Examples: UV-Vis spectrophotometry, AAS, ICP-OES, HPLC, GC, FTIR, NMR, MS.
- **Advantages:** High sensitivity (µg/L to ng/L), high selectivity, rapid, automatable, multi-analyte capability.
- **Disadvantages:** Expensive, require trained analysts, need pure reference standards, matrix interferences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Historical Note for KTU:** The shift from **classical to instrumental methods** revolutionized food analysis in the late 20th century. The introduction of **Beer's Law (1852)** laid the foundation for spectrophotometry, while the development of **partition chromatography by Martin & Synge (1941, Nobel Prize 1952)** opened the door for HPLC and GC, the two most dominant analytical techniques in modern food laboratories.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Analytical Workflow — Structured Logical Steps

Every food analysis, irrespective of the analyte or technique, follows a standardized workflow that must be understood by KTU students as a logical sequence:

### Step 1 — Define the Analytical Problem
- Clearly articulate **WHAT** is to be measured, **WHY** it must be measured, and **AT WHAT LEVEL** of precision and accuracy.
- Example: "Determine the lead (Pb) content of a branded infant formula at a regulatory limit of **≤ 0.02 mg/kg** with a measurement uncertainty of ± 10%."

### Step 2 — Sampling
- Obtain a **representative sample** from the bulk lot. A poorly drawn sample invalidates even the most sophisticated analysis.
- Statistical formula for minimum sample size (see Formula Sheet below).

### Step 3 — Sample Preparation (Pre-treatment)
- Homogenization, drying, grinding, fat removal (defatting), digestion (wet or dry ashing for minerals), extraction (solvent, Soxhlet, supercritical fluid), clean-up (SPE, QuEChERS), derivatization (where required).

### Step 4 — Separation / Isolation
- Chromatographic techniques (TLC, GC, HPLC, IC, SFC) separate complex mixtures into individual components.

### Step 5 — Detection & Quantification
- The separated components are detected by a suitable detector (UV, DAD, FID, MS, ECD, FLD, ELSD, CAD) and quantified against authentic reference standards.

### Step 6 — Statistical Treatment & Reporting
- Application of calibration curves, recovery studies, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), standard deviation, relative standard deviation (RSD), and final report generation with **measurement uncertainty**.

### Step 7 — Interpretation & Decision
- Compare results against regulatory limits, specifications, or prior data to draw actionable conclusions.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it matters | How it is implemented |
|------|----------------|----------------------|
| Sampling | Heterogeneity of food lot → one bad sample = wrong decision | Random, stratified, or composite sampling per AOAC/IS standards |
| Pre-treatment | Most instruments cannot accept raw food | Grinding, digestion, extraction, derivatization |
| Separation | Food contains 1000s of compounds; detector sees only one at a time | HPLC column (C18, C8, amino, ion-exchange), GC capillary column |
| Detection | Each compound produces a unique signal | UV absorbance, fluorescence, mass spectrum, refractive index |
| Quantification | Need numerical answer, not just yes/no | Calibration curve, standard addition, internal standard method |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **one-stop cheat sheet** covering every formula that the KTU examiner expects a Food Technology student to know for Module 1. Memorize the **units and boundary conditions** alongside the equations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum sample size (statistical)** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}$ | $Z$ = confidence value (1.96 for 95%); $\sigma$ = standard deviation of property; $E$ = acceptable error | dimensionless | Determining how many sub-samples make a representative lot sample |
| 2 | **Moisture content (wet basis)** | $\% M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | $W_w$ = wet weight; $W_d$ = dry weight | % | Drying oven / moisture analyzer |
| 3 | **Moisture content (dry basis)** | $\% M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Same as above | % | Used in grain science, engineering calculations |
| 4 | **Crude protein (Kjeldahl)** | $\% \text{Protein} = \% N \times F$ | $F$ = conversion factor (6.25 for general; 5.70 for wheat; 5.18 for milk; 6.38 for casein) | % | FSSAI & AOAC standard for protein |
| 5 | **Ash content** | $\% \text{Ash} = \dfrac{W_a}{W_s} \times 100$ | $W_a$ = weight of ash; $W_s$ = weight of sample | % | Muffle furnace at 550–600 °C |
| 6 | **Carbohydrate by difference** | $\% \text{CHO} = 100 - (\%M + \%P + \%F + \%A)$ | M = moisture; P = protein; F = fat; A = ash | % | Proximate analysis completion |
| 7 | **Calorific value (Atwater factors)** | $E = 4 \times P + 9 \times F + 4 \times CHO$ | $E$ = energy (kcal/100 g); P, F, CHO in grams | kcal | Nutrition labelling |
| 8 | **Beer's Law (UV-Vis)** | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | $A$ = absorbance; $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity; $c$ = molar concentration; $l$ = path length | dimensionless | Spectrophotometric quantification |
| 9 | **Limit of Detection (LOD)** | $\text{LOD} = 3.3 \times \dfrac{\sigma}{S}$ | $\sigma$ = std dev of blank; $S$ = slope of calibration | analyte units | Method validation |
| 10 | **Limit of Quantification (LOQ)** | $\text{LOQ} = 10 \times \dfrac{\sigma}{S}$ | Same as LOD | analyte units | Method validation |
| 11 | **Recovery (%)** | $\%R = \dfrac{C_{found} - C_{native}}{C_{spiked}} \times 100$ | $C_{found}$ = concentration in spiked sample; $C_{native}$ = concentration in unspiked; $C_{spiked}$ = spiked amount | % | Accuracy validation |
| 12 | **Relative Standard Deviation (RSD)** | $\% \text{RSD} = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | $s$ = standard deviation; $\bar{x}$ = mean | % | Precision check; should be ≤ 2% for instrumental, ≤ 5% for classical |
| 13 | **Sandell's sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot MW}{1000}$ | $S$ = Sandell's sensitivity (µg/cm²); MW = molecular weight | µg/cm² | Comparing spectrophotometric methods |
| 14 | **Acid value of oil** | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | $V$ = mL of KOH; $N$ = normality; $W$ = g of oil | mg KOH/g | Oil rancidity |
| 15 | **Peroxide value** | $\text{PV} = \dfrac{(S - B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | $S$ = sample titre; $B$ = blank titre; $N$ = normality of Na₂S₂O₃; $W$ = g of oil | meq O₂/kg | Oxidative rancidity |
| 16 | **Iodine value** | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | $B$ = blank; $S$ = sample; $N$ = normality; $W$ = g of oil | g I₂/100 g | Degree of unsaturation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A common error in KTU answer sheets is confusion between **wet-basis** and **dry-basis** moisture formulas. They give **numerically different values**. Always check what the question demands and mention the basis explicitly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

The food analysis workflow is the **backbone of every food manufacturing enterprise**. Without it:

- An infant formula manufacturer cannot verify that **essential amino acids** meet Codex standards (risk of protein-energy malnutrition in infants).
- An exporter of basmati rice to the EU cannot generate the **Certificate of Analysis (CoA)** required for customs clearance.
- A dairy plant cannot guarantee the **fat and SNF (Solids-Not-Fat)** percentages claimed on the milk pouch (FSSAI mandate).
- A bottled water company cannot confirm compliance with **BIS 14543** microbiological limits.
- A pesticide residue testing laboratory cannot certify that fruits and vegetables meet **Maximum Residue Limits (MRL)** as per Codex MRL database.

Instrumental methods, in particular, have enabled:
- **High-throughput screening** of thousands of food samples per day using **LC-MS/MS** and **GC-MS/MS**.
- **Real-time in-process monitoring** using **NIR (Near-Infrared) probes** for moisture, protein, and fat in grain elevators and dairy plants.
- **Trace-level detection** (parts-per-trillion) of dioxins, PCBs, and PFAS contaminants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Minimum Number of Sub-Samples for a Representative Sample

### Problem Setup
Suppose a food analyst wishes to estimate the mean fat content of a lot of 5000 kg of peanut butter. From prior experience, the standard deviation of fat content within a lot is **σ = 0.45%**, and the analyst wishes to be **95% confident** that the estimate lies within an error of **± 0.1%** of the true mean. Determine the minimum number of sub-samples *n*.

### Step 1 — Identify the Statistical Model
For a large population where the standard deviation σ is approximately known (from historical data), the sample size required to estimate the population mean with a specified absolute error is given by the formula derived from the **central limit theorem**:

$$
n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Each Parameter
For a **two-tailed 95% confidence interval**, the standard normal deviate is:

$$
Z_{0.025} = 1.96
$$

The acceptable absolute error is given as:

$$
E = 0.1\%
$$

The known population standard deviation is:

$$
\sigma = 0.45\%
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$
n = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \times (0.45)^2}{(0.1)^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Squares Numerator
Compute $(1.96)^2$:

$$
(1.96)^2 = 3.8416
$$

Compute $(0.45)^2$:

$$
(0.45)^2 = 0.2025
$$

So the numerator becomes:

$$
3.8416 \times 0.2025 = 0.777924
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Denominator

$$
(0.1)^2 = 0.01
$$

### Step 6 — Final Division

$$
n = \dfrac{0.777924}{0.01} = 77.7924
$$

### Step 7 — Round Up to the Next Whole Number
Since we cannot take a fractional sample, **always round up**:

$$
n = 78 \text{ sub-samples}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The analyst must draw a **minimum of 78 independent sub-samples** from the 5000 kg lot, mix them to form a composite sample, and then perform the analysis. Any fewer samples would risk the estimate falling outside the ± 0.1% tolerance at 95% confidence.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Moisture Content Calculation

### Problem Statement
A 5.000 g sample of wet food is placed in a hot-air oven at 105 °C for 5 hours. After cooling in a desiccator, the residue weighs 1.250 g. Calculate:
- (i) Moisture content on wet basis (% Mwb)
- (ii) Moisture content on dry basis (% Mdb)

### Step 1 — Identify the Weights
Wet weight of sample (initial):

$$
W_w = 5.000 \text{ g}
$$

Dry weight of sample (after oven drying):

$$
W_d = 1.250 \text{ g}
$$

Weight of moisture lost:

$$
W_m = W_w - W_d = 5.000 - 1.250 = 3.750 \text{ g}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute % Moisture on Wet Basis
By definition:

$$
\% M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_m}{W_w} \times 100
$$

Substituting:

$$
\% M_{wb} = \dfrac{3.750}{5.000} \times 100 = 75.0\%
$$

### Step 3 — Compute % Moisture on Dry Basis
By definition:

$$
\% M_{db} = \dfrac{W_m}{W_d} \times 100
$$

Substituting:

$$
\% M_{db} = \dfrac{3.750}{1.250} \times 100 = 300.0\%
$$

### Step 4 — Cross-Verification Using the Relationship

$$
\% M_{db} = \dfrac{\% M_{wb}}{100 - \% M_{wb}} \times 100
$$

Verify:

$$
\% M_{db} = \dfrac{75.0}{100 - 75.0} \times 100 = \dfrac{75.0}{25.0} \times 100 = 300.0\%
$$

Both approaches agree. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **The wet basis value is ALWAYS smaller than the dry basis value** because the denominator is larger. In the food industry, moisture content is overwhelmingly reported on a **wet basis** because it is intuitive ("75% of this food is water").

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Crude Protein by Kjeldahl Method

### Problem Statement
A 2.000 g sample of milk powder is digested, distilled, and titrated. The sample requires 18.50 mL of 0.1 N HCl, while the blank requires 0.40 mL of 0.1 N HCl. Calculate the % crude protein using a conversion factor of **6.38** (for dairy products).

### Step 1 — Recall the Kjeldahl Acid-Base Reaction Stoichiometry
The ammonium borate (or ammonia) liberated during distillation is trapped in boric acid and then titrated with standard acid:

$$
\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}
$$

Each mole of HCl consumes one mole of NH₃, which corresponds to one mole of nitrogen.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Net Titre Volume
Subtract the blank titre from the sample titre:

$$
V_{net} = V_{sample} - V_{blank} = 18.50 - 0.40 = 18.10 \text{ mL}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Milli-Equivalents of Nitrogen
One milli-equivalent (meq) of nitrogen weighs **14 mg** (atomic mass of N = 14 g/mol; equivalent weight = 14/1 = 14 for N in NH₃).

The number of meq of N = $N \times V$ (in mL):

$$
\text{meq of N} = 0.1 \times 18.10 = 1.810 \text{ meq}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Grams of Nitrogen

$$
\text{Mass of N} = 1.810 \times 14 \text{ mg} = 25.34 \text{ mg} = 0.02534 \text{ g}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate % Nitrogen in the Sample

$$
\% N = \dfrac{0.02534}{2.000} \times 100 = 1.267\%
$$

### Step 6 — Multiply by the Conversion Factor

$$
\% \text{Protein} = 1.267 \times 6.38 = 8.083\%
$$

### Step 7 — Express with Appropriate Significant Figures

$$
\% \text{Protein} \approx 8.08\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Anchor:** The Kjeldahl conversion factor depends on the nitrogen content of the specific protein. Different conversion factors exist because proteins have different amino acid compositions:
> - Wheat flour (gluten): **5.70**
> - General foods (mixed): **6.25**
> - Milk and dairy products (casein): **6.38**
> - Rice: **5.95**
> - Soy: **5.71**
> - Eggs: **6.25**

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Beer's Law Calibration

### Problem Statement
A standard solution of **β-carotene** in hexane has a molar absorptivity (ε) of **139,000 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹** at 450 nm. The spectrophotometer cell has a path length of **1.0 cm**. Calculate the absorbance expected for a solution of concentration **5 × 10⁻⁶ mol/L**. If the measured absorbance is **0.685**, calculate the actual concentration and the % error.

### Step 1 — State Beer's Law

$$
A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Theoretical Absorbance

$$
A_{theoretical} = 139{,}000 \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.0
$$

$$
A_{theoretical} = 139{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.695
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the % Error in Measured Absorbance

$$
\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{|A_{measured} - A_{theoretical}|}{A_{theoretical}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{|0.685 - 0.695|}{0.695} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.010}{0.695} \times 100 = 1.439\%
$$

### Step 4 — Back-Calculate Actual Concentration
Using $A_{measured} = 0.685$:

$$
c_{actual} = \dfrac{A_{measured}}{\varepsilon \cdot l} = \dfrac{0.685}{139{,}000 \times 1.0} = 4.928 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/L}
$$

### Step 5 — Comment on the Result
A 1.44% error is well within acceptable analytical tolerance (typically ± 5%), confirming that the spectrophotometer is performing correctly and the Beer-Lambert law is obeyed at this concentration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Beer-Lambert Limitations:** Beer's Law holds only at low to moderate concentrations (typically **A ≤ 1.0**). At high concentrations, **deviations occur due to electrostatic interactions between solute molecules, scattering, and chemical changes**. Students who blindly extrapolate calibration curves to A > 1.0 lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Comprehensive Food Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **seven-stage analytical workflow** that governs every food analysis laboratory. This block-level architecture helps visualize the interconnections between sampling, preparation, separation, detection, and reporting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["STAGE 1: PROBLEM DEFINITION"] --> node2["STAGE 2: REPRESENTATIVE SAMPLING"]
    node2 --> node3["STAGE 3: SAMPLE PREPARATION AND PRE-TREATMENT"]
    node3 --> node4["STAGE 4: SEPARATION AND ISOLATION"]
    node4 --> node5["STAGE 5: DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION"]
    node5 --> node6["STAGE 6: STATISTICAL TREATMENT AND VALIDATION"]
    node6 --> node7["STAGE 7: INTERPRETATION AND DECISION"]
    node7 --> node8["REGULATORY COMPLIANCE REPORT"]

    node3a["Homogenization"] -.-> node3
    node3b["Drying and Grinding"] -.-> node3
    node3c["Digestion and Extraction"] -.-> node3
    node3d["Derivatization"] -.-> node3

    node4a["HPLC and UPLC"] -.-> node4
    node4b["GC and GC-MS"] -.-> node4
    node4c["TLC and HPTLC"] -.-> node4
    node4d["Ion Chromatography"] -.-> node4

    node5a["UV-Vis Detector"] -.-> node5
    node5b["Mass Spectrometer"] -.-> node5
    node5c["FID and ECD"] -.-> node5
    node5d["FTIR and NIR"] -.-> node5

    node6a["LOD and LOQ"] -.-> node6
    node6b["Recovery Studies"] -.-> node6
    node6c["Precision and Accuracy"] -.-> node6

    classDef stage fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detail fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class node1,node2,node3,node4,node5,node6,node7,node8 stage
    class node3a,node3b,node3c,node3d,node4a,node4b,node4c,node4d,node5a,node5b,node5c,node5d,node6a,node6b,node6c detail
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Food Analysis Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["FOOD ANALYSIS METHODS"]

    root --> classical["CLASSICAL WET METHODS"]
    root --> instrumental["INSTRUMENTAL METHODS"]

    classical --> c1["Gravimetric Analysis"]

    classical --> c4["Polarimetry"]

    c1 --> c1a["Example: Ash determination by muffle furnace"]
    c2 --> c2a["Example: Kjeldahl protein estimation"]
    c2 --> c2b["Example: Acid value of oils"]

    instrumental --> i1["Spectroscopic Methods"]
    instrumental --> i2["Chromatographic Methods"]
    instrumental --> i3["Electroanalytical Methods"]
    instrumental --> i4["Hyphenated Methods"]
    instrumental --> i5["Thermal and Other Methods"]

    i1 --> i1a["UV-Vis Spectrophotometry"]
    i1 --> i1b["Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy AAS"]
    i1 --> i1c["ICP-OES and ICP-MS"]
    i1 --> i1d["FTIR and NIR Spectroscopy"]
    i1 --> i1e["Fluorescence Spectroscopy"]

    i2 --> i2a["HPLC and UPLC"]
    i2 --> i2b["Gas Chromatography GC"]
    i2 --> i2c["Ion Chromatography IC"]
    i2 --> i2d["Supercritical Fluid Chromatography SFC"]

    i3 --> i3a["pH Meter and Ion Selective Electrodes"]
    i3 --> i3b["Conductometry"]
    i3 --> i3c["Potentiometry and Voltammetry"]

    i4 --> i4a["GC-MS and LC-MS"]
    i4 --> i4b["HPLC-MS-MS Tandem"]
    i4 --> i4c["GC-IR and LC-NMR"]

    i5 --> i5a["Differential Scanning Calorimetry DSC"]

    i5 --> i5c["Electrophoresis SDS-PAGE"]

    class c1a,c2a,c2b,c4a exCls
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Proximate Analysis Flow (Weende Method)

The **Weende Method**, developed at the Weende Experiment Station in Germany (1860), remains the cornerstone of proximate food analysis and is frequently tested in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sample["RAW FOOD SAMPLE 100 g"]
    sample --> m1["MOISTURE DETERMINATION: 105 C OVEN DRYING"]
    sample --> m2["ASH DETERMINATION: 550 C MUFFLE FURNACE"]
    sample --> m3["CRUDE FAT: SOXHLET EXTRACTION WITH PETROLEUM ETHER"]
    sample --> m4["CRUDE PROTEIN: KJELDAHL N x 6.25"]
    sample --> m5["CRUDE FIBRE: ACID-ALKALI DIGESTION"]
    sample --> m6["CARBOHYDRATE: BY DIFFERENCE 100 - M+P+F+A"]

    m1 --> o1["% Moisture"]
    m2 --> o2["% Ash or Mineral Matter"]
    m3 --> o3["% Crude Fat or Ether Extract"]
    m4 --> o4["% Crude Protein"]
    m5 --> o5["% Crude Fibre"]
    m6 --> o6["% Nitrogen Free Extractives NFE"]

    o1 --> final["PROXIMATE COMPOSITION REPORT"]
    o2 --> final
    o3 --> final
    o4 --> final
    o5 --> final
    o6 --> final

    classDef inCls fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procCls fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outCls fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef finalCls fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class sample inCls
    class m1,m2,m3,m4,m5,m6 procCls
    class o1,o2,o3,o4,o5,o6 outCls
    class final finalCls
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Method Validation Workflow

Method validation is **mandatory** under ISO/IEC 17025 and is a high-yield KTU topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["DEVELOPED ANALYTICAL METHOD"]

    start --> param1["ACCURACY: Recovery studies 80 to 120 percent"]
    start --> param2["PRECISION: Repeatability and Reproducibility"]
    start --> param3["LINEARITY: R squared >= 0.99 over working range"]
    start --> param4["SELECTIVITY and SPECIFICITY"]
    start --> param5["LOD and LOQ"]
    start --> param6["ROBUSTNESS: Deliberate small variations"]
    start --> param7["RANGE: 80 to 120 percent of test concentration"]
    start --> param8["MEASUREMENT UNCERTAINTY: GUM approach"]

    param1 --> decision{"ALL CRITERIA SATISFIED?"}
    param2 --> decision
    param3 --> decision
    param4 --> decision
    param5 --> decision
    param6 --> decision
    param7 --> decision
    param8 --> decision

    decision -->|YES| valid["METHOD VALIDATED: Ready for Routine Use"]
    decision -->|NO| revise["REVISE METHOD: Optimize and Re-validate"]

    classDef startCls fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef paramCls fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decCls fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outCls fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class start startCls
    class param1,param2,param3,param4,param5,param6,param7,param8 paramCls
    class decision decCls
    class valid,revise outCls
```
