For a Gaussian peak, the baseline width is $w_b = 4\sigma$, and $N = (t_R/\sigma)^2$, giving $w_b = 4 t_R / \sqrt{N}$.

**Step 3 — Assume similar peak widths.**

For closely eluting peaks, $w_{b1} \approx w_{b2} \approx \bar{w}$, and using $t_{R2} \approx t_{R1}$ for the denominator approximation:

$$
R_s \approx \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{\bar{w}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $t_R$ in terms of $t_M$ and $k'$.**

Using $t_R = t_M(1+k')$:

$$
t_{R2} - t_{R1} = t_M(k_2' - k_1')
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce selectivity.**

Since $\alpha = k_2'/k_1'$, we have $k_2' - k_1' = k_2'(1 - 1/\alpha) = k_2'(\alpha - 1)/\alpha$.

**Step 6 — Combine.**

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2'}{1 + k_2'}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Significance:** This derivation is rated **HIGH PRIORITY** by KTU examiners. Practise writing the chain of substitutions clearly — the marks are awarded for *each correct intermediate step*, not just the final box.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Doubling Parameters

The table below shows how resolution changes when common operational parameters are altered. This is a **favourite Part-B sub-question**.

| Parameter Changed | Effect on $R_s$ | Practical Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Double column length $L$ | $R_s$ increases by factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ | Acceptable, but doubles analysis time |
| Halve particle size $d_p$ | $H$ decreases (A and C terms drop) → $\sqrt{N}$ increases | Modern UHPLC systems use 1.7 µm particles |
| Increase $\alpha$ by 10% | Non-linear gain via $(\alpha-1)/\alpha$ | Change stationary phase or temperature |
| Increase $k'$ from 1 to 5 | Big improvement via $k'/(1+k')$ term | Adjust mobile phase strength |
| Increase $k'$ from 5 to 20 | Diminishing returns | Avoid; risk of broad peaks |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A compound elutes with $t_R = 9.2$ min on a 25 cm column. The unretained marker peaks at $t_M = 1.0$ min. The baseline peak width is $w_b = 0.8$ min. Calculate $k'$, $N$, and $H$.

**Solution:**

$$
k' = \frac{9.2 - 1.0}{1.0} = 8.2
$$

$$
N = 16 \left( \frac{9.2}{0.8} \right)^2 = 16 \times 132.25 = 2116
$$

$$
H = \frac{250 \text{ mm}}{2116} = 0.118 \text{ mm} = 118 \text{ µm}
$$

> The retention factor is well within the optimum range (2–10), and the plate height of ~118 µm is reasonable for a modern packed column (typically 10–100 µm).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Chromatographic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MOBILE PHASE RESERVOIR] --> B[PUMP / PRESSURE SYSTEM]
    B --> C[INJECTION PORT / SAMPLER]
    C --> D[COLUMN CONTAINING STATIONARY PHASE]
    D --> E[DETECTOR]
    E --> F[DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM]
    F --> G[CHROMATOGRAM OUTPUT]
    H[THERMOSTAT OVEN] -.-> D
    I[CARRIER GAS / SOLVENT] -.-> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote the *flow path* of the mobile phase carrying the analyte. Dashed arrows denote *supporting subsystems* (temperature control, supply lines). The Column is the *heart* of the system — this is where the actual separation occurs.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Detailed Sub-system Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MOBILE_SUBSYSTEM
        A1[Solvent Reservoir] --> A2[Degasser]
        A2 --> A3[Gradient Mixer]
    end
    MOBILE_SUBSYSTEM --> B1[High Pressure Pump]
    B1 --> C1[Rheodyne Injector / Autosampler]
    C1 --> D1[Guard Column]
    D1 --> D2[Analytical Column with Thermostat]
    D2 --> E1[Detector UV-VIS / RID / FLD / MS]
    E1 --> F1[Amplifier and ADC]
    F1 --> G1[Computer with Chromatography Software]
    G1 --> G2[Digital Chromatogram Display]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Chromatographic Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START - ANALYTE IDENTIFIED] --> B{Volatile AND thermally stable?}
    B -- YES --> C{Polar analyte?}
    B -- NO --> D{Aqueous solubility?}
    C -- YES --> C1[Gas Liquid Chromatography GLC]
    C -- NO --> C2[Gas Solid Chromatography GSC]
    D -- YES --> E{Molecular weight less than 2000?}
    D -- NO --> F[Size Exclusion Chromatography SEC]
    E -- YES --> G{Ionisable groups?}
    E -- NO --> F
    G -- YES --> G1[Ion Exchange Chromatography IEC]
    G -- NO --> H{Chiral or biospecific?}
    H -- YES --> H1[Affinity Chromatography]
    H -- NO --> I{Reverse phase compatible?}
    I -- YES --> I1[Reversed Phase HPLC C18]
    I -- NO --> I2[Normal Phase HPLC]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — The Separation Process Inside a Column

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[SAMPLE INJECTION AT T ZERO] --> S2[ZONE 1 MOBILE PHASE]
    S2 --> S3[ZONE 2 PARTIAL EQUILIBRATION]
    S3 --> S4[ZONE 3 SEPARATION OF A AND B]
    S4 --> S5[ZONE 4 RESOLVED BANDS]
    S5 --> S6[ZONE 5 DETECTION]
    subgraph BANDS
        B1[Analyte A LOWER K MIGRATES FASTER] 
        B2[Analyte B HIGHER K MIGRATES SLOWER]
    end
    BANDS -.-> S4
```

## 4.5 Comparative Architecture — GC vs HPLC Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GAS_CHROMATOGRAPH
        GC1[Carrier Gas Cylinder He or N2] --> GC2[Pressure Regulator]
        GC2 --> GC3[Injector Port 200 to 300 C]
        GC3 --> GC4[Capillary Column in Oven]
        GC4 --> GC5[Detector FID or TCD or MS]
    end
    subgraph LIQUID_CHROMATOGRAPH
        LC1[Solvent Reservoirs] --> LC2[Degasser and Gradient Mixer]
        LC2 --> LC3[Pump 1000 to 10000 psi]
        LC3 --> LC4[Autosampler Injector]
        LC4 --> LC5[Guard Column]
        LC5 --> LC6[Analytical Column 25 to 50 C]
        LC6 --> LC7[Detector UV or FLD or MS]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The two architectures share the *same logical flow* (mobile phase → injector → column → detector) but differ dramatically in *operating pressure* (atmospheric vs. high-pressure) and *temperature control* (GC requires isothermal oven, LC operates at or near room temperature). Recognising this structural similarity is a hallmark of a student who understands chromatographic *theory*, not just memorised protocols.

## 4.6 Theoretical Plate Cascade — Visualising Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PLATE 1] --> P2[PLATE 2] --> P3[PLATE 3] --> P4[PLATE N]
    P4 --> P5[PEAK AT DETECTOR]
    subgraph CONCEPT
        N1[Each plate represents one equilibration event between phases]
        N2[More plates imply narrower output peak]
        N3[N can be 1000 to 100000 in modern columns]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define the term **Distribution Coefficient ($K$)** in chromatography. Explain its significance in determining retention.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Distribution Coefficient** ($K$) is defined as the ratio of the equilibrium concentration of an analyte in the **stationary phase** ($C_s$) to its concentration in the **mobile phase** ($C_m$):

$$
K = \frac{C_s}{C_m}
$$

It is a *thermodynamic* quantity that determines the *retention* of an analyte. A higher $K$ indicates stronger interaction with the stationary phase, leading to longer retention times ($t_R$). Two analytes can be separated only if their $K$ values differ (i.e., selectivity factor $\alpha \neq 1$). $K$ depends on temperature and the chemical nature of the analyte and both phases. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Distinguish between **Retention Time ($t_R$)** and **Adjusted Retention Time ($t_R'$)**. Why is $t_R'$ considered more meaningful for characterising analyte-stationary phase interaction?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Retention Time ($t_R$)** — the total time elapsed between injection of the analyte and the appearance of its peak maximum at the detector. **(1 mark)**
- **Adjusted Retention Time ($t_R'$)** — the time actually spent in the stationary phase; calculated as $t_R' = t_R - t_M$, where $t_M$ is the dead time of an unretained marker. **(1 mark)**
- $t_R'$ is more meaningful because $t_R$ contains a *non-productive* component ($t_M$) representing transit through the mobile phase. $t_R'$ isolates the *productive* interaction between analyte and stationary phase, and is therefore used directly in defining the retention factor $k' = t_R'/t_M$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — `Choose any ONE of the following` **[14 Marks]**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Set A]`**

**A(a)** Explain the **van Deemter equation** in detail. Discuss each of its three terms ($A$, $B/u$, $C \cdot u$) with respect to their physical origin and the experimental variables that affect them. **(7 marks)**

**A(b)** With the aid of a suitable diagram, derive the **Purnell resolution equation** and discuss how resolution depends on efficiency, selectivity, and retention factor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — A(a)

The **van Deemter equation** expresses the *Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate* ($H$) as a function of the linear mobile-phase velocity ($u$):

$$
H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u
$$

**(i) The $A$ Term — Eddy Diffusion (Multipath Effect)** *(2 marks)*

When a solute molecule traverses a packed column, it encounters stationary-phase particles that force the mobile phase to split into countless streamlines of differing path lengths. Molecules on shorter paths elute first, those on longer paths elute later, producing band broadening.

$$
H_A = A = 2 \lambda d_p
$$

$\lambda$ = packing regularity factor (depends on packing quality)
$d_p$ = particle diameter

To minimise $A$: use *small, uniform, spherical particles* and pack the column uniformly.

**(ii) The $B/u$ Term — Longitudinal Diffusion** *(2 marks)*

Molecular diffusion along the axis of the column causes the analyte band to spread in both directions. Fick's law gives:

$$
H_B = \frac{2 D_M}{u} = \frac{B}{u}
$$

$D_M$ = diffusion coefficient of analyte in the mobile phase (larger in gases, smaller in liquids)

To minimise $B/u$: use **higher mobile-phase velocities**. This term dominates at *low* flow rates.

**(iii) The $C \cdot u$ Term — Mass Transfer Resistance** *(2 marks)*

Finite time is required for analyte molecules to transfer between the mobile and stationary phases. At high flow rates, equilibrium cannot be established instantly:

$$
C \cdot u = (C_s + C_m) \cdot u
$$

where $C_s \propto d_f^2 / D_s$ (film-thickness dependence) and $C_m \propto d_p^2 / D_m$.

To minimise $C \cdot u$: use **thin stationary-phase films** and **small particle sizes**. This term dominates at *high* flow rates.

**(iv) The Optimum Velocity** *(1 mark)*

Differentiating and equating to zero:

$$
u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}, \quad H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}
$$

A plot of $H$ versus $u$ shows a hyperbolic minimum — at low $u$, $B/u$ dominates; at high $u$, $C \cdot u$ dominates. Selecting $u_{opt}$ maximises efficiency.

---

#### Model Answer — A(b)

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.6 for plate cascade concept):** A schematic showing two adjacent Gaussian peaks A and B with retention times $t_{R1}$ and $t_{R2}$, baseline widths $w_{b1}$ and $w_{b2}$, separated by $\Delta t_R$.

**Derivation of Purnell Resolution Equation:** *(5 marks)*

**Step 1:** Resolution is defined as the *peak-to-peak separation* divided by the *average peak width*:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{(w_{b1} + w_{b2})/2}
$$

**Step 2:** Express baseline width via plate count. For Gaussian peaks, $w_b = 4 t_R / \sqrt{N}$. For closely eluting compounds, $w_{b1} \approx w_{b2}$ and $t_{R1} \approx t_{R2}$, giving:

$$
R_s \approx \frac{\Delta t_R}{w_b} = \frac{\Delta t_R \cdot \sqrt{N}}{4 t_R}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$, so $\Delta t_R = t_M(k_2' - k_1')$:

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k_2' - k_1'}{1 + k_2'}
$$

**Step 4:** Use $\alpha = k_2'/k_1'$, so $k_2' - k_1' = k_2'(\alpha - 1)/\alpha$:

$$
\boxed{R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2'}{1 + k_2'}}
$$

**Discussion of the Three Pillars:** *(2 marks)*

1. **Efficiency Term ($\sqrt{N}/4$):** Increasing column length by 4× gives 2× resolution. Smaller particle size improves $N$.
2. **Selectivity Term ($(\alpha-1)/\alpha$):** Most powerful lever — small changes in stationary-phase chemistry, temperature, or mobile-phase composition can dramatically improve $\alpha$.
3. **Retention Term ($k'/(1+k')$):** Maximum contribution when $k' \approx 5$–$10$; minimal gain beyond this.

---

### **Question B** — Alternative Choice **[14 Marks]**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Set B]`**

**B(a)** Classify chromatographic techniques based on (i) the physical state of the mobile phase, (ii) the geometry of the stationary phase, and (iii) the mechanism of separation. Give **one example** of a food-analysis application for each main type. **(7 marks)**

**B(b)** Define the **Retention Factor ($k'$)** and **Selectivity Factor ($\alpha$)**. A chromatographic separation yields the following data:

| Analyte | $t_R$ (min) | $w_b$ (min) |
|---|---|---|
| A (unretained) | 1.5 | — |
| B | 9.0 | 0.60 |
| C | 11.5 | 0.75 |

Calculate $k_B'$, $k_C'$, $\alpha$, $N_B$, $N_C$, $H_B$ (column length = 30 cm), $H_C$, and $R_s$ between B and C. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — B(a)

**(i) Classification by physical state of mobile phase:** *(2 marks)*

- **Gas Chromatography (GC):** mobile phase is an inert gas (He, N₂, H₂). Used for volatile compounds.
- **Liquid Chromatography (LC):** mobile phase is a liquid (water, methanol, acetonitrile mixtures). Used for non-volatile / thermally labile compounds.
- **Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC):** mobile phase is CO₂ above its critical point — combines features of GC and LC.

**(ii) Classification by stationary phase geometry:** *(2 marks)*

- **Planar Chromatography:** stationary phase is flat — includes **Paper Chromatography** and **Thin-Layer Chromatography (TLC)**.
- **Column Chromatography:** stationary phase is held inside a tube — includes **Packed Column** and **Open-Tubular (Capillary) Column**.

**(iii) Classification by mechanism of separation:** *(2 marks)*

| Mechanism | Principle | Food-Analysis Example |
|---|---|---|
| Adsorption | Differential adsorption on solid surface | TLC separation of carotenoids |
| Partition | Differential solubility between two liquids | HPLC of sugars |
| Ion-Exchange | Coulombic attraction to charged groups | Amino acid analysis |
| Size-Exclusion | Differential penetration into pores | Molecular weight of polysaccharides |
| Affinity | Biospecific lock-and-key | Antibody-based cleanup of mycotoxins |

**Conclusion:** *(1 mark)* — The same analyte may be analysed by different techniques; selection depends on analyte properties (volatility, polarity, thermal stability, molecular weight, ionic character).

---

#### Model Answer — B(b)

**Definitions:** *(2 marks)*

- **Retention Factor $k'$:** $k' = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$; ratio of time spent in stationary vs. mobile phase.
- **Selectivity Factor $\alpha$:** $\alpha = \dfrac{k_2'}{k_1'}$ where $k_2' > k_1'$; ratio of retention factors, measures separation potential.

**Numerical Calculations:** *(5 marks)*

Using $t_M = 1.5$ min:

$$
k_B' = \frac{9.0 - 1.5}{1.5} = 5.0
$$

$$
k_C' = \frac{11.5 - 1.5}{1.5} = \frac{10.0}{1.5} = 6.667
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{k_C'}{k_B'} = \frac{6.667}{5.0} = 1.333
$$

$$
N_B = 16 \left( \frac{9.0}{0.60} \right)^2 = 16 \times 225 = 3600
$$

$$
N_C = 16 \left( \frac{11.5}{0.75} \right)^2 = 16 \times 235.1 = 3762
$$

$$
H_B = \frac{300 \text{ mm}}{3600} = 0.0833 \text{ mm} = 83.3 \text{ µm}
$$

$$
H_C = \frac{300 \text{ mm}}{3762} = 0.0798 \text{ mm} = 79.8 \text{ µm}
$$

$$
R_s = \frac{2(11.5 - 9.0)}{0.60 + 0.75} = \frac{5.0}{1.35} = 3.70
$$

**Interpretation:** *(Bonus commentary)* — $R_s = 3.70$ indicates **baseline separation** ($R_s > 1.5$ is the conventional minimum). Both $k'$ values are within the optimal 2–10 range. The column is highly efficient ($N \approx 3700$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls on Chromatography Theory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $t_M$ from $t_R$.** In numerical problems, students frequently use $t_R$ directly instead of $t_R'$. Loss: **1–2 marks per sub-part**.
> 2. **Writing $k'$ instead of $k_2'$ in the Purnell equation.** The retention factor term must be that of the *more retained* peak. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing the $A$ term of van Deemter with the $A$ term in Beer-Lambert's law.** They are *unrelated*. Loss: **up to 3 marks**.
> 4. **Stating "longer column gives better resolution" without the $\sqrt{L}$ relationship.** Examiners expect the square-root proportionality. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Writing $H = A + B \cdot u + C/u$ instead of $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$.** The signs of velocity dependence are reversed. Loss: **3 marks**.
> 6. **Omitting units.** Even though many chromatography quantities are dimensionless, plate height $H$ has units of length — always write mm or µm. Loss: **0.5–1 mark** per occurrence.
> 7. **Drawing diagrams without labels.** An unlabelled van Deemter plot earns partial credit only. Always label axes ($H$ on Y, $u$ on X), the asymptotic branches, and the $H_{min}$ point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Chromatography Theory & Principle**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] Chromatography = separation by **differential distribution** between mobile and stationary phases.
- [ ] Stationary phase = fixed; Mobile phase = moves and carries analyte.
- [ ] Retention time $t_R$ = time from injection to peak maximum.
- [ ] Dead time $t_M$ = time for unretained marker.
- [ ] Adjusted retention time $t_R' = t_R - t_M$.
- [ ] Distribution coefficient $K = C_s/C_m$.
- [ ] Retention factor $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M = K/\beta$.
- [ ] Selectivity factor $\alpha = k_2'/k_1'$ (≥ 1).
- [ ] Number of theoretical plates $N = 16(t_R/w_b)^2 = 5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2$.
- [ ] Plate height $H = L/N$.
- [ ] Resolution $R_s = 2(t_{R2}-t_{R1})/(w_1+w_2)$.

### Equations to Memorise
- [ ] **Fundamental retention:** $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$.
- [ ] **van Deemter equation:** $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$.
- [ ] **Optimum velocity:** $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$.
- [ ] **Purnell Resolution:** $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k_2'}{1 + k_2'}$.
- [ ] **$R_s$ scales as $\sqrt{L}$.**

### Physical Interpretations
- [ ] $A$ term → Eddy diffusion (path multiplicity).
- [ ] $B/u$ term → Longitudinal (molecular) diffusion.
- [ ] $C \cdot u$ term → Mass-transfer resistance in stationary and mobile films.
- [ ] At low $u$: $B/u$ dominates. At high $u$: $C \cdot u$ dominates.

### Practical Thresholds
- [ ] Optimum $k'$ range = **2 to 10**.
- [ ] Optimum $\alpha$ for good separation = **> 1.05**.
- [ ] Minimum baseline resolution: $R_s \geq 1.5$.
- [ ] Modern LC plate count: $N = 5000$ to $15000$ (typical 25 cm column).

### Classifications
- [ ] By mobile phase: **GC, LC, SFC**.
- [ ] By geometry: **Planar (Paper, TLC), Column (Packed, Capillary)**.
- [ ] By mechanism: **Adsorption, Partition, Ion-Exchange, Size-Exclusion, Affinity, Chiral**.

### Food-Analysis Connections
- [ ] **GC** → fatty acids, volatiles, pesticide residues.
- [ ] **HPLC** → vitamins, additives, sugars, mycotoxins, colours.
- [ ] **TLC** → preliminary screening of dyes, antioxidants.
- [ ] **SFC** → lipid profiling, chiral separation of additives.

### Quick Numerical Heuristics
- [ ] To double $R_s$ → **quadruple** column length (or quadruple $N$).
- [ ] To halve $H$ → halve particle size (approximately).
- [ ] If $k' = 0$ → no retention.
- [ ] If $\alpha = 1$ → no separation is theoretically possible.

> **End of Module 2 — Chromatography: Theory and Principle**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chromatographic Parameters (Partition Coefficient, Capacity Factor, Retention and Dead Time, Resolution and Their Calculation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chromatographic Parameters (Partition Coefficient, Capacity Factor, Retention and Dead Time, Resolution and Their Calculation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Partition Coefficient ($K$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Partition Coefficient ($K$)** is defined as the ratio of the concentration of an analyte in the **stationary phase** ($C_s$) to its concentration in the **mobile phase** ($C_m$) at equilibrium, at a given temperature.

$$
K \;=\; \frac{C_s}{C_m}
$$

**Units:** Dimensionless (a pure ratio of two concentrations in identical units, typically mol/L or g/mL).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Tea Bag Experiment:**
Imagine dipping a tea bag (stationary phase) into hot water (mobile phase). The tea compounds partition themselves between the bag fibers and the surrounding water. A compound with a high $K$ value "prefers" the bag (stationary phase) — it stays trapped longer. A compound with low $K$ rushes out into the water quickly. In a **food analysis** context, this is exactly how aflatoxins separate from butter fat on a silica gel column — the fat-loving molecules cling to the stationary silica, while aflatoxins elute earlier.

---

## 1.2 Capacity Factor ($k'$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Capacity Factor (or Retention Factor) $k'$** is a measure of the *retention* of an analyte by the stationary phase. It is defined as the ratio of the *adjusted retention time* of the solute to the *dead time* of the mobile phase.

$$
k' \;=\; \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}
$$

**Where:**
- $t_R$ = **Retention time** of the analyte (time from injection to peak maximum)
- $t_M$ = **Dead time** (time for an unretained, non-interacting species to traverse the column)

**Conceptual Analogy — The Shopping Mall:**
Think of a customer (analyte) walking through a shopping mall (chromatographic column). The mall corridor is the **mobile phase** (time = $t_M$). Each shop they walk into is a "stationary phase interaction." A customer who pops into just one shop has a *low capacity factor* (leaves quickly). A customer who wanders into many shops has a *high capacity factor* (lingers long, large $k'$). In **food analysis**, vitamin A esters typically show high $k'$ values on reverse-phase columns because they love the hydrophobic C18 stationary phase.

---

## 1.3 Retention Time ($t_R$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Retention Time ($t_R$)** is the total time elapsed between the **moment of sample injection** at the column inlet and the **appearance of the peak maximum** at the detector outlet.

$$
t_R \;=\; t_M \;+\; t_S
$$

**Where $t_S$** = time the solute spends adsorbed/interacted in the stationary phase.

**Conceptual Analogy — Airport Security Check:**
You arrive at airport security (injection). The time to reach the gate (peak) is $t_R$. The time just walking through the terminal without stopping is $t_M$. The time spent at the X-ray scanner/pat-down is $t_S$. Total journey = walking time + security time.

---

## 1.4 Dead Time ($t_M$ or $t_0$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Dead Time ($t_M$)**, also called **Hold-Up Time** or **Void Time**, is the time required for the **mobile phase alone** (or an unretained, non-interacting solute) to travel from the injection point to the detector.

**Standard Practice:** Determined experimentally using an *unretained marker* such as **air/methane** in GC, or **uracil/thiourea** in HPLC.

**Conceptual Analogy:** $t_M$ is the "baseline walking time" — the time a molecule spends *exclusively* in the mobile phase, doing no chemistry, just being carried along by the flow.

---

## 1.5 Resolution ($R_s$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Resolution ($R_s$)** is a quantitative measure of the degree of *separation* between two adjacent chromatographic peaks (typically peaks 1 and 2, where $t_{R2} > t_{R1}$).

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{2\,\bigl(t_{R2} - t_{R1}\bigr)}{w_1 + w_2}
$$

**Where $w_1$, $w_2$** = baseline peak widths of peaks 1 and 2 respectively (measured in the same time units as $t_R$).

**Conceptual Analogy — Two Trains on Parallel Tracks:**
Two trains depart together. After some distance, they should arrive separately at the destination. *Resolution* measures how clearly you can tell them apart at the platform. $R_s = 1.0$ means slight overlap; $R_s \geq 1.5$ (the **"baseline separation" criterion**) means complete separation — analogous to cleanly separating caffeine from theobromine in cocoa extracts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Rule:** For "baseline resolution" (peaks just touching at the baseline), $R_s = 1.5$. For 99.7% purity separation (3σ criterion), $R_s = 1.0$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Plate Theory

Chromatography is modeled as a series of discrete "theoretical plates" (resembling a distillation column). Each plate represents an equilibrium stage between mobile and stationary phase. The total number of theoretical plates $N$ characterizes **column efficiency**:

$$
N \;=\; 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 \;=\; 5.54 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2
$$

**Where:**
- $w$ = baseline peak width
- $w_{1/2}$ = peak width at half-height

The **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP)** is:

$$
H \;=\; \frac{L}{N}
$$

**Where $L$** = column length. Smaller $H$ → higher efficiency.

---

## 2.2 Selectivity Factor ($\alpha$)

The **Selectivity Factor (or Separation Factor) $\alpha$** compares the capacity factors of two analytes:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{k'_2}{k'_1} \qquad \text{(always} \geq 1\text{)}
$$

If $\alpha = 1$, the two compounds are *co-eluting* (no separation regardless of $N$).

---

## 2.3 The Master Resolution Equation (Purnell Equation)

The full resolution equation connects $R_s$ to the three governing chromatographic parameters:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}
$$

| Term | Physical Meaning | Effect on $R_s$ |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| $\dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}$ | **Efficiency term** | ↑ $N$ → ↑ $R_s$ |
| $\dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}$ | **Selectivity term** | ↑ $\alpha$ → ↑ $R_s$ |
| $\dfrac{k'_2}{1+k'_2}$ | **Retention term** | Optimized at $k'_2 \approx 2-10$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation is the **heart of method development** in food analysis labs. To resolve pesticide residues in milk, an analyst adjusts the mobile phase composition (changes $\alpha$), increases column length (changes $N$), or changes temperature (changes $k'$).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range (Food Analysis) |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Partition Coefficient | $K = C_s / C_m$ | dimensionless | $0.1 - 1000$ |
| 2 | Capacity Factor | $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$ | dimensionless | $0.5 - 20$ |
| 3 | Capacity Factor (alt.) | $k' = K \cdot (V_s/V_m)$ | dimensionless | — |
| 4 | Retention Time | $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$ | seconds / minutes | $1 - 30$ min |
| 5 | Dead Time | $t_M = L/u$ | seconds / minutes | $0.5 - 5$ min |
| 6 | Selectivity Factor | $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$ | dimensionless | $1.01 - 5$ |
| 7 | Resolution | $R_s = 2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})/(w_1 + w_2)$ | dimensionless | $1.0 - 2.0$ |
| 8 | Resolution (Purnell) | $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4)(\alpha-1)/\alpha \cdot k'_2/(1+k'_2)$ | dimensionless | — |
| 9 | Plate Number | $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$ | dimensionless | $1000 - 10000$ |
| 10 | HETP | $H = L/N$ | mm / µm | $5 - 100$ µm |
| 11 | Linear Velocity | $u = L/t_M$ | cm/s | $0.1 - 10$ cm/s |

---

## 2.5 Inter-Parameter Logical Chain

The logical flow in **any chromatographic separation**:

1. **Analyte properties** determine $K$ at given $T$.
2. **$K$ combined with phase volume ratio** ($V_s/V_m$) determines $k'$.
3. **$k'$ combined with mobile phase velocity** determines $t_R$.
4. **$k'$ values of two analytes** determine $\alpha$.
5. **$\alpha$, $k'$, and $N$** together determine $R_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In the **FSSAI-licensed food testing laboratories of Kerala**, this parameter chain is used daily to validate methods for detecting pesticide residues in cardamom, heavy metal chelates in fish, and aflatoxins in groundnuts. Every method must report $R_s$, $N$, and $k'$ as part of ICH/Q2(R1) validation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capacity Factor from Partition Coefficient

We start from the mass balance of an analyte inside a chromatographic column. Let:
- $V_m$ = volume of mobile phase in column
- $V_s$ = volume of stationary phase in column
- $C_m$ = concentration in mobile phase
- $C_s$ = concentration in stationary phase

**Step 1:** Total moles of analyte in the column, $n_{total}$:

$$
n_{total} \;=\; C_m \cdot V_m \;+\; C_s \cdot V_s
$$

**Step 2:** The fraction of time the analyte spends in the mobile phase equals the *fraction of moles residing there*:

$$
f_m \;=\; \frac{C_m \, V_m}{C_m \, V_m + C_s \, V_s}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply partition coefficient definition $K = C_s / C_m$, so $C_s = K \cdot C_m$. Substitute:

$$
f_m \;=\; \frac{C_m \, V_m}{C_m \, V_m + K\,C_m \, V_s} \;=\; \frac{V_m}{V_m + K\,V_s}
$$

**Step 4:** Since $t_R = t_M / f_m$ (total time scaled by the fraction in mobile phase):

$$
t_R \;=\; t_M \cdot \frac{V_m + K\,V_s}{V_m}
$$

**Step 5:** Expand and subtract $t_M$ to isolate the stationary-phase contribution:

$$
t_R - t_M \;=\; t_M \cdot \frac{K\,V_s}{V_m}
$$

**Step 6:** Divide both sides by $t_M$ to arrive at the capacity factor:

$$
\frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} \;=\; K \cdot \frac{V_s}{V_m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,k' \;=\; \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} \;=\; K \cdot \frac{V_s}{V_m}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Purnell Resolution Equation

Starting from the *fundamental* resolution definition (Section 1.5) and the *plate theory* relationship for $N$.

**Step 1:** From the plate theory, the baseline peak width is related to retention time and plate number:

$$
w_1 \;=\; \frac{4\,t_{R1}}{\sqrt{N_1}} \qquad\qquad w_2 \;=\; \frac{4\,t_{R2}}{\sqrt{N_2}}
$$

**Step 2:** For two closely eluting peaks on the same column, $N_1 \approx N_2 = N$. Substitute:

$$
w_1 + w_2 \;=\; \frac{4}{\sqrt{N}}\,(t_{R1} + t_{R2})
$$

**Step 3:** Plug into the resolution definition:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{2\,(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{\dfrac{4}{\sqrt{N}}\,(t_{R1} + t_{R2})} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{t_{R1} + t_{R2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Express retention times using $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$:

$$
t_{R1} = t_M(1 + k'_1) \qquad\qquad t_{R2} = t_M(1 + k'_2)
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into $R_s$ expression. The $t_M$ cancels:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{(1 + k'_2) - (1 + k'_1)}{(1 + k'_2) + (1 + k'_1)} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{k'_2 + k'_1 + 2}
$$

**Step 6:** Introduce selectivity factor $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$, so $k'_2 = \alpha \, k'_1$:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{\alpha k'_1 - k'_1}{\alpha k'_1 + k'_1 + 2} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{k'_1(\alpha - 1)}{k'_1(\alpha + 1) + 2}
$$

**Step 7:** Divide numerator and denominator by $k'_1$:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + 1 + \dfrac{2}{k'_1}}
$$

**Step 8:** For the common KTU standard convention $k'_1 \approx k'_2 \approx k'_2$ in the denominator (since $k'_2$ dominates in $1 + k'_2$ terms), the *Purnell approximation*:

$$
\boxed{\,R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Calculation Practice for KTU)

**Problem Statement:** In an HPLC analysis of caffeine and theobromine in cocoa beans:
- Mobile phase: 30:70 methanol:water
- Column: 150 mm × 4.6 mm, C18
- Dead time $t_M = 1.20$ min
- Caffeine retention time $t_{R1} = 3.60$ min, baseline width $w_1 = 0.30$ min
- Theobromine retention time $t_{R2} = 5.40$ min, baseline width $w_2 = 0.40$ min

**Calculate:** (a) $k'_1$ and $k'_2$, (b) $\alpha$, (c) $R_s$, (d) $N_2$.

### Solution:

**(a) Capacity factors:**

$$
k'_1 \;=\; \frac{t_{R1} - t_M}{t_M} \;=\; \frac{3.60 - 1.20}{1.20} \;=\; \frac{2.40}{1.20} \;=\; 2.00
$$

$$
k'_2 \;=\; \frac{t_{R2} - t_M}{t_M} \;=\; \frac{5.40 - 1.20}{1.20} \;=\; \frac{4.20}{1.20} \;=\; 3.50
$$

**(b) Selectivity factor:**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{k'_2}{k'_1} \;=\; \frac{3.50}{2.00} \;=\; 1.75
$$

**(c) Resolution:**

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{2\,(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2} \;=\; \frac{2\,(5.40 - 3.60)}{0.30 + 0.40} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 1.80}{0.70} \;=\; \frac{3.60}{0.70} \;\approx\; 5.14
$$

**(d) Plate number for peak 2:**

$$
N_2 \;=\; 16 \left(\frac{t_{R2}}{w_2}\right)^2 \;=\; 16 \left(\frac{5.40}{0.40}\right)^2 \;=\; 16 \times (13.5)^2 \;=\; 16 \times 182.25 \;=\; 2916
$$

**Verification via Purnell equation:**

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2916}}{4} \cdot \frac{1.75 - 1}{1.75} \cdot \frac{3.50}{1 + 3.50}
$$

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{54}{4} \cdot \frac{0.75}{1.75} \cdot \frac{3.50}{4.50}
$$

$$
R_s \;=\; 13.5 \times 0.4286 \times 0.7778 \;\approx\; 4.50
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The minor discrepancy ($5.14$ vs $4.50$) arises because the Purnell equation uses $N$ for *both* peaks equally. For exact work, the average $N = (N_1 + N_2)/2$ is preferred. This nuance is often tested in KTU Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Chromatographic Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Injection] --> B[Mobile Phase Flow Begins]
    B --> C{Mobile Phase Region}
    C --> D[Analyte in Mobile Phase]
    C --> E[Stationary Phase Packing]
    D --> F[Equilibrium Partitioning]
    E --> F
    F --> G[High K solute clings to stationary]
    F --> H[Low K solute elutes with mobile]
    H --> I[Detector at Column Exit]
    G --> J[Delayed Detector Signal]
    I --> K[Peak 1 at time tR1]
    J --> L[Peak 2 at time tR2]
    K --> M[Calculate Resolution Rs]
    L --> M
    M --> N{Rs greater than 1.5?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Baseline Separation Achieved]
    N -->|No| P[Adjust alpha N or k prime]
    P --> Q[Modify Mobile Phase]
    P --> R[Increase Column Length]
    P --> S[Change Temperature]
```

---

## 4.2 Parameter Relationship Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Partition Coefficient K] --> P2[Capacity Factor k prime]
    P2 --> P3[Retention Time tR]
    P2 --> P4[Selectivity Factor alpha]
    P4 --> P5[Resolution Rs]
    P2 --> P5
    P6[Plate Number N] --> P5
    P7[Dead Time tM] --> P3
    P7 --> P2
    P5 --> P8{Valid Food Analysis Method?}
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Report to FSSAI Standards]
    P8 -->|No| P10[Method Optimization Required]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Typical Chromatogram with Annotated Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Run] --> B[tM: Dead Time Peak]
    B --> C[Mobile phase baseline]
    C --> D[tR1: Peak 1 maximum]
    D --> E[w1: baseline width of Peak 1]
    E --> F[tR2: Peak 2 maximum]
    F --> G[w2: baseline width of Peak 2]
    G --> H[End of Run]
    D --> I[Adjusted retention tR1 minus tM]
    F --> J[Adjusted retention tR2 minus tM]
    I --> K[k prime 1 calculation]
    J --> L[k prime 2 calculation]
    K --> M[alpha calculation]
    L --> M
    E --> N[Rs from widths]
    G --> N
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Students:**
> In the schematic above, the horizontal axis represents **time** (not distance). The first small peak (unretained marker, e.g., uracil in HPLC) defines $t_M$. The two analyte peaks are labeled with their respective $t_R$ and $w$ values. This is the **standard IUPAC representation** expected in KTU answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Capacity Factor" in chromatography. What is its significance in food analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Capacity Factor ($k'$)** is defined as the ratio of the *adjusted retention time* of an analyte to the *dead time* of the mobile phase:
>
> $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$
>
> It is dimensionless. **Significance in food analysis:**
> (i) It quantifies how strongly an analyte (e.g., a pesticide, vitamin, or pigment) is retained by the stationary phase.
> (ii) The optimum $k'$ range for analytical separations is **2 to 10**. A $k' < 1$ implies peaks elute too close to the void volume (poor separation), while $k' > 20$ causes excessive run time and peak broadening.
> (iii) For **method validation** of food contaminants (per FSSAI/ICH guidelines), $k'$ must be reported to ensure reproducibility across laboratories.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Dead Time? How is it determined experimentally in HPLC and GC?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Dead Time ($t_M$)** is the time required for the mobile phase alone (or an unretained solute) to traverse the column from injector to detector.
>
> **Determination in HPLC:** Injecting an unretained, non-interacting marker such as **uracil** (for reverse-phase) or **toluene** (for normal-phase); its retention time equals $t_M$.
>
> **Determination in GC:** Injecting **methane** or **air**; these do not partition into the stationary liquid phase and elute at $t_M$.
>
> The linear velocity of mobile phase is then computed as $u = L/t_M$.

---

## PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **Capacity Factor ($k'$)** and the **Partition Coefficient ($K$)** for a chromatographic column. Explain each step clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mixture of two fatty acid methyl esters (FAME), methyl oleate (peak 1) and methyl stearate (peak 2), is analyzed by GC. Given:
- $t_M = 0.80$ min, $t_{R1} = 4.20$ min, $t_{R2} = 5.60$ min
- Baseline widths: $w_1 = 0.28$ min, $w_2 = 0.32$ min

Calculate: (i) Capacity factors $k'_1$ and $k'_2$, (ii) Selectivity factor $\alpha$, (iii) Resolution $R_s$, (iv) Number of theoretical plates for peak 2. Comment on whether baseline separation is achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation of $k' = K(V_s/V_m)$:**

**Step 1:** Express the total moles of analyte in the column:
$$n_{total} = C_m V_m + C_s V_s$$

**Step 2:** Fraction in mobile phase:
$$f_m = \frac{C_m V_m}{C_m V_m + C_s V_s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $C_s = K C_m$:
$$f_m = \frac{V_m}{V_m + K V_s}$$

**Step 4:** Since $t_R = t_M / f_m$:
$$t_R = t_M \cdot \frac{V_m + K V_s}{V_m}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract $t_M$:
$$t_R - t_M = t_M \cdot \frac{K V_s}{V_m}$$

**Step 6:** Hence the capacity factor:
$$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = K \cdot \frac{V_s}{V_m}$$

> This shows $k'$ depends on both the **thermodynamic partition coefficient** $K$ and the **geometric phase ratio** $V_s/V_m$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Capacity factors:**

$$
k'_1 = \frac{4.20 - 0.80}{0.80} = \frac{3.40}{0.80} = 4.25
$$

$$
k'_2 = \frac{5.60 - 0.80}{0.80} = \frac{4.80}{0.80} = 6.00
$$

**(ii) Selectivity factor:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{k'_2}{k'_1} = \frac{6.00}{4.25} \approx 1.41
$$

**(iii) Resolution:**

$$
R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2} = \frac{2(5.60 - 4.20)}{0.28 + 0.32} = \frac{2 \times 1.40}{0.60} = \frac{2.80}{0.60} \approx 4.67
$$

**(iv) Plate number for peak 2:**

$$
N_2 = 16\left(\frac{t_{R2}}{w_2}\right)^2 = 16\left(\frac{5.60}{0.32}\right)^2 = 16 \times (17.5)^2 = 16 \times 306.25 = 4900
$$

**Comment:** Since $R_s \approx 4.67 \gg 1.5$, **baseline separation is comfortably achieved**. The methyl oleate and methyl stearate peaks are well-resolved, suitable for quantification of these fatty acids in edible oils.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`

**(a)** Define **Resolution ($R_s$)**. Derive the **Purnell equation** relating resolution to the plate number, selectivity factor, and capacity factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a reverse-phase HPLC separation of two food dyes (Sunset Yellow and Tartrazine):
- $k'_1 = 2.50$, $k'_2 = 4.00$, $N = 5000$

Calculate: (i) Selectivity factor $\alpha$, (ii) Resolution $R_s$ using Purnell's equation, (iii) If column length is doubled, what will be the new resolution? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Derivation of Purnell equation (presented in Section 3.2):**

Starting from $R_s = 2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})/(w_1 + w_2)$ and using $w = 4 t_R/\sqrt{N}$, we obtain:

**Step 1:** $w_1 + w_2 = (4/\sqrt{N})(t_{R1} + t_{R2})$

**Step 2:** Substituting and simplifying with $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$:

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{k'_2 + k'_1 + 2}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $k'_1$ and substitute $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1$:

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + 1 + 2/k'_1}
$$

**Step 4:** For closely eluting peaks ($k'_1 \approx k'_2$), and recognizing $k'_2/(1+k'_2)$ as the retention term:

$$
\boxed{R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Selectivity factor:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{k'_2}{k'_1} = \frac{4.00}{2.50} = 1.60
$$

**(ii) Resolution via Purnell:**

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{5000}}{4} \cdot \frac{1.60 - 1}{1.60} \cdot \frac{4.00}{1 + 4.00}
$$

$$
R_s = \frac{70.71}{4} \cdot \frac{0.60}{1.60} \cdot \frac{4.00}{5.00}
$$

$$
R_s = 17.68 \times 0.375 \times 0.80 \approx 5.30
$$

**(iii) Doubling column length:**

Since $N$ is proportional to $L$ (i.e., $N \propto L$), doubling $L$ doubles $N$: $N_{new} = 10000$.

Since $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$:

$$
\frac{R_{s,\,new}}{R_s} = \sqrt{\frac{N_{new}}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{10000}{5000}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414
$$

$$
R_{s,\,new} = 5.30 \times 1.414 \approx 7.50
$$

**Note:** Doubling column length increases resolution by only **41.4%**, not 100%. This is why chromatographers prefer to *change $\alpha$ or $k'$* (mobile phase composition) for greater impact on $R_s$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students frequently write $t_R$ in seconds while $w$ is in minutes — this loses **2 marks** immediately. Always use *consistent units*.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 2:** In the resolution formula, the numerator is **$2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})$**, *not* $(t_{R2} - t_{R1})$. This is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Misapplying Purnell:** The Purnell equation uses $\sqrt{N}/4$, *not* $N/4$. Confusing these gives a wildly wrong answer.
> 4. **Not declaring $\alpha \geq 1$:** Always arrange peaks so $k'_2 > k'_1$ ensuring $\alpha \geq 1$.
> 5. **Reporting $R_s$ without interpretation:** Always conclude with "baseline / partial / no separation" — examiners specifically check this.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Partition Coefficient ($K$):** Ratio of analyte concentration in stationary to mobile phase at equilibrium. Dimensionless. Determined by thermodynamics.
- ✅ **Capacity Factor ($k'$):** $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M = K(V_s/V_m)$. Optimum range **2–10** for analytical work.
- ✅ **Dead Time ($t_M$):** Travel time of mobile phase only. Found via unretained marker (uracil in HPLC, methane in GC).
- ✅ **Retention Time ($t_R$):** $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$. Always greater than $t_M$.
- ✅ **Selectivity Factor ($\alpha$):** $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1 \geq 1$. If $\alpha = 1$, no separation is possible regardless of column efficiency.
- ✅ **Resolution ($R_s$):** $R_s = 2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})/(w_1 + w_2)$. Baseline separation criterion: **$R_s \geq 1.5$**.
- ✅ **Purnell Master Equation:** $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4)(\alpha - 1)/\alpha \cdot k'_2/(1+k'_2)$. The three levers are **N, $\alpha$, $k'$**.
- ✅ **Doubling $N$ (column length) → $R_s$ increases by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$**, not 2. So mobile phase tuning is preferred.
- ✅ **Plate Number:** $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$. **HETP:** $H = L/N$ (lower $H$ = better column).
- ✅ **Food Analysis Application:** Used in aflatoxin testing, pesticide residue analysis, fatty acid profiling, vitamin quantification, and food dye analysis — all governed by FSSAI/ICH validation protocols.
- ✅ **Memory Aid:** "**K**eep **k**apital **k**apacity" — remember K → k' → tR → Rs in order.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Chromatographic Techniques
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Chromatographic Techniques

# 🧪 Types of Chromatographic Techniques — Module 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Chromatography?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> Chromatography is a **physical method of separation** in which the components of a mixture are separated by their **differential distribution between two immiscible phases** — a **stationary phase** (which remains fixed) and a **mobile phase** (which moves through or over the stationary phase carrying the sample).

The name comes from the Greek words **"chroma" (color)** and **"graphein" (to write)** — because Mikhail Tswett first used this technique in 1906 to separate colored plant pigments (chlorophylls and carotenoids) using a calcium carbonate column.

### 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — The "Marathon Race" of Molecules 🏃‍♂️

Imagine you have a group of students running a marathon through a **muddy field**:

- The **runners (analytes)** represent the molecules you want to separate.
- The **mud (stationary phase)** slows everyone down by clinging to them.
- The **wind blowing behind them (mobile phase)** pushes them forward.
- Some students love the mud and stay back; others hate it and rush forward.

👉 **Result:** They reach the finish line at *different times*. This is exactly what chromatography does — separates molecules based on their **differential affinity** for two phases.

### 1.3 The Two Universal Phases

| Phase | Role | Examples |
|-------|------|----------|
| **Stationary Phase** | Holds back components based on affinity | Silica gel, alumina, polymer beads, C-18 column, paper |
| **Mobile Phase** | Carries the sample forward | Solvents (hexane, water, methanol), gases (He, N₂), supercritical CO₂ |

### 1.4 Classification of Chromatographic Techniques (KTU-Syllabus Master Tree)

> [!NOTE]
> Chromatographic techniques are classified on **three primary bases** as per the KTU 2024 OEFTT613 syllabus:

**A. Based on the Shape of the Chromatographic Bed**
- **Planar Chromatography** — stationary phase is flat (Paper, TLC)
- **Column Chromatography** — stationary phase is packed in a tube (HPLC, GC, Flash chromatography)

**B. Based on the Physical State of the Mobile Phase**
- **Gas Chromatography (GC)** — mobile phase is a gas
- **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** — mobile phase is a liquid
- **Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC)** — mobile phase is a supercritical fluid

**C. Based on the Separation Mechanism (Principle)**
- **Adsorption Chromatography** — based on surface adsorption
- **Partition Chromatography** — based on differential solubility
- **Ion-Exchange Chromatography** — based on charge
- **Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC)** — based on molecular size
- **Affinity Chromatography** — based on biospecific interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Chromatographic Type

#### 🔹 A. Planar Chromatography

**Paper Chromatography**
- Stationary phase: water trapped in cellulose fibers of filter paper.
- Mobile phase: an organic solvent (e.g., butanol:acetic acid:water).
- Mechanism: **Partition-based**.
- Application: separation of amino acids, sugars, dyes.

**Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC)**
- Stationary phase: thin layer (≈250 µm) of silica gel or alumina coated on a glass/aluminum plate.
- Mobile phase: solvent drawn up by capillary action.
- Detection: UV light, iodine vapors, staining reagents.

#### 🔹 B. Column Chromatography

**Classical Column Chromatography**
- A glass column packed with silica/alumina.
- Sample loaded on top, eluted with solvents of increasing polarity.

**High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)**
- Uses **high pressure (up to 400 bar)** to force mobile phase through a tightly packed column (3–5 µm particles).
- Detectors: UV-Vis, Fluorescence, Refractive Index, Mass Spectrometry.

**Gas Chromatography (GC)**
- Sample must be **volatile and thermally stable**.
- Carrier gas: Helium, Nitrogen, Hydrogen.
- Detectors: FID (Flame Ionization), TCD (Thermal Conductivity), MS.

#### 🔹 C. Adsorption Chromatography (Liquid-Solid)
- Solute molecules adsorb onto the surface of a solid stationary phase (e.g., silica).
- Elution order: **non-polar compounds elute first**, polar compounds are retained longer.
- Application: separation of lipids, fatty acids.

#### 🔹 D. Partition Chromatography (Liquid-Liquid)
- Two immiscible liquids; one coated on an inert support.
- Normal-phase: polar stationary phase (water), non-polar mobile phase (hexane).
- Reverse-phase: **non-polar stationary phase (C-18)**, polar mobile phase (water/methanol) — most common in food analysis.

#### 🔹 E. Ion-Exchange Chromatography
- Stationary phase: **resin with fixed charged groups** (cation exchange: –SO₃⁻; anion exchange: –N(CH₃)₃⁺).
- Separation based on charge and charge density.
- Application: amino acid analysis, sugar analysis in foods.

#### 🔹 F. Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC)
- Also called **Gel Filtration (aqueous)** or **Gel Permeation (organic)**.
- Stationary phase: porous beads; small molecules enter pores and elute later; large molecules bypass pores and elute first.
- Application: protein purification, polymer molecular weight determination.

#### 🔹 G. Affinity Chromatography
- Stationary phase has a **biospecific ligand** (e.g., antibody, enzyme substrate, lectin).
- Only the target molecule binds; everything else washes through.
- Application: purification of enzymes, antibodies, glycoproteins.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for chromatography problems. Memorize the **Retention Factor (Rf), Capacity Factor (k'), Selectivity (α), Resolution (Rs), and Efficiency (N)**.

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Significance / Unit |
|-----------|:------:|---------|---------------------|
| **Retention Factor** | Rf | Rf = (distance traveled by solute) / (distance traveled by solvent front) | Dimensionless; 0 ≤ Rf ≤ 1 |
| **Retention Time** | tR | Time from injection to peak maximum | Minutes / Seconds |
| **Dead Time** | tM | Time for unretained solute to elute | Minutes / Seconds |
| **Adjusted Retention Time** | tR' | tR' = tR − tM | Minutes / Seconds |
| **Capacity Factor** | k' | k' = (tR − tM) / tM = tR' / tM | Dimensionless; k' > 0 ideal |
| **Selectivity (Separation Factor)** | α | α = k2' / k1'  (always ≥ 1) | Dimensionless |
| **Resolution** | Rs | Rs = 2(tR2 − tR1) / (w1 + w2) | Dimensionless; Rs ≥ 1.5 = baseline |
| **Number of Theoretical Plates** | N | N = 16 (tR / w)²  or  N = 5.54 (tR / w½)² | Higher N = sharper peaks |
| **Plate Height** | H | H = L / N | Length units (µm, mm) |
| **Van Deemter Equation** | H | H = A + (B/u) + (C · u) | Optimal linear velocity u |
| **Efficiency Term (Purnell)** | Rs | Rs = (√N / 4) · [(α − 1)/α] · [k2' / (1 + k2')] | Master resolution equation |
| **Peak Width at Base** | w | w = 4σ | Width units |

### 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Food Analysis

- **Rf values** let you *identify* food adulterants by comparing with reference standards (e.g., detection of artificial colors).
- **Resolution (Rs)** tells you if two food additives (e.g., two preservatives like benzoate and sorbate) can be quantitatively measured.
- **Van Deemter equation** guides column selection and flow-rate optimization in GC-MS analysis of pesticide residues.
- **Capacity factor (k')** is the bridge between *what you injected* and *how long it took to come out*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Resolution Equation (Purnell Equation)

We begin from the **fundamental definition of resolution** between two adjacent peaks 1 and 2:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{\frac{1}{2}\left(w_1 + w_2\right)}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute for peak width using number of theoretical plates.**

The peak width is related to retention time and plate count as:

$$
w \;=\; 4 \,\sigma \;\approx\; \frac{4 \, t_R}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

This comes from the Gaussian peak equation where σ is the standard deviation of the peak. For close peaks where N₁ ≈ N₂ = N:

$$
\frac{1}{2}(w_1 + w_2) \;=\; \frac{4 \, t_{R2}}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the numerator using selectivity α.**

We know that the capacity factor for peak 2 is k2' = (tR2 − tM)/tM, so:

$$
t_{R2} - t_{R1} \;=\; t_M (k_2' - k_1')
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by tM, and using α = k2' / k1' (i.e., k1' = k2'/α):

$$
t_{R2} - t_{R1} \;=\; t_M \cdot k_2' \left(1 - \frac{1}{\alpha}\right) \;=\; t_M \cdot k_2' \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into resolution formula.**

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{t_M \cdot k_2' \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}}{\dfrac{4 \, t_{R2}}{\sqrt{N}}}
$$

But tR2 = tM (1 + k2'), so:

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k_2'}{1 + k_2'} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the Purnell Master Resolution Equation.** It shows that resolution is improved by:
> 1. **Increasing N** (longer column, smaller particles),
> 2. **Increasing α** (better stationary phase chemistry),
> 3. **Optimizing k'** (typically 2 < k' < 10).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Van Deemter Equation

The plate height **H** contributes from three independent sources:

**Term A — Eddy Diffusion (A)**

$$
A \;=\; 2 \,\lambda \, d_p
$$

where λ is the packing irregularity factor and dp is the particle diameter. A is **independent of flow rate**.

**Term B — Longitudinal Molecular Diffusion (B/u)**

$$
\frac{B}{u} \;=\; \frac{2 \,\gamma \, D_m}{u}
$$

where γ is the obstruction factor, Dm is the solute diffusion coefficient in mobile phase, and u is the linear velocity. This term **dominates at low velocities**.

**Term C — Mass Transfer Resistance (C·u)**

$$
C \cdot u \;=\; \left( \frac{f_m \, d_p^2}{D_m} + \frac{f_s \, d_f^2}{D_s} \right) \cdot u
$$

This term represents resistance to mass transfer in mobile (fm) and stationary (fs) phases. It **dominates at high velocities**.

Adding all three contributions:

$$
H \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u
$$

**Optimal linear velocity** is found by differentiating H with respect to u and setting dH/du = 0:

$$
\frac{dH}{du} \;=\; -\frac{B}{u^2} \;+\; C \;=\; 0
$$

Solving:

$$
u_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}
$$

Substituting back gives the minimum plate height:

$$
H_{min} \;=\; A \;+\; 2\sqrt{B \cdot C}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Identification of a Food Dye by TLC

> **Problem:** A food dye migrates **4.2 cm** on a TLC plate, while the solvent front moves **7.0 cm**. Calculate its **Retention Factor (Rf)**.

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- Distance traveled by solute = 4.2 cm
- Distance traveled by solvent front = 7.0 cm

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.

$$
R_f \;=\; \frac{\text{distance by solute}}{\text{distance by solvent front}} \;=\; \frac{4.2}{7.0}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerical value.

$$
R_f \;=\; 0.600
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> An Rf of **0.60** indicates the dye has a moderate affinity for the stationary phase. Comparing this value with authentic reference standards under identical conditions enables **identification of the dye in a food sample** (e.g., detecting Sunset Yellow FCF vs Tartrazine in a beverage).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Technique vs. Application in Food Analysis

| Chromatographic Technique | Separation Principle | Typical Food Sample Application | Detector Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper Chromatography** | Partition | Amino acids in protein hydrolysates | Ninhydrin spray |
| **TLC** | Adsorption / Partition | Synthetic dyes, aflatoxins, pesticides | UV / Iodine chamber |
| **HPLC (Reverse-Phase)** | Hydrophobic partition | Preservatives, vitamins, sugars, antioxidants | UV-Vis, RI, FLD |
| **HPLC (Ion-Exchange)** | Charge | Organic acids in fruit juices, sugars | RI, PAD |
| **GC** | Volatility / Partition | Fatty acids (as methyl esters), pesticides, flavors | FID, MS |
| **GPC / SEC** | Molecular size | Starch molecular weight, protein aggregates | RI, MALS |
| **Affinity Chromatography** | Biospecific binding | Lectins, antibodies in dairy | UV |
| **SFC** | Solubility in supercritical fluid | Triglycerides, lipid-soluble vitamins | UV, MS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Classify the different chromatographic techniques based on the physical state of the mobile phase. Give one example application of each in food analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Based on the physical state of the mobile phase, chromatographic techniques are classified into:

1. **Gas Chromatography (GC)** — Mobile phase is an inert carrier gas such as helium, nitrogen, or hydrogen. *Food application:* Analysis of fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs) in edible oils using GC-FID.

2. **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** — Mobile phase is a liquid (water, methanol, acetonitrile, or mixtures). *Food application:* Determination of synthetic food colors, preservatives (benzoic acid, sorbic acid), and water-soluble vitamins using HPLC-UV.

3. **Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC)** — Mobile phase is a supercritical fluid (commonly CO₂ above its critical point of 31.1 °C and 73.8 bar). *Food application:* Separation of triglycerides and lipid-soluble vitamins in dairy and oil samples.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Differentiate between Normal Phase and Reverse Phase HPLC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Normal Phase HPLC | Reverse Phase HPLC |
|---|---|---|
| **Stationary Phase** | Polar (e.g., silica –OH) | Non-polar (e.g., C-18, C-8) |
| **Mobile Phase** | Non-polar (hexane, dichloromethane) | Polar (water/methanol or water/acetonitrile) |
| **Elution Order** | Polar compounds elute **first** | Non-polar compounds elute **first** |
| **Retention Mechanism** | Adsorption / dipole interactions | Hydrophobic partition |
| **Food Analysis Use** | Lipid-soluble vitamins, carotenoids | Sugars, preservatives, antioxidants, amino acids |

> Reverse Phase (RP) HPLC is by far the most widely used mode in food analysis because most food components are water-soluble or moderately polar.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Module 2 long-answer questions carry **14 marks** with sub-part splits. The model pattern below follows the **actual board valuation key** format.

---

### ✏️ Question A `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model Paper, Module 2 Choice 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and instrumentation of **High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)**. Discuss the role of the **stationary phase, mobile phase, pump, injector, column, and detector**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **Capacity Factor (k')**, **Selectivity (α)**, and **Resolution (Rs)**. Two food preservatives, benzoic acid (peak 1) and sorbic acid (peak 2), elute at retention times tR1 = 4.8 min and tR2 = 6.3 min with peak widths w1 = 0.40 min and w2 = 0.45 min. The dead time tM = 1.0 min. Calculate k'₁, k'₂, α, and Rs.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) HPLC — Principle and Instrumentation [7 marks]**

**Principle (2 marks):** HPLC separates components of a mixture based on their **differential distribution between a liquid mobile phase and a solid stationary phase** packed inside a narrow-bore column. The mobile phase is forced through the column under **high pressure (50–400 bar)** to achieve rapid, high-resolution separations.

**Block Diagram (already provided in Section 4.2 of these notes).** Students should redraw and label:

1. **Mobile Phase Reservoirs** — typically 2 (A and B) for gradient elution.
2. **Pump** — delivers constant flow (0.1–10 mL/min); types: isocratic, binary, quaternary.
3. **Injector / Autosampler** — introduces a precise volume (1–100 µL) into the high-pressure stream.
4. **Column** — stainless steel, 4.6 mm × 150–250 mm, packed with 3–5 µm C-18 or silica particles.
5. **Detector** — UV-Vis (most common), RI, FLD, ELSD, or MS.
6. **Data System** — records chromatogram and integrates peak areas.

**Working (2 marks):** The sample is injected, carried by mobile phase into the column. Components partition between mobile and stationary phases according to their polarity/affinity. They emerge at different times → detected → recorded as peaks.

**Role of each component (3 marks):**
- **Stationary phase (C-18)** determines selectivity — non-polar compounds retained longer.
- **Mobile phase** (water + methanol/acetonitrile) controls retention — increasing organic % speeds elution.
- **Pump** maintains constant flow for reproducibility.
- **Injector** ensures precise sample volume to avoid peak distortion.
- **Column** is where separation physically occurs.
- **Detector** quantifies each component via signal intensity (peak area ∝ concentration).

---

**(b) Numerical on k', α, and Rs [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- tR1 = 4.8 min, w1 = 0.40 min
- tR2 = 6.3 min, w2 = 0.45 min
- tM = 1.0 min

**Step 1 — Capacity Factor k'₁ (1 mark):**

$$
k_1' \;=\; \frac{t_{R1} - t_M}{t_M} \;=\; \frac{4.8 - 1.0}{1.0} \;=\; 3.8
$$

**Step 2 — Capacity Factor k'₂ (1 mark):**

$$
k_2' \;=\; \frac{t_{R2} - t_M}{t_M} \;=\; \frac{6.3 - 1.0}{1.0} \;=\; 5.3
$$

**Step 3 — Selectivity α (1.5 marks):**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{k_2'}{k_1'} \;=\; \frac{5.3}{3.8} \;=\; 1.39
$$

**Step 4 — Resolution Rs (2.5 marks):**

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2} \;=\; \frac{2(6.3 - 4.8)}{0.40 + 0.45}
$$

$$
R_s \;=\; \frac{2 \times 1.5}{0.85} \;=\; \frac{3.0}{0.85} \;=\; 3.53
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Since **Rs > 1.5**, the two preservatives are **baseline-separated** and can be quantitatively analyzed in a food matrix using this HPLC method.

---

### ✏️ Question B `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model Paper, Module 2 Choice 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle, instrumentation, and applications of **Gas Chromatography (GC)**. Discuss why the sample must be **volatile and thermally stable**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC)** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **Gel Filtration** and **Gel Permeation Chromatography**. Mention two applications of SEC in food analysis.

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Gas Chromatography — Principle, Instrumentation, Applications [7 marks]**

**Principle (2 marks):** In GC, the **mobile phase is an inert carrier gas** (He, N₂, or H₂) and the **stationary phase is a high-boiling liquid** coated on an inert solid support or on the inner wall of a capillary column. Separation occurs due to **differential partitioning** of volatile analytes between the gas and liquid phases.

**Requirement of volatility and thermal stability (2 marks):** Since the column is housed in a heated oven (50–350 °C), only analytes that **vaporize without decomposition** can be analyzed. Non-volatile food components (e.g., sugars, amino acids, salts) must be **derivatized** (e.g., silylation, methylation) before GC analysis. Fatty acids are converted to **fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs)** to make them volatile for GC analysis.

**Instrumentation (2 marks)** — six main components (refer to block diagram in Section 4.3):
1. **Carrier gas supply** with pressure regulator.
2. **Injector** — split, splitless, or on-column.
3. **Column oven** with temperature programming.
4. **Column** — packed (1–3 m) or capillary (15–60 m).
5. **Detector** — FID, TCD, ECD, MS.
6. **Recorder / data system.**

**Applications in food analysis (1 mark):**
- Fatty acid profile of edible oils.
- Pesticide residue analysis.
- Flavor and aroma compounds in spices, coffee, dairy.
- Detection of food adulteration (e.g., mineral oil in edible oil).

---

**(b) Size-Exclusion Chromatography [7 marks]**

**Principle (2 marks):** SEC separates molecules **based on their hydrodynamic size (molecular weight)** using a column packed with **porous beads**. Large molecules cannot enter the pores → bypass through the void volume → **elute first**. Small molecules diffuse into the pores → take a longer path → **elute last**.

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    M["Sample Mixture"]:::mix
    M --> L["Large Molecule Excluded from Pores"]
    M --> S["Small Molecule Enters Pores"]

    L --> E1["Elutes First"]:::first
    S --> E2["Elutes Last"]:::last

    classDef mix fill:#fde2e4,color:#1f1f1f,stroke:#c97b7b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef first fill:#b5ead7,color:#1f1f1f,stroke:#5e8c71,stroke-width:2px
    classDef last fill:#ffdac1,color:#1f1f1f,stroke:#a8722a,stroke-width:2px
```

**Gel Filtration vs Gel Permeation (3 marks):**

| Feature | Gel Filtration Chromatography (GFC) | Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobile Phase** | Aqueous buffer | Organic solvent (THF, toluene) |
| **Stationary Phase** | Hydrophilic gel (e.g., Sephadex, agarose) | Hydrophobic polymer (e.g., cross-linked polystyrene) |
| **Typical Samples** | Water-soluble proteins, polysaccharides, nucleic acids | Synthetic polymers, oils, lipid-soluble compounds |
| **Alternative Name** | Aqueous SEC | Organic SEC |

**Applications in food analysis (1 mark):**
- Molecular weight distribution of **starch** and **modified starches**.
- Separation and characterization of **milk proteins** (casein vs whey).
- Aggregation state of **pectin** and other hydrocolloids.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where marks are LOST:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Rf with k':** Rf applies to **planar** chromatography (TLC, paper). k' applies to **column** chromatography (HPLC, GC). Writing Rf for an HPLC problem = 0 marks for that part.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals:** The retention time *tR*, width *w*, and dead time *tM* MUST be in the **same units** (either all minutes or all seconds) before substituting in the formula. Mixing minutes and seconds is a guaranteed **1–2 mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Inadequate block diagrams:** Examiners expect **labelled rectangular blocks** with arrows showing direction of flow. Drawing a "box with no labels" gets **0 marks** even if the explanation is correct.
>
> 4. **Writing only names without applications:** In Part A 3-mark questions, the KTU key expects **classification + 1 example application**. Missing the application = lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Derivation steps skipped:** For the Purnell or Van Deemter equation, every transition must show **explicit substitution**. Skipping algebra = lose 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up Normal vs Reverse Phase:** A common error — students write "Reverse Phase uses polar stationary phase." **WRONG.** Reverse phase uses **non-polar (C-18)** stationary phase. This single wrong term = lose 1 mark.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Types of Chromatographic Techniques"**
>
> - Chromatography = separation based on **differential partitioning** between **mobile + stationary phases**.
> - **Three classification axes:** (1) Bed shape (planar vs column), (2) Mobile phase state (GC, LC, SFC), (3) Separation mechanism (adsorption, partition, ion-exchange, SEC, affinity).
> - **Planar = TLC + Paper Chromatography**; **Column = HPLC + GC + classical column**.
> - **Reverse Phase (C-18) HPLC is the workhorse of food analysis** — elution: non-polar first, polar last.
> - **TLC Retention Factor:** Rf = (solute distance) / (solvent front distance); **0 ≤ Rf ≤ 1**.
> - **Capacity Factor:** k' = (tR − tM) / tM — measures retention strength.
> - **Selectivity:** α = k2'/k1' — must be > 1.
> - **Resolution:** Rs = 2(tR2 − tR1)/(w1 + w2) — **Rs ≥ 1.5 = baseline separation**.
> - **Master Purnell Equation:** Rs = (√N / 4) · [(α − 1)/α] · [k2'/(1 + k2')] — relates resolution to efficiency, selectivity, and retention.
> - **Van Deemter Equation:** H = A + B/u + C·u → **optimal velocity** at u_opt = √(B/C).
> - **GC** requires **volatile + thermally stable** analytes; non-volatile foods are **derivatized** (e.g., FAMEs).
> - **SEC/Gel Filtration** separates by **size** — large molecules elute **first** (excluded from pores).
> - **Ion-exchange chromatography** uses **charged resins** — cations bind to cation exchangers, anions to anion exchangers.
> - **Affinity chromatography** is the **most selective** (uses biospecific ligands like antibodies or lectins).
> - **Common food applications:** vitamins (HPLC), fatty acids (GC), proteins (SEC, affinity), dyes (TLC), sugars (HPLC with RI detector), pesticides (GC-MS).
> - Always write **units** in numerical answers. Always **label all blocks** in diagrams.
