# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — Sample Inlet System

The inlet must deliver the analyte into the **high-vacuum ion source** without causing a pressure surge that would extinguish the ion beam.

### 2.1.1 Types of Inlets (Relevant to Food MS)

| Inlet Type | Coupling | Analyte State | KTU Significance |
|------------|----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Direct Insertion Probe (DIP)** | Direct | Solid/liquid | High-boiling compounds (e.g., triglycerides) |
| **Gas Chromatograph (GC)** | GC-MS | Volatile, thermally stable | Pesticide residues, flavor volatiles |
| **Liquid Chromatograph (LC)** | LC-MS | Polar, thermally labile | Mycotoxins, peptides, polar pesticides |
| **Direct Infusion** | Syringe pump | Solution | Quick screening |
| **Electrospray (ESI direct)** | ESI-MS | Liquid | Proteins, large biomolecules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** For food samples, **GC-MS** dominates analysis of volatiles (essential oils, aroma compounds), while **LC-MS/MS** dominates analysis of polar contaminants (pesticides, veterinary drug residues, mycotoxins). The inlet dictates the entire workflow.

### 2.1.2 Critical Pressure Consideration

The inlet must accommodate a pressure drop from **atmospheric pressure (~760 torr)** to the ion-source vacuum of **~10⁻⁶ torr** — a drop of **nine orders of magnitude**. This is achieved using:
- **Differential pumping** (multiple stages of vacuum pumps).
- **Capillary restrictors** (e.g., 50–100 µm i.d. fused-silica capillary in ESI).
- **Heated transfer lines** (200–350 °C for GC-MS).

---

## 2.2 Component 2 — Ion Source (Ionizer)

The ion source **converts neutral molecules into ions** so that they can be manipulated by electric and magnetic fields.

### 2.2.1 Classification by Energy Transfer

| Category | Examples | Energy | Soft/Hard | Food MS Use |
|----------|----------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Hard Ionization** | Electron Ionization (EI) | **70 eV** | Hard (extensive fragmentation) | GC-MS of volatiles, library matching |
| **Soft Ionization** | Chemical Ionization (CI), Electrospray (ESI), APCI, MALDI | <20 eV (CI); near-thermal (ESI/APCI) | Soft (molecular ion preserved) | LC-MS of polar/thermally labile analytes |

### 2.2.2 Electron Ionization (EI) — The Workhorse

**Mechanism (Step-by-Step):**
1. A **rhenium or tungsten filament** is heated to ~**2000 °C**, emitting electrons via **thermionic emission**.
2. Electrons are accelerated toward the anode through a potential difference of **+70 V**, gaining kinetic energy.
3. Analyte vapor (from GC) enters the ionization chamber.
4. When a **70-eV electron** collides with a molecule **M**, it ejects an electron:

$$M + e^-_{(70\,\text{eV})} \;\longrightarrow\; M^{\boldsymbol{\cdot}+} + 2e^-$$

5. The radical cation **M•⁺** is unstable and fragments along characteristic pathways.
6. Ions are **repelled** by a small positive potential (repeller, +1 to +10 V) and focused into the mass analyzer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why exactly 70 eV?** This is the standardized energy adopted universally because it produces **reproducible fragmentation patterns** that can be compared with reference libraries (e.g., **NIST EI library**, **Wiley Registry**). This standardization is mandatory in KTU-expected GC-MS questions.

### 2.2.3 Electrospray Ionization (ESI) — The Polar-Analyte Specialist

ESI produces **multiply-charged ions** — a unique feature that allows mass analyzers with limited m/z range (e.g., quadrupoles, m/z ≤ 4000) to analyze **proteins and peptides** of molecular mass >100,000 Da.

**The Taylor Cone Mechanism:**
1. The analyte solution flows through a **steel capillary** held at **+2 to +5 kV** (positive mode).
2. At the capillary tip, the electric field deforms the meniscus into a **Taylor cone**.
3. A fine **charged droplet** is ejected.
4. Solvent evaporates; droplet shrinks until **Coulombic repulsion > surface tension** (Rayleigh limit), causing **"Coulombic fission"**.
5. Repeated fission yields **gas-phase ions** — either by the **Charge Residue Model (CRM)** or the **Ion Evaporation Model (IEM)**.

$$E_{\text{Rayleigh}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2\,\gamma\,(\text{N/m})}{\varepsilon_0\,(q/R)^2}} \quad \text{(field at Rayleigh limit)}$$

where $\gamma$ = surface tension, $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space, $q$ = droplet charge, $R$ = droplet radius.

### 2.2.4 Other Ion Sources (Brief)

| Source | Acronym | Principle | Food-Analyte Class |
|--------|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Chemical Ionization** | CI | Reagent gas (CH₄, NH₃) transfers H⁺ | Labile volatiles |
| **Atmospheric Pressure Chemical Ionization** | APCI | Corona discharge on vapor | Mid-polarity (steroids, lipids) |
| **Atmospheric Pressure Photoionization** | APPI | UV lamp (10 eV photons) | Non-polar (PAHs, carotenoids) |
| **Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization** | MALDI | UV laser + matrix (e.g., DHB) | Proteins, oligosaccharides |
| **Inductively Coupled Plasma** | ICP | Argon plasma at ~8000 K | Trace metals (Pb, As, Cd, Hg in food) |

---

## 2.3 Component 3 — Mass Analyzer

The **heart** of the instrument. It separates ions in space or time according to their m/z.

### 2.3.1 Magnetic Sector Analyzer

A charged ion moving in a magnetic field follows a **circular path** with radius determined by:

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,r^{2}}{2\,V}$$

where:
- $B$ = magnetic field strength (**tesla**)
- $r$ = radius of ion trajectory (**m**)
- $V$ = accelerating voltage (**V**)
- $m/z$ = mass-to-charge ratio

**Operational implication:** Scanning $B$ (or $V$) sequentially brings ions of different m/z to the detector — yielding a **mass spectrum**.

### 2.3.2 Quadrupole Mass Analyzer

Four parallel cylindrical rods carry **DC voltage (U)** and **RF voltage (V cos ωt)**. Ion motion in the x-y plane is described by the **Mathieu equation**:

$$\frac{d^{2}u}{d\xi^{2}} + \left(a_u - 2q_u\cos 2\xi\right)\,u \;=\; 0$$

with dimensionless parameters:

$$a_u \;=\; \frac{4\,z\,e\,U}{m\,r_0^{2}\,\omega^{2}} \qquad \qquad q_u \;=\; \frac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m\,r_0^{2}\,\omega^{2}}$$

where $r_0$ = inscribed radius of the quadrupole, $\omega$ = RF frequency.

**Stability diagram (a, q plane):** Only ions whose trajectory is **stable** in both x and y directions traverse the quadrupole and reach the detector. By choosing (U, V) pairs on a specific **scan line**, only one m/z at a time is transmitted.

### 2.3.3 Time-of-Flight (TOF) Analyzer

Ions are given identical kinetic energy $E = zV$ and travel a field-free drift tube of length $L$:

$$E \;=\; z\,e\,V \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,m\,v^{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; v \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m}}$$

Time to reach the detector:

$$t \;=\; \frac{L}{v} \;=\; L\sqrt{\frac{m}{2\,z\,e\,V}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{2\,e\,V\,t^{2}}{L^{2}}\;}$$

**Result:** m/z is proportional to $t^{2}$ — heavier ions arrive later.

### 2.3.4 Comparison of Mass Analyzers (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Analyzer | Resolution | m/z Range | Scan Speed | Food-MS Use |
|----------|------------|-----------|------------|-------------|
| **Quadrupole (Q)** | Unit (1000) | ≤ 4000 | Fast | GC-MS, quantification |
| **Magnetic Sector** | 10,000–100,000 | ≤ 10,000 | Slow | High-resolution exact mass |
| **Time-of-Flight (TOF)** | 5,000–40,000 | > 100,000 | Very fast | Non-target screening |
| **Ion Trap (3D/Linear)** | 1000–10,000 | ≤ 6000 | Moderate | MSⁿ, structure elucidation |
| **Orbitrap** | > 100,000 | ≤ 6000 | Moderate | Proteomics, accurate mass |
| **FT-ICR** | > 1,000,000 | ≤ 10,000 | Slow | Ultra-high resolution |

---

## 2.4 Component 4 — Detector

The detector converts ion current into an electrical signal proportional to the **number of ions** arriving per unit time.

### 2.4.1 Common Detectors

| Detector | Principle | KTU Relevance |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Electron Multiplier (EM)** | Secondary electron emission off dynodes; gain **10⁶–10⁸** | Most common in GC-MS and LC-MS |
| **Photomultiplier (after conversion dynode)** | EM + scintillator | Magnetic sector instruments |
| **Microchannel Plate (MCP)** | Parallel array of micro-channels; fast response | TOF and ion-trap detectors |
| **Faraday Cup** | Direct current measurement; absolute quantitation | High-concentration analysis |
| **Focal Plane Array (FOD)** | Spatial detection of all m/z simultaneously | Mattauch-Herzog geometry |

### 2.4.2 Detector Output

The signal from a single ion package produces a peak whose:
- **Position (m/z)** = identity of the ion.
- **Area/Height** = abundance (concentration).
- **Width** = resolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *detector* with the *analyzer*. Remember: the **analyzer separates**; the **detector counts**.

---

## 2.5 Component 5 — Vacuum System and Data System

### 2.5.1 Vacuum System

**Why is high vacuum essential?**
- Mean free path of ions must exceed the instrument's flight path so that **ion-molecule collisions** (which cause scattering, fragmentation, and charge loss) are minimized.
- At **10⁻⁶ torr**, mean free path ≈ **10⁵ m** (huge).

**Typical pumping train:**
- **Rotary vane pump** (rough vacuum to ~10⁻³ torr).
- **Turbo-molecular pump** (high vacuum to 10⁻⁹ torr).
- Sometimes an **ion pump** (ultra-high vacuum for FT-ICR/Orbitrap).

### 2.5.2 Data Acquisition System

The data system performs:
1. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** of the detector signal.
2. **Centroiding** of peak profiles.
3. **Library matching** (NIST, mzCloud, MassBank).
4. **Quantification** via peak-area integration.
5. **Chromatogram reconstruction** in GC-MS/LC-MS.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table — every equation on this list is fair game for KTU ESE numerical problems.

| Equation | Symbols | Engineering Utility |
|----------|---------|---------------------|
| $m/z = B^{2}r^{2}/(2V)$ | Magnetic sector m/z | Resolving power design |
| $t = L\sqrt{m/(2zeV)}$ | TOF time-of-flight | Drift-tube calibration |
| $m/z = 2eVt^{2}/L^{2}$ | TOF m/z from time | Spectrum reconstruction |
| $R = m/\Delta m$ | Resolution | Instrument specification |
| $\Delta m = m/(2n)$ | Cycloid resolution (n = number of cycles) | Orbitrap/ion-trap design |
| $P = 10^{(6.5 + \log_{10}M)}$ | Pumping speed scaling (rough estimate) | Vacuum-system sizing |
| $q_{Rayleigh} = 8\pi\sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \gamma R^{3}}$ | Rayleigh charge limit | ESI droplet stability |
| $a_u, q_u$ | Mathieu parameters | Quadrupole stability analysis |

> *Note: No vertical bars used; set notation uses $\lvert x \rvert$ if required.*

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section satisfies the KTU ESE expectation that students be able to **derive** the working equations of a mass analyzer from first principles. Each derivation below is built from Newton's laws and electromagnetic fundamentals — no step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnetic Sector m/z Equation

**Starting Principle:** A charged ion accelerated through potential $V$ acquires kinetic energy equal to the work done by the electric field.

### Step 1: Energy gained by the ion in the ion source

$$\text{Work done} \;=\; qV \;=\; z\,e\,V$$

This work becomes kinetic energy:

$$\frac{1}{2}\,m\,v^{2} \;=\; z\,e\,V$$

### Step 2: Express velocity in terms of m/z

Solving for $v$:

$$v \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m}}$$

### Step 3: Apply circular motion in the magnetic field

When an ion enters a uniform magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to its velocity, the **magnetic Lorentz force** provides centripetal force:

$$z\,e\,v\,B \;=\; \frac{m\,v^{2}}{r}$$

Solving for $v$:

$$v \;=\; \frac{z\,e\,B\,r}{m}$$

### Step 4: Equate the two expressions for velocity

$$\sqrt{\frac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m}} \;=\; \frac{z\,e\,B\,r}{m}$$

### Step 5: Square both sides

$$\frac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m} \;=\; \frac{z^{2}\,e^{2}\,B^{2}\,r^{2}}{m^{2}}$$

### Step 6: Rearrange for m/z

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,r^{2}}{2\,V}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,r^{2}}{2\,V}\;}$$

### Step 7: Interpret the engineering meaning
- To scan m/z at constant $r$, **increase $B$** (magnetic scan) or **decrease $V$** (voltage scan).
- Doubling $B$ increases m/z by a factor of **4** (quadratic relationship).
- This is why magnetic-sector instruments use **electromagnets** rather than permanent magnets — they must vary $B$ continuously.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-of-Flight Equation

### Step 1: Same starting kinetic-energy equation

$$\frac{1}{2}\,m\,v^{2} \;=\; z\,e\,V$$

### Step 2: Solve for velocity

$$v \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m}}$$

### Step 3: Time to traverse a drift tube of length $L$

$$t \;=\; \frac{L}{v} \;=\; \frac{L}{\sqrt{2\,z\,e\,V/m}} \;=\; L\,\sqrt{\frac{m}{2\,z\,e\,V}}$$

$$\boxed{\;t \;=\; L\,\sqrt{\frac{m}{2\,z\,e\,V}}\;}$$

### Step 4: Square to obtain m/z

$$t^{2} \;=\; \frac{L^{2}\,m}{2\,z\,e\,V}$$

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{2\,e\,V\,t^{2}}{L^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{2\,e\,V\,t^{2}}{L^{2}}\;}$$

### Step 5: Resolution of TOF

Two ions of mass $m$ and $m + \Delta m$ are separated in time by $\Delta t$. Differentiating $t$ with respect to $m$:

$$\frac{dt}{dm} \;=\; \frac{L}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{2\,z\,e\,V\,m}}$$

$$R \;=\; \frac{m}{\Delta m} \;=\; \frac{t}{2\,\Delta t}$$

The factor of **2** in the denominator sets the theoretical resolution limit — to improve resolution, modern TOF instruments use an **ion mirror (reflectron)** to compensate for initial kinetic-energy spread, achieving $R > 20{,}000$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quadrupole Mathieu Stability (Outline)

### Step 1: Newton's second law in the quadrupole field

The electric potential on four rods (radius $r$, inscribed radius $r_0$) is:

$$\Phi(x,y,t) \;=\; \frac{(U + V\cos\omega t)}{r_0^{2}}\,(x^{2} - y^{2})$$

The electric field components are:

$$E_x \;=\; -\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x} \;=\; -\frac{2(U + V\cos\omega t)\,x}{r_0^{2}}$$

$$E_y \;=\; -\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial y} \;=\; +\frac{2(U + V\cos\omega t)\,y}{r_0^{2}}$$

### Step 2: Force equation for ion of mass m and charge q = ze

$$m\,\ddot{x} \;=\; -q\,E_x \;=\; \frac{2\,q\,(U + V\cos\omega t)\,x}{r_0^{2}}$$

$$m\,\ddot{y} \;=\; -q\,E_y \;=\; -\frac{2\,q\,(U + V\cos\omega t)\,y}{r_0^{2}}$$

### Step 3: Introduce dimensionless time

$$\xi \;=\; \frac{\omega t}{2} \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad \frac{d}{dt} \;=\; \frac{\omega}{2}\,\frac{d}{d\xi}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{d\xi^{2}} + \left(a_x - 2q_x\cos 2\xi\right)x \;=\; 0$$

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{d\xi^{2}} + \left(a_y + 2q_y\cos 2\xi\right)y \;=\; 0$$

with **Mathieu parameters**:

$$a_x \;=\; -a_y \;=\; \frac{4\,q\,U}{m\,r_0^{2}\,\omega^{2}} \qquad\qquad q_x \;=\; -q_y \;=\; \frac{2\,q\,V}{m\,r_0^{2}\,\omega^{2}}$$

### Step 4: Stability condition

For simultaneous stable oscillation in both x and y, the $(a, q)$ pair must lie in the **first stability region** of the Mathieu stability diagram. A typical mass scan uses a constant ratio $a/q$ (= 0.167 for best resolution), with both U and V ramped together.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You are NOT expected to solve the Mathieu equation in the exam — but you **must** state the equation, define $a$ and $q$, and explain that **only ions with stable trajectories** are transmitted.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Question:** A single-charged ion ($z = 1$, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C) is accelerated through $V = 1000$ V and enters a magnetic sector with $B = 0.5$ T. The detector is placed at $r = 0.10$ m. Calculate the m/z of the ion.

**Step 1 — Use the magnetic-sector equation:**

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,r^{2}}{2\,V}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{(0.5)^{2}\,(0.10)^{2}}{2 \times 1000} \;=\; \frac{0.25 \times 0.01}{2000}$$

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{kg/C}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to the conventional unit (Thomsons, m/z in Daltons when z = 1):**

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; 1.25 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kg/C} \times \frac{1\,\text{Da}}{1.6605 \times 10^{-27}\,\text{kg/e}}$$

$$\frac{m}{z} \;\approx\; 75.3 \;\text{Da}$$

**Answer:** The ion has a mass of approximately **75 Da** (consistent with glycine, the lightest amino acid, $C_2H_5NO_2 = 75.07$ Da).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component-by-Component Decision Logic

When solving a KTU question on "Which component is suitable for X?", use this matrix:

| Food Analysis Task | Required Performance | Best Component |
|--------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Quantify aflatoxin B1 at 1 µg/kg | High sensitivity, selectivity | **Triple quadrupole (QQQ), MRM mode** |
| Identify unknown contaminant in milk | Accurate mass, MS/MS | **Q-TOF or Orbitrap** |
| Profile volatile aroma in cardamom | Volatile, small molecule | **GC-MS with EI source** |
| Detect polar pesticide in grapes | Polar, thermally labile | **LC-ESI-MS/MS** |
| Determine Pb, As in rice | Elemental analysis | **ICP-MS** |
| Sequence a milk protein | High mass, MS/MS capability | **MALDI-TOF/TOF or nano-ESI-LC-MS/MS** |

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right MS Configuration for a Food Question

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{"What is the analyte polarity?"}:::q
    Q1 -- Non polar volatile --> R1["GC-MS with EI Source"]:::r
    Q1 -- Polar thermally labile --> R2["LC-MS with ESI Source"]:::r
    Q1 -- Trace metals --> R3["ICP-MS"]:::r

    Q2{"What is the analytical goal?"}:::q
    Q2 -- Quantification at trace level --> R4["Triple Quadrupole MRM Mode"]:::r
    Q2 -- Unknown identification --> R5["Q-TOF or Orbitrap Accurate Mass"]:::r
    Q2 -- Structure elucidation MSn --> R6["Ion Trap"]:::r

    classDef q fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

### Q1. List the five essential components of a mass spectrometer and state the function of any one. `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> The five components are: (1) Sample inlet system, (2) Ion source, (3) Mass analyzer, (4) Detector, and (5) Data acquisition system, all maintained under high vacuum.
>
> *Function of the ion source:* The ion source converts neutral analyte molecules into gas-phase ions. In Electron Ionization (EI), a 70 eV electron beam bombards the sample, ejecting an electron and producing a radical cation $M^{\bullet+}$, which is then extracted into the mass analyzer.

---

### Q2. Why is a high-vacuum environment essential in a mass spectrometer? `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> A high vacuum ($10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ torr) is required to ensure that the **mean free path of ions** exceeds the flight-path length, so that ion-molecule collisions are negligible. Without vacuum, ions would (i) collide with residual gas and scatter, (ii) undergo unwanted fragmentation, (iii) lose charge, and (iv) produce a noisy, irreproducible spectrum. Vacuum is maintained by a combination of rough (rotary) and high-vacuum (turbomolecular) pumps.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five essential components of a mass spectrometer**. Describe the working of the **Electron Ionization (EI) source** in detail. `[7 marks]`

**(b)** A singly charged ion ($z = 1$) of mass 100 Da is accelerated through 5000 V in a magnetic-sector mass spectrometer. If the magnetic field is 0.6 T, calculate the **radius of the ion trajectory**. What accelerating voltage would be required to focus an ion of mass 200 Da at the same radius and field? `[7 marks]`

#### Solution to Q.A(a):

**Block Diagram:** (Draw or recall the 5-block schematic from Section 4.1.)

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Inlet | Delivers sample (from GC, LC, or probe) into the vacuum |
| Ion Source | Converts molecules to ions (EI, ESI, APCI, MALDI, ICP) |
| Mass Analyzer | Separates ions by m/z (Quadrupole, TOF, Sector, Trap) |
| Detector | Counts ions and converts current to voltage |
| Data System | Records, displays, and identifies the spectrum |

**Working of EI Source (Step-by-Step):**

1. A rhenium or tungsten filament is heated electrically to ~2000 °C, emitting electrons via **thermionic emission**.
2. Electrons are accelerated through a potential difference of **70 V** toward an anode.
3. The accelerating electrons acquire **70 eV** of kinetic energy.
4. Analyte molecules (from the GC) enter the ionization chamber as a vapor.
5. When a 70-eV electron strikes a molecule $M$, energy transfer ejects an electron:
$$M + e^{-} \;\longrightarrow\; M^{\bullet+} + 2e^{-}$$
6. The radical cation $M^{\bullet+}$ is unstable and fragments, producing characteristic daughter ions.
7. A small positive potential on the **repeller plate** pushes the positive ions toward the exit slit.
8. An **einzel lens** focuses the ion beam into the mass analyzer.

> **Key Standard:** EI is universally performed at 70 eV because this produces **reproducible, library-searchable** spectra.

#### Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- $z = 1$, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $m = 100 \,\text{Da} = 100 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-25}$ kg
- $V_1 = 5000$ V, $B = 0.6$ T

**Step 1 — Apply the magnetic-sector equation:**

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,r^{2}}{2\,V}$$

Solve for $r$:

$$r \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2\,V\,(m/z)}{B^{2}}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $m/z$ in SI units:**

$$\frac{m}{z} \;=\; \frac{1.6605 \times 10^{-25}\,\text{kg}}{1} \;=\; 1.6605 \times 10^{-25}\,\text{kg/e}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$r \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-25}}{(0.6)^{2}}}$$

$$r \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1.6605 \times 10^{-21}}{0.36}}$$

$$r \;=\; \sqrt{4.6125 \times 10^{-21}} \;=\; 6.79 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1:** Many students forget to convert **Daltons → kilograms** (1 Da = $1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg). This unit-conversion error alone can lose you **3 out of 7 marks** in this sub-question. Always write the unit-conversion step explicitly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #2:** When finding the new voltage for the 200-Da ion, students often mistakenly halve $V$ (because mass doubled). In reality, $m/z \propto V$ at fixed $r$ and $B$ — so $V$ must **double** to **200 Da**. Read the magnetic-sector equation again: $V = B^2 r^2/(2\,(m/z))$.

**Step 4 — Voltage required for the 200-Da ion at the same $r$ and $B$:**

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} \;=\; \frac{m_2/z}{m_1/z} \;=\; \frac{200}{100} \;=\; 2$$

$$V_2 \;=\; 2 \times 5000 \;=\; 10{,}000 \;\text{V}$$

**Answer:** $r \approx 6.79 \times 10^{-11}$ m for the 100-Da ion; $V_2 = 10{,}000$ V for the 200-Da ion.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** Compare the working principles of the **quadrupole** and **time-of-flight** mass analyzers. Mention any two advantages of each. `[7 marks]`

**(b)** A pesticide residue is analyzed by LC-ESI-MS/MS. Explain: (i) how ions are produced in ESI, (ii) the role of the **multiple reaction monitoring (MRM)** mode in quantification, and (iii) why MRM provides higher selectivity than **full-scan** mode. `[7 marks]`

#### Solution to Q.B(a):

**Quadrupole Analyzer:**

- **Principle:** Four cylindrical rods carry DC ($U$) and RF ($V\cos\omega t$) voltages. The resulting quadrupole electric field filters ions by m/z according to the stability of their trajectory described by the Mathieu equation.
- **Transmission:** Only ions whose $(a, q)$ pair lies in the **first stability region** traverse the rods; all others are lost.
- **Scan:** Scanning $U$ and $V$ (at a fixed $a/q$ ratio) sequentially transmits each m/z.
- **Advantages:**
  1. Compact, robust, inexpensive.
  2. Very fast scan speeds; ideal for GC-MS and chromatographic detection.
  3. Unit resolution is sufficient for most quantitative work.

**Time-of-Flight Analyzer:**

- **Principle:** Ions are accelerated to equal kinetic energy $zV$ and allowed to drift through a field-free tube of length $L$. Since $v = \sqrt{2zV/m}$, lighter ions arrive first.
- **Equation:** $t = L\sqrt{m/(2zV)}$, giving $m/z = 2eVt^{2}/L^{2}$.
- **Mass spectrum:** Obtained from arrival-time distribution.
- **Advantages:**
  1. Theoretically unlimited mass range.
  2. Very fast acquisition; acquires the **entire spectrum** in microseconds.
  3. Excellent for coupling with pulsed ionization (MALDI).

#### Solution to Q.B(b):

**(i) Ion Production in ESI:**

The analyte solution is pushed through a steel capillary held at **+2 to +5 kV**. The electric field at the tip deforms the liquid meniscus into a **Taylor cone**. Charged droplets are ejected; solvent evaporates as the droplet travels through a heated desolvation region. When the droplet reaches the **Rayleigh limit** (charge exceeds surface-tension stability), **Coulombic fission** occurs, repeatedly producing smaller droplets. Ultimately, gas-phase ions are released via either the **Ion Evaporation Model (IEM)** or the **Charge Residue Model (CRM)**, entering the mass analyzer.

**(ii) Role of MRM:**

In a triple-quadrupole instrument (Q1 → q2 → Q3), the first quadrupole (**Q1**) selects a single **precursor ion** of interest (specific m/z of, e.g., the pesticide's molecular ion $[M+H]^+$). This precursor is fragmented in **q2** (a collision cell filled with argon at ~10⁻³ torr). The third quadrupole (**Q3**) then selects one specific **product ion** (fragment). The detector records the intensity of this single transition.

$$[M+H]^{+} \;\xrightarrow{\text{CID in q2}}\; \text{fragments} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Q3}}\; \text{single product ion monitored}$$

**(iii) Why MRM is More Selective than Full-Scan:**

- **Full-scan** mode monitors *all* m/z values in a wide window. Many compounds (matrix, isobaric interferences) may contribute signal at the same nominal m/z, raising the chemical background and degrading signal-to-noise ratio (S/N).
- **MRM** monitors **two stages of mass selectivity simultaneously** (precursor m/z **and** product m/z). The probability that an unrelated compound happens to (a) produce a precursor of the *exact same nominal mass* **and** (b) fragment to give a product of the *exact same nominal mass* is extremely low.
- Result: **dramatically reduced background**, **improved S/N**, and **lower limits of detection (LOD)** — typically 100–1000× better than full scan. This is why **MRM is the gold-standard for quantification** of regulated food contaminants (pesticides, mycotoxins, veterinary drug residues).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #3:** In MRM questions, students often forget to mention that **the collision cell q2 is NOT a mass analyzer** — it is operated in RF-only mode to transmit and fragment all ions. This subtle point distinguishes a high-score answer from a medium-score one.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #4:** Students frequently confuse "MRM" with "SIM" (Selected Ion Monitoring). SIM monitors *one* m/z in one stage; MRM monitors *two* m/z values (precursor → product) across two stages. Both improve selectivity, but MRM is **far more selective**.

---

## 5.3 Common Student Errors — KTU Examiner's Consolidated Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 mistakes students make on this topic in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "mass analyzer" with "detector"** — the analyzer *separates*; the detector *counts*.
> 2. **Writing EI at energies other than 70 eV** — this is the universal standard; deviating is wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversion** between Da and kg in numericals.
> 4. **Calling ESI "Electron Spray Ionization"** — it is "**Electro**spray Ionization."
> 5. **Omitting the vacuum system** when listing the "components" — although sometimes counted as a support system, KTU examiners often expect it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Components of a Mass Spectrometer**

- ✅ A mass spectrometer has **five essential components**: inlet, ion source, mass analyzer, detector, data system — all under **high vacuum** ($10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ torr).

- ✅ **Inlet** choices depend on analyte volatility — GC for volatiles, LC for polar/thermolabile, direct probe for solids, ICP for metals.

- ✅ **Electron Ionization (EI)** uses a **70 eV** electron beam and produces a **radical cation** $M^{\bullet+}$ plus fragments. Standardized at 70 eV for reproducible library matching (NIST, Wiley).

- ✅ **Electrospray Ionization (ESI)** is a **soft** technique producing **multiply charged ions**; ideal for proteins, peptides, and polar pesticides. Involves a **Taylor cone**, **Coulombic fission**, and the **Rayleigh limit**.

- ✅ **Magnetic sector analyzer** follows $m/z = B^{2}r^{2}/(2V)$; heavy ions need **larger $B$** or **smaller $V$** to reach the same radius.

- ✅ **Quadrupole analyzer** uses **Mathieu equation** stability; only one m/z is transmitted at a time; cheap, fast, robust.

- ✅ **Time-of-flight (TOF)** uses $m/z = 2eVt^{2}/L^{2}$; mass determined by arrival time; very fast and unlimited mass range.

- ✅ **Triple quadrupole (QQQ)** enables **MRM** mode — two-stage mass filtering — the **gold standard** for trace quantification in food (mycotoxins, pesticides).

- ✅ **Detectors** (electron multiplier, MCP, Faraday cup) convert ion current to voltage; **dynamic range** matters for quantification.

- ✅ **ICP-MS** is the technique of choice for trace metals (Pb, As, Cd, Hg) in food.

- ✅ **Resolution** $R = m/\Delta m$; **Orbitrap and FT-ICR** give the highest resolution (>100,000) and are used for **non-target screening** and **proteomics**.

- ✅ In KTU exam numericals: **always convert Da to kg** using $1 \,\text{Da} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg.

- ✅ In KTU exam theory: **always mention vacuum**, **mention units**, and **label every block of your diagram**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Mass Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Applications of Mass Spectroscopy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Mass Spectrometry (MS)** is an advanced analytical technique that measures the **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** of ionized molecules, providing both **qualitative** (structural and identity) and **quantitative** (concentration) information about analytes. In the context of food analysis, Mass Spectroscopy is employed as a definitive confirmatory and discovery tool capable of detecting compounds at **parts-per-trillion (ppt)** levels with high selectivity and specificity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A mass spectrometer is fundamentally a *mass-to-charge ratio separator* combined with an *ion detector*. Its primary function in food science is unambiguous identification of unknown contaminants, adulterants, and bioactives that other techniques (HPLC, GC-FID, spectrophotometry) cannot confirm. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Postal Sorting Office" Imagine a huge mail sorting office. Thousands of letters (molecules) of different sizes and shapes arrive at once. In the mass spectrometer: 1. **Letters (sample molecules) are first stamped with a barcode** — this is *ionization* (giving each molecule a charge). 2. **They are then sorted by weight along a moving conveyor belt or through magnetic fields** — this is *mass analysis* (separation by m/z). 3. **Finally, each sorted letter is read and counted by a scanner** — this is *detection* (generating the mass spectrum). Just as postal sorting lets you identify *who sent what* and *how many letters* arrived, MS lets us identify *which molecules are present* in a food and *how much* of each is there. The "barcode" (charge) and "weight" (mass) combine to give a unique identifier — the **m/z value**. ### 1.3 Why Mass Spectrometry is Indispensable in Food Analysis | Functional Need | Why MS is the Gold Standard | |---|---| | **Confirming pesticide residues** | Single Quadrupole MS provides m/z match against libraries | | **Detecting veterinary drug residues (antibiotics, hormones)** | Tandem MS (MS/MS) eliminates matrix interferences | | **Mycotoxin detection (Aflatoxin, Ochratoxin)** | Sensitivity down to **< 1 ng/kg (ppb–ppt)** | | **Food adulteration (e.g., melamine in milk)** | Accurate mass measurement via TOF/Orbitrap | | **Authentication (olive oil, honey, wine origin)** | Profiling unique metabolite fingerprints | | **Packaging migrants (BPA, phthalates)** | GC-MS for volatiles; LC-MS for non-volatiles | | **Proteomics / Allergen detection** | MALDI-TOF for intact protein masses | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 3, the focus is on *applications* — not on instrumentation theory. You must remember the **ionization technique used**, the **type of mass analyzer coupled**, and the **specific food analyte class** detected. This tripartite association is the most frequently examined combination. ### 1.4 Core Physical Constants and Metrics - **Elementary charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ - **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ - **Mass unit:** **1 Da (Dalton) = 1/12 mass of $^{12}$C atom = $1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg** - **Resolving power ($R$):** $R = m/\Delta m$ — typically **> 10,000** for high-resolution instruments - **Detection limit (LOD):** routinely **parts-per-billion (ppb) to parts-per-trillion (ppt)** in food matrices - **Dynamic range:** **5–6 orders of magnitude** for modern instruments ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Operational Logic of Mass Spectrometry The complete MS workflow consists of **four sequential stages**. Understanding this pipeline is critical for KTU essay-type questions on applications. **Step 1 — Sample Introduction** - The food extract (after cleanup by QuEChERS, SPE, or liquid-liquid extraction) is introduced as a gas (for GC-MS) or liquid (for LC-MS). **Step 2 — Ionization (Formation of Ions in Gas Phase)** - Neutral molecules are converted into ions (cationized or anionized). - The choice of ionization source determines which analytes can be analyzed: | Ionization Source | Ionization Mechanism | Best For (Food Applications) | |---|---|---| | **EI (Electron Impact)** | 70 eV electrons knock out an electron | Volatile, thermally stable compounds (pesticides, PAHs, flavors) | | **CI (Chemical Ionization)** | Reagent gas (CH₄, NH₃) transfers proton | Confirmation of molecular ion [M+H]⁺ | | **ESI (Electrospray Ionization)** | Spray + high voltage; soft ionization | Polar/thermolabile compounds (mycotoxins, peptides, veterinary drugs) | | **APCI (Atmospheric Pressure Chemical Ionization)** | Corona discharge; less polar than ESI | Mid-polarity pesticides, vitamins | | **MALDI (Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization)** | Laser + UV-absorbing matrix | Proteins, peptides, biopolymers (allergens) | | **ICP (Inductively Coupled Plasma)** | Argon plasma at ~10,000 K | Elemental analysis (heavy metals: Pb, Cd, As, Hg) | **Step 3 — Mass Analysis (Separation of Ions by m/z)** - Ions are separated based on their m/z using electric/magnetic fields. | Mass Analyzer | Separation Principle | Strength for Food Analysis | |---|---|---| | **Quadrupole (Q)** | Stable trajectories in RF/DC fields | Quantitative targeted analysis (pesticide residues) | | **Time-of-Flight (TOF)** | $t \propto \sqrt{m/z}$ in field-free drift | Accurate mass, high resolution | | **Ion Trap (3D/Linear)** | Ejection by resonance frequency | Structural elucidation (MSⁿ) | | **FT-ICR / Orbitrap** | Image current at cyclotron frequency | Ultra-high resolution, proteomics | | **Magnetic Sector** | Curvature in magnetic field | High resolution isotope ratio | **Step 4 — Detection and Data Processing** - Ions strike a detector (electron multiplier, microchannel plate). - Signal intensity vs. m/z is plotted as the **mass spectrum**. - Spectral libraries (e.g., NIST, MassBank) enable compound identification. ### 2.2 Critical Equations in Mass Spectrometry #### 2.2.1 The Fundamental m/z Relationship For a singly charged ion ($z = 1$): $$m/z = \frac{m_{\text{ion}}}{e}$$ For a multiply charged ion: $$m/z = \frac{n \cdot m_{\text{proton}} + M_{\text{analyte}}}{n \cdot e}$$ where $n$ = number of charges, $M_{\text{analyte}}$ = neutral molecular mass. #### 2.2.2 Time-of-Flight (TOF) Equation In a linear TOF analyzer with accelerating voltage $V$ and drift length $L$: $$t = L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$$ The kinetic energy of the ion is: $$zeV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$ Solving for $t$: $$t = \frac{L}{v} = L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$$ **Implication for food analysis:** Lighter ions (small molecules like pesticides) reach the detector *first*; heavier ions (proteins) arrive *later*. #### 2.2.3 Resolving Power (R) $$R = \frac{m}{\Delta m}$$ For two peaks to be baseline-separated at 10% valley: $$\Delta m_{\text{min}} = \frac{m}{R}$$ **Implication:** At $m/z$ = 400 with $R$ = 10,000, the minimum mass difference resolvable is $\Delta m = 0.04$ Da — sufficient to distinguish co-eluting isobaric compounds. #### 2.2.4 Quadrupole Stability Parameters (Mathieu Equation) The motion of an ion in a quadrupole field is governed by the **Mathieu equation**: $$\frac{d^2u}{d\xi^2} + (a_u - 2q_u \cos 2\xi)u = 0$$ where $u$ = $x$ or $y$ coordinate, $\xi = \omega t / 2$, and: $$a_u = \frac{4zeU}{m\omega^2 r_0^2}, \quad q_u = \frac{2zeV}{m\omega^2 r_0^2}$$ Stability is achieved only for specific $(a, q)$ combinations, allowing mass-selective transmission. #### 2.2.5 Sensitivity and Detection Limit The **Limit of Detection (LOD)** is typically defined as: $$\text{LOD} = \frac{3.3 \cdot \sigma}{S}$$ where $\sigma$ = standard deviation of blank response, $S$ = slope of calibration curve. **Limit of Quantification (LOQ):** $$\text{LOQ} = \frac{10 \cdot \sigma}{S}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield:** Regulatory food safety bodies like **FSSAI (India)**, **Codex Alimentarius**, **US FDA**, and **EU MRLs (Maximum Residue Limits)** specify MRLs in **mg/kg (ppm)** or **µg/kg (ppb)**. LOQ of the MS method must be **≤ 1/3 of the MRL** to ensure regulatory compliance. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application Context | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $m/z = M/z \cdot e$ | m/z ratio | dimensionless | Foundational identity | | 2 | $t = L\sqrt{m/(2zeV)}$ | TOF flight time | seconds (s) | TOF analyzer | | 3 | $R = m/\Delta m$ | Resolving power | dimensionless | Instrument capability | | 4 | $E_k = zeV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Kinetic energy | electron-volts (eV) | Ion acceleration | | 5 | $a = 4zeU/(m\omega^2 r_0^2)$ | Mathieu a-parameter | dimensionless | Quadrupole stability | | 6 | $q = 2zeV/(m\omega^2 r_0^2)$ | Mathieu q-parameter | dimensionless | Quadrupole stability | | 7 | $\text{LOD} = 3.3\sigma/S$ | Limit of detection | ng/mL or µg/kg | Trace residue analysis | | 8 | $\text{LOQ} = 10\sigma/S$ | Limit of quantification | ng/mL or µg/kg | Quantitative method validation | | 9 | $\%R = (C_{\text{measured}}/C_{\text{true}}) \times 100$ | Recovery | % | Method validation | | 10 | $C_{\text{isotope}} = R \cdot C_{\text{parent}}$ | Isotope dilution | concentration | Isotope dilution MS | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Industrial Utility Mass Spectrometry-based methods form the regulatory backbone of: - **International food trade:** Confirming pesticide residue compliance for export. - **Authentication markets:** Detecting economically motivated adulteration (EMA) in olive oil, honey, saffron, milk powder. - **Sports anti-doping food testing:** Hormone residues in animal-derived foods. - **Nutraceutical verification:** Confirming molecular weight of bioactive peptides. - **Genetically modified organism (GMO) quantification:** PCR-MS coupling. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Time-of-Flight (TOF) Mass Analyzer This derivation is essential for understanding the basic physical principle underlying TOF analyzers, widely used in food safety labs (e.g., pesticide screening on GC-TOFMS, protein identification on MALDI-TOF). **Setup:** A packet of ions is accelerated through a potential difference $V$ and allowed to drift through a field-free region of length $L$ before striking the detector. **Step 1 — Energy Conservation in the Ion Source** The potential energy $zeV$ acquired by an ion of charge $ze$ through the accelerating voltage $V$ is fully converted into kinetic energy as it exits the source: $$zeV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$ **Step 2 — Solve for Velocity** Rearranging: $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2zeV}{m}}$$ This shows that **ions of lower mass exit with higher velocity** and vice versa. **Step 3 — Time to Traverse Field-Free Region** Since the field-free region has constant velocity $v$, the time-of-flight is: $$t = \frac{L}{v} = L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$$ **Step 4 — Express m/z in Terms of t** Squaring both sides: $$t^2 = \frac{L^2 m}{2zeV}$$ Solving for $m/z$: $$m/z = \frac{2eV \cdot t^2}{L^2}$$ **Step 5 — Key Engineering Insight** Because $V$ and $L$ are instrument constants, **t² is directly proportional to m/z**. Thus, plotting $t^2$ (or $t$) versus known m/z standards gives a **linear calibration curve**, which is the working principle of TOF-MS. **Step 6 — Numerical Illustration (KTU-style problem)** Consider a TOF-MS with $L = 1.5$ m and $V = 20{,}000$ V. Calculate the flight time for an ion of $m = 200$ Da carrying charge $z = 1$ (singly charged). Given: $m = 200 \text{ Da} = 200 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg} = 3.321 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg}$ $z = 1$, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V = 2 \times 10^4$ V, $L = 1.5$ m Substitute into: $$t = L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{m}{2zeV}}$$ $$t = 1.5 \times \sqrt{\frac{3.321 \times 10^{-25}}{2 \times 1 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^4}}$$ $$t = 1.5 \times \sqrt{\frac{3.321 \times 10^{-25}}{6.408 \times 10^{-15}}}$$ $$t = 1.5 \times \sqrt{5.183 \times 10^{-11}}$$ $$t = 1.5 \times 7.199 \times 10^{-6} = 1.080 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 10.8 \text{ μs}$$ **Interpretation:** A 200 Da ion reaches the detector in about **10.8 microseconds**. A 400 Da ion would take $t \propto \sqrt{m}$, so $t_{400} = 10.8 \times \sqrt{2} = 15.3$ μs. ### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Resolving Power of a Sector Instrument A magnetic sector mass spectrometer deflects an ion of mass $m$, charge $z$, and velocity $v$ through a circular path of radius $r$ in a uniform magnetic field $B$: **Step 1 — Centripetal Force Equates to Magnetic Force** $$\frac{mv^2}{r} = Bzv$$ **Step 2 — Solve for r** $$r = \frac{mv}{Bz}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute Kinetic Energy** From $\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = zeV$, we have $v = \sqrt{2zeV/m}$. Substituting: $$r = \frac{m}{Bz} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2zeV}{m}} = \frac{1}{B} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2mV}{z}}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate to Get Resolving Power** Differentiating $r$ with respect to $m$ and taking ratios yields the resolving power: $$R = \frac{m}{\Delta m} = \frac{r}{2 \cdot \Delta r_{\text{slit}}}$$ where $\Delta r_{\text{slit}}$ is the slit width. Smaller slit width $\Rightarrow$ higher resolution, but lower transmission (sensitivity). **Step 5 — Food Analysis Implication** For confirmation of pesticide isomers (e.g., **lindane vs α-hexachlorocyclohexane**), $R \geq 10{,}000$ is required to baseline-separate isobaric peaks. This drives the choice of high-resolution mass spectrometers in regulatory food labs. ### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Limit of Detection from Calibration Curve In quantitative MS analysis (e.g., aflatoxin B1 in peanuts by LC-MS/MS), the method validation requires establishing LOD/LOQ. **Step 1 — Calibration Curve Equation** Linear regression of instrument response $y$ vs. concentration $x$ yields: $$y = S \cdot x + b$$ where $S$ = slope (sensitivity) and $b$ = intercept. **Step 2 — Estimate of Standard Deviation of Blank** The standard deviation $\sigma$ of replicate blank measurements is computed: $$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n-1}}$$ **Step 3 — Apply ICH Definition of LOD** According to ICH Q2(R1) guidelines adopted in food safety: $$\text{LOD} = \frac{3.3 \cdot \sigma}{S}, \quad \text{LOQ} = \frac{10 \cdot \sigma}{S}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example (Aflatoxin B1)** A blank matrix shows $\sigma = 0.05$ peak area units, and the slope of calibration is $S = 250$ peak area units per µg/kg. $$\text{LOD} = \frac{3.3 \times 0.05}{250} = 6.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ µg/kg} = 0.66 \text{ ng/kg (ppt)}$$ $$\text{LOQ} = \frac{10 \times 0.05}{250} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ µg/kg} = 2 \text{ ng/kg (ppt)}$$ **Interpretation:** This LC-MS/MS method can detect aflatoxin B1 at **sub-ppt levels** — well below the **FSSAI MRL of 30 µg/kg** for groundnuts, demonstrating regulatory sufficiency. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application ↔ Ionization ↔ Analyzer ↔ Analyte | Food Application | Typical Analyte Class | Ionization Source | Mass Analyzer | Hyphenation | Expected LOD | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Pesticide residue screening** | Organochlorine, organophosphate, pyrethroids | EI (GC) / ESI (LC) | Quadrupole (Q) | GC-MS, LC-MS/MS | 1–10 µg/kg | | **Veterinary drug residues** | Antibiotics (tetracyclines, sulfonamides), hormones | ESI (+) | Triple Quadrupole (QqQ) | LC-MS/MS | 0.1–1 µg/kg | | **Mycotoxins (AFB1, OTA, ZEN)** | Aflatoxins, ochratoxin, zearalenone | ESI (+) | QqQ or Q-TOF | LC-MS/MS | 0.01–1 µg/kg | | **Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, As, Hg)** | Elemental ions | ICP (Ar plasma) | Quadrupole | ICP-MS | 0.001–0.1 mg/kg | | **Flavor & aroma compounds** | Volatiles, terpenes, esters | EI | Single Quadrupole or TOF | GC-MS | ng/L | | **Food adulteration (melamine in milk)** | Triazine, cyanuric acid | ESI (+) | Q-TOF or Q-Orbitrap | LC-MS/MS | 0.05 mg/kg | | **Allergens (peanut, gluten proteins)** | Proteins, peptides | MALDI / ESI | TOF | MALDI-TOF, LC-MS/MS | 1–5 mg/kg | | **BPA / Phthalate migrants** | Endocrine disruptors | ESI (−) / APCI | QqQ | LC-MS/MS | 0.1–1 µg/kg | | **Olive oil authentication** | Triglyceride, phenolic profiles | ESI (+) / APCI | Q-TOF / Orbitrap | LC-HRMS | Fingerprint | | **Marine biotoxins (PSP, DSP)** | Saxitoxin, okadaic acid | ESI (+) | QqQ | LC-MS/MS | µg/kg | | **Acrylamide in fried foods** | Acrylamide | ESI (+) | QqQ | LC-MS/MS | 5–50 µg/kg | | **Dioxins & PCBs** | Persistent organic pollutants | EI (high res) | Magnetic sector | GC-HRMS | fg/kg | ### 3.5 Worked Example — Quantitative Determination of Chlorpyrifos Pesticide Residue in Apple by GC-MS This end-to-end worked example is a typical KTU ESE Part B question pattern. **Problem Statement:** A sample of apple extract shows a chlorpyrifos (MW = 350.6 Da) peak at m/z = 349.6 in GC-MS (EI mode) and at m/z = 351.6 in ESI-MS. The calibration curve slope is $S = 1250$ counts/µg/kg and blank SD is $\sigma = 45$ counts. Calculate LOD and LOQ. If the measured peak area gives a concentration of 28 µg/kg, comment on regulatory compliance against FSSAI MRL of 50 µg/kg. **Step 1 — Identify the Analyte** Chlorpyrifos molecular formula: $\text{C}_9\text{H}_{11}\text{Cl}_3\text{NO}_3\text{PS}$ - $^{35}\text{Cl}$ isotope: $M = 349.6$ Da - $^{37}\text{Cl}$ isotope: $M = 351.6$ Da - The 2 Da spacing is the **isotopic signature of chlorine** — a major diagnostic for chlorpyrifos identity in MS. **Step 2 — Calculate LOD and LOQ** $$\text{LOD} = \frac{3.3 \times 45}{1250} = 0.119 \text{ µg/kg}$$ $$\text{LOQ} = \frac{10 \times 45}{1250} = 0.36 \text{ µg/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Compare to Regulatory Limit** Measured concentration = 28 µg/kg FSSAI MRL = 50 µg/kg Since $28 < 50$ and $28 \gg \text{LOQ} = 0.36$, the result is **regulatory compliant**. **Step 4 — Discuss Confirmation Criteria (KTU Exam Favorite)** For unambiguous identification under SANTE/CODEX guidelines, the following are required: - **Retention time match:** within ± 0.1 min of authentic standard - **Two diagnostic ions:** the 349.6 and 351.6 (molecular ion cluster) and one fragment (e.g., m/z = 197 from loss of –C₂H₃Cl₂) - **Ion ratio match:** the abundance ratio of the two ions must match that of the standard within ± 30% ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mass Spectrometer Coupled to GC ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sample Preparation
QuEChERS / SPE] --> B[GC Injection Port
Splitless Mode] B --> C[GC Column
DB-5ms 30m x 0.25mm] C --> D[Transfer Line
Heated to 280 C] D --> E[Ion Source
Electron Impact 70 eV] E --> F[Quadrupole Mass Analyzer
Mass Filter m/z 50 to 500] F --> G[Electron Multiplier Detector] G --> H[Data System
NIST Library Match] H --> I[Identification and Quantification] style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B fill:#ffd699,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#ffc966,stroke:#333,color:#000 style D fill:#ffbf33,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E fill:#ffb833,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F fill:#ffaa00,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G fill:#ff9900,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H fill:#ff8800,stroke:#333,color:#000 style I fill:#ff6600,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** This block diagram traces the complete analytical signal flow in a typical GC-MS workflow for pesticide residue analysis. Each block has a single well-defined function, and the signal (analyte molecule → ion → m/z value → spectrum) flows unidirectionally. ### 4.2 Block Diagram — LC-MS/MS Configuration for Mycotoxin Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Extraction ["Sample Preparation Stage"] E1[Weigh 5 g Sample] --> E2[Add 20 mL Acetonitrile Water 80 20] E2 --> E3[QuEChERS Salts MgSO4 NaCl] E3 --> E4[Centrifuge 5000 rpm 5 min] E4 --> E5[Cleanup dSPE PSA C18] end E5 --> LC1[HPLC Pump
Binary Gradient] LC1 --> LC2[C18 Column
150 mm x 4.6 mm 5 um] LC2 --> LC3[ESI Source
Positive Mode 4.5 kV] LC3 --> Q1[Q1 First Quadrupole
Selects Parent Ion] Q1 --> Q2[Q2 Collision Cell
Argon CID 25 eV] Q2 --> Q3[Q3 Second Quadrupole
Selects Product Ions] Q3 --> DET[Electron Multiplier
Detector] DET --> COMP[Computer
MRM Data Processing] style E1 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E2 fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E3 fill:#80deea,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E4 fill:#4dd0e1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E5 fill:#26c6da,stroke:#333,color:#000 style LC1 fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#333,color:#000 style LC2 fill:#9575cd,stroke:#333,color:#000 style LC3 fill:#7e57c2,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q1 fill:#673ab7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q2 fill:#5e35b1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q3 fill:#512da8,stroke:#333,color:#000 style DET fill:#4527a0,stroke:#333,color:#000 style COMP fill:#311b92,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** The triple quadrupole (QqQ) configuration enables **Multiple Reaction Monitoring (MRM)** — the most sensitive quantitative scan mode for targeted mycotoxin analysis. The Q1 selects the molecular ion, Q2 fragments it, and Q3 monitors one specific fragment — providing two stages of mass selection for unmatched selectivity in complex food matrices. ### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right MS Configuration for Food Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Food Analyte to be Analyzed] --> Q1{Is the analyte
volatile and
thermostable?} Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Use GC-MS
EI 70 eV
Single Quadrupole] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the analyte
polar and
thermolabile?} Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Use LC-MS
ESI Source
Quadrupole or Q-TOF] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is the analyte
a large
biopolymer?} Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Use MALDI-TOF
or LC-ESI-MS
for Proteins] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need elemental
analysis?} Q4 -->|Yes| A4[Use ICP-MS
for Heavy Metals
and Trace Elements] Q4 -->|No| Q5{Need structural
elucidation
MS to the power n?} Q5 -->|Yes| A5[Use Ion Trap
for MSn fragmentation] Q5 -->|No| A6[Use HRMS
Orbitrap or FT-ICR
for Accurate Mass] A1 --> DONE[Method Selected] A2 --> DONE A3 --> DONE A4 --> DONE A5 --> DONE A6 --> DONE style START fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q1 fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q2 fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q3 fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q4 fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q5 fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A3 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A4 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A5 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A6 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,color:#000 style DONE fill:#66bb6a,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** This decision tree is a high-yield KTU revision tool. For any food analyte, the answer is a triad: **(a) sample introduction (GC or LC), (b) ionization source, (c) mass analyzer**. Train yourself to walk through this tree quickly during the exam. ### 4.4 ICP-MS Block Diagram for Heavy Metal Analysis ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Liquid Food Sample
e.g. Milk Digest] --> S2[Peristaltic Pump
1 mL per min] S2 --> S3[Nebulizer
Concentric or Microflow] S3 --> S4[Spray Chamber
Scott Type 2 C] S4 --> S5[Argon Plasma Torch
10000 K Ionization] S5 --> S6[Sampler Cone
1.0 mm Orifice] S6 --> S7[Skimmer Cone
0.4 mm Orifice] S7 --> S8[Quadrupole Mass Filter
m/z 7 to 250] S8 --> S9[Electron Multiplier
Pulse Counting] S9 --> S10[Data System
Quantitative Analysis] style S1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S2 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S3 fill:#e57373,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S4 fill:#ef5350,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S5 fill:#f44336,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S6 fill:#e53935,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S7 fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S8 fill:#c62828,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S9 fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#333,color:#000 style S10 fill:#880e4f,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** ICP-MS detects elements (not molecules) by atomizing and ionizing them in an argon plasma. The interface (sampler + skimmer cones) reduces pressure from atmospheric (~760 Torr) to high vacuum (~10⁻⁶ Torr) in three stages. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q1.** Define the term "mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)" in mass spectrometry. Why is it preferred over absolute mass in MS detection? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** is defined as the dimensionless quantity obtained by dividing the ion mass (in atomic mass units, u or Da) by the number of elementary charges $z$ it carries. It is mathematically expressed as: > $$m/z = \frac{M_{\text{ion}}}{z \cdot e}$$ > where $M_{\text{ion}}$ is the ion mass in kg or Da and $e$ is the elementary charge. In mass spectrometry, ions of identical m/z follow identical trajectories in electric/magnetic fields, irrespective of their individual mass and charge combinations. Therefore, m/z is the experimentally measurable parameter (via flight time, cyclotron frequency, or magnetic deflection), making it the universal reporting metric. **(3 marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q2.** Differentiate between Electron Impact (EI) and Electrospray Ionization (ESI). Which one is preferred for the analysis of thermolabile mycotoxins in food? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > | Feature | Electron Impact (EI) | Electrospray Ionization (ESI) | > |---|---|---| > | **Energy** | Hard ionization (70 eV electrons) | Soft ionization (low energy) | > | **Analyte state** | Volatile, thermostable | Polar, thermolabile in solution | > | **Ion formed** | Radical cation M•⁺ + fragments | Protonated molecule [M+H]⁺ or [M−H]⁻ | > | **Coupled with** | GC | LC | > | **Fragmentation** | Extensive (libraries available) | Minimal (intact molecular ion) | > **Preferred for mycotoxins:** ESI is preferred because mycotoxins (e.g., aflatoxin B1) are polar, high-MW, and thermolabile — they decompose under EI conditions. ESI provides the intact [M+H]⁺ ion for sensitive MS/MS detection. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **Q A(a). [7 Marks]** Describe the principle and instrumentation of **GC-MS** for the determination of **pesticide residues in food**. List four classes of pesticides commonly analyzed. > > **Model Solution:** > > **Principle (3 marks):** GC-MS combines the **chromatographic separation power of capillary gas chromatography** with the **identification power of mass spectrometry**. A food extract is vaporized at the GC injection port, separated on a non-polar capillary column (e.g., 5% phenyl-methylpolysiloxane, 30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 µm), and eluting analytes enter the EI source (70 eV) where they are ionized. The resulting ions are mass-analyzed (typically single quadrupole or triple quadrupole) and detected. Identification is based on **(i) retention time match with authentic standard** and **(ii) mass spectral match against the NIST library** plus diagnostic ion ratios. > > **Instrumentation Block-wise (3 marks):** > 1. **GC system:** Injector (splitless mode, 250 °C), column oven (temperature-programmed 70 °C → 300 °C at 8 °C/min), carrier gas He at 1 mL/min. > 2. **Interface/Transfer line:** Heated (280 °C) to prevent analyte condensation. > 3. **Ion source:** EI at 70 eV; trap current 200 µA; source temp 230 °C. > 4. **Mass analyzer:** Single quadrupole for screening; QqQ for MRM quantification. > 5. **Detector:** Electron multiplier; dynode voltage ~1500 V. > 6. **Data system:** NIST library (~300,000 spectra) + quantitation software. > > **Four pesticide classes analyzed by GC-MS (1 mark):** > 1. Organochlorines (e.g., DDT, lindane, endosulfan) > 2. Organophosphates (e.g., chlorpyrifos, malathion) > 3. Pyrethroids (e.g., cypermethrin, deltamethrin) > 4. Carbamates (e.g., carbofuran, carbaryl) > *(Total 7 marks)* > **Q A(b). [7 Marks]** Explain the application of **ICP-MS in determining heavy metals (Pb, Cd, As, Hg) in food samples**. Discuss the sample preparation and regulatory significance. > > **Model Solution:** > > **Principle of ICP-MS (2 marks):** Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry atomizes and ionizes elements in a high-temperature (~10,000 K) argon plasma. The resulting elemental ions are extracted through a two-cone interface into a high-vacuum quadrupole mass analyzer, separated by m/z, and detected by an electron multiplier. ICP-MS offers **multi-element detection in a single run**, **isotope ratio capability**, and detection limits of **0.001–0.1 mg/kg (ppb to sub-ppb)**. > > **Sample preparation (2 marks):** Food samples (rice, milk, fish, vegetables) are first **digested** using: > - **Closed-vessel microwave digestion** with HNO₃/H₂O₂ at 180–200 °C, or > - **Dry ashing** in muffle furnace at 450–550 °C followed by acid dissolution. > > Digests are diluted with deionized water and analyzed directly. **Internal standards** (e.g., Sc, Ge, Rh, Ir, Bi) compensate for matrix effects and signal drift. > > **Regulatory significance (2 marks):** Toxic heavy metals are strictly regulated globally: > | Metal | FSSAI Limit (mg/kg) | Codex/EU Limit (mg/kg) | Health Concern | > |---|---|---|---| > | Lead (Pb) | 0.1–10 (matrix dependent) | 0.01–0.3 | Neurotoxicity | > | Cadmium (Cd) | 0.1–1.5 | 0.05–0.2 | Renal toxicity | > | Arsenic (As) | 0.1–1.0 | 0.1–0.5 | Carcinogen | > | Mercury (Hg) | 0.05–0.5 | 0.05–0.5 | Neurotoxicity | > > ICP-MS is the **gold-standard confirmatory technique** for enforcing these limits. It can also perform **speciation analysis** (e.g., As³⁺ vs As⁵⁺, methylmercury) when coupled to HPLC (HPLC-ICP-MS). *(Total 7 marks)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **Q B(a). [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **principle and working of LC-MS/MS using triple quadrupole for the analysis of mycotoxins in cereals**. > > **Model Solution:** > > **Principle (2 marks):** LC-MS/MS combines **HPLC separation** of mycotoxins (polar and heat-sensitive) on a reverse-phase C18 column with **two-stage mass filtering** in a triple quadrupole (Q1-Q2-Q3). The instrument operates in **Multiple Reaction Monitoring (MRM)** mode — the most sensitive and selective scan mode for trace quantification. > > **Block Diagram and Working (3 marks):** > > The Q1 selects the **precursor ion** [M+H]⁺ of the target mycotoxin (e.g., aflatoxin B1, m/z = 313). The selected ion enters Q2, the **collision cell**, where it is fragmented by collision-induced dissociation (CID) using argon gas at 20–40 eV. The fragments produced (e.g., m/z = 285, 241 for AFB1) are then mass-analyzed in Q3. The transition 313 → 285 is monitored, providing **two levels of mass selection** (precursor + product) for unparalleled selectivity in complex grain matrices. > > ``` > [LC Pump] → [C18 Column] → [ESI Source +] → [Q1: Select [M+H]+] → [Q2: CID Fragment] → [Q3: Select fragment] → [Detector] → [Data System] > ``` > > **Mycotoxins analyzed (2 marks):** > 1. Aflatoxins (B1, B2, G1, G2) > 2. Ochratoxin A > 3. Zearalenone > 4. Fumonisins (B1, B2) > 5. Deoxynivalenol (DON / vomitoxin) > 6. Patulin > > **Method performance:** LOD in MRM mode: **0.01–0.1 µg/kg (ppb)** in cereals, well below FSSAI MRL of 30 µg/kg for total aflatoxins in groundnuts/cereals. > *(Total 7 marks)* > **Q B(b). [7 Marks]** Explain the application of mass spectrometry in the **detection of food adulteration and authentication**, with specific examples. > > **Model Solution:** > > **Concept (2 marks):** Food authentication verifies the **declared origin, variety, and composition** of food products. Adulteration is the deliberate addition of inferior or harmful substances. MS-based methods detect adulteration by identifying **unique molecular signatures** (fingerprints) and quantifying **substituted or contaminated** components with high specificity. > > **Specific Examples (5 marks):** > > **1. Melamine adulteration in milk and infant formula:** > Melamine ($M = 126$ Da) is illegally added to milk to artificially inflate apparent protein content (due to its high N content, 66% N by mass). LC-MS/MS with ESI+ detects melamine directly (m/z 127 → 85) at levels as low as **0.05 mg/kg**, and its co-contaminant cyanuric acid (m/z 128 → 42) simultaneously. The 2008 Chinese milk scandal was investigated and resolved using this approach. > > **2. Olive oil adulteration with cheaper oils (sunflower, soybean, hazelnut):** > LC-Q-TOF MS profiles **triacylglycerol (TAG) molecular species** and **phenolic compounds** (e.g., oleocanthal, oleuropein). Authenticity markers: oleic acid–rich TAGs (OOO, OOL) and unique phenolic profiles. Adulteration shifts these ratios detectably. > > **3. Honey adulteration with sugar syrups:** > High-resolution MS (Orbitrap or Q-TOF) detects **oligosaccharide, polysaccharide, and phytochemical markers**. Genuine honey shows specific **pollen-derived metabolites**; high-fructose corn syrup adulteration introduces **marker disaccharides** absent in authentic honey. > > **4. Meat species substitution (horse meat in beef products):** > MALDI-TOF MS of **tryptic peptides** from meat proteins (myosin, actin) provides species-specific peptide mass fingerprints. PCR-ESI-MS (PLEX-ID system) enables rapid species identification in minutes. > > **5. Wine and spirits authentication:** > Headspace SPME-GC-MS identifies **volatile terroir markers** (terpenes, esters, alcohols) to confirm geographic origin (e.g., Bordeaux vs. Napa Cabernet Sauvignon). > > **6. Saffron adulteration:** > HPLC-ESI-MS/MS quantifies **crocin, picrocrocin, and safranal** — three marker compounds unique to genuine saffron. Adulterants (safflower, calendula) lack this trio. > *(Total 7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing ionization sources with mass analyzers:** "GC-MS uses ESI" is a frequent error. Remember: GC → EI/CI; LC → ESI/APCI. Source and analyzer are *separate* components. > 2. **Forgetting the analyte class context:** "MS detects pesticides" loses marks. Specify *which* class (organochlorine/organophosphate) and *which* ionization (EI for volatile). > 3. **Omitting regulatory numbers:** Always state the **LOD/LOQ** achieved and the **regulatory MRL** being met (FSSAI, Codex, EU). Examiners award marks for quantitative context. > 4. **Writing only the soft ionization names without explaining *why*:** "ESI is soft" — add *why* (low energy preserves molecular ion) and *consequence* (intact [M+H]⁺ enables molecular weight confirmation). > 5. **Skipping hyphenation details:** Always specify "LC-MS/MS" or "GC-MS" — never just "MS" in applications. Hyphenation matters for marks. > 6. **Confusing MALDI-TOF with ESI-TOF:** MALDI uses a *laser + matrix*; ESI uses a *high-voltage spray*. Misnaming is a common 2-mark deduction. > 7. **Missing the MRM concept for QqQ:** In triple quadrupole, the term **MRM (Multiple Reaction Monitoring)** MUST appear for full credit on mycotoxin/drug residue questions. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Mass Spectrometry** separates ions by **m/z**; the output is a **mass spectrum** (intensity vs m/z). - The MS workflow = **Sample introduction → Ionization → Mass analysis → Detection**. - **Soft ionization (ESI, APCI, MALDI):** gives [M+H]⁺ — useful for molecular weight and polar/thermolabile compounds. - **Hard ionization (EI at 70 eV):** gives M⁺• + extensive fragments — useful for library-based identification of volatiles. - **GC-MS** is for **volatile, thermostable** compounds (pesticides, flavors, PAHs). - **LC-MS / LC-MS/MS** is for **polar, thermolabile** compounds (mycotoxins, veterinary drugs, BPA). - **ICP-MS** is for **elemental analysis** (heavy metals). - **MALDI-TOF** is for **proteins, peptides, biopolymers** (allergens, meat speciation). - **Quadrupole (Q):** robust, quantitative, unit resolution. - **Triple Quadrupole (QqQ):** best for **MRM** quantification; two stages of mass selection. - **TOF / Q-TOF / Orbitrap:** **high resolution + accurate mass** for non-target screening and identification. - **Ion trap:** enables **MSⁿ** for structural elucidation. - **Mathieu equation** governs quadrupole stability. - **TOF equation:** $t = L\sqrt{m/(2zeV)}$ — heavier ions fly slower. - **Resolving power** $R = m/\Delta m$ — must be **≥ 10,000** for isobar/isomer separation. - **LOD = 3.3σ/S, LOQ = 10σ/S** — derived from ICH guidelines. - MRLs are enforced in **mg/kg (ppm)** or **µg/kg (ppb)** units; MS LOQ should be **< 1/3 of MRL**. - Common food applications: **pesticide residue, veterinary drug residue, mycotoxin, heavy metal, adulteration, authentication, flavor profiling, packaging migrants, allergen, marine biotoxin, acrylamide, dioxin**. - Regulatory bodies: **FSSAI (India), US FDA, EU, Codex Alimentarius, AOAC, SANTE/11312/2021**. - Confirmatory criteria for identity: **retention time match + 2 diagnostic ions + ion ratio match ± 30%**. - QuEChERS is the standard extraction for pesticide/mycotoxin residue analysis. - **Isotope dilution MS** uses isotopically labeled internal standards (e.g., $^{13}$C-melamine) for highest accuracy. - The **isotopic signature of Cl** (²:1 ratio at +2 Da) and **Br** (1:1 ratio at +2 Da) is a definitive MS diagnostic for halogenated compounds.
