- **Examiner's mantra:** Always state direction of signal change, tracer identity, separation method, and mathematical basis (mass action + Scatchard) in any descriptive RIA answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of PCR for Detection of Genetically Modified Organisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Use of PCR for Detection of Genetically Modified Organisms ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition **Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)** is an *in vitro* enzymatic amplification technique that exponentially replicates a specific target DNA sequence, generating millions to billions of copies from a minute starting template. In the context of food analysis, **PCR-based detection of Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs)** refers to the molecular identification and quantification of recombinant DNA sequences (transgenes) that have been artificially integrated into the genome of food crops such as soybean, maize, canola, and cotton. The technique relies on the hybridisation of **sequence-specific oligonucleotide primers** to flanking regions of the inserted genetic element, followed by enzymatic extension by a **thermostable DNA polymerase** (typically *Taq* polymerase isolated from *Thermus aquaticus*). The amplified amplicon is then visualised by agarose gel electrophoresis, fluorescent dye binding, or hybridisation probes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PCR is the *gold-standard* confirmatory method for GMO detection because it offers extraordinary sensitivity (detection of as few as **1–10 copies** of target DNA), specificity (single-nucleotide discrimination), and reproducibility, making it indispensable for regulatory compliance under FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius, and EU labelling thresholds (**0.9% GMO content**). ### Intuitive Analogy — "The Photocopier for DNA" Imagine you have a single **needle** hidden in a **thousand haystacks**, and you need to prove it exists. A normal search (chemical or protein-based test) might miss it. But PCR is like a **smart molecular photocopier**: you design a *photocopy request slip* (the primer) that uniquely fits only the needle's handle. Every cycle, the photocopier makes an exact copy of the needle using the original as a template. After 30–40 cycles, you have **over a billion identical needles** — easily detected by the naked eye on a UV transilluminator. The *needle* = inserted transgene (e.g., **Cry1Ab** in Bt cotton) The *request slip* = forward & reverse primers (18–25 nucleotides) The *photocopier* = *Taq* DNA polymerase The *power source* = thermal cycler providing precise temperatures > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Protein-based methods (ELISA) detect the *expressed product* (e.g., Bt toxin), while PCR detects the *genetic blueprint* itself. PCR therefore works on **processed foods** (oils, flours, syrups) where proteins are denatured but DNA fragments survive. ### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **PCR amplicon size (typical):** **80–300 base pairs (bp)** for GMO detection in processed foods - **Limit of Detection (LOD):** **≤ 10 copies of target DNA** (or **0.01% GMO** w/w) - **Minimum Information for Publication of Quantitative Real-Time PCR Experiments (MIQE)** compliance threshold - **Magnesium chloride (MgCl₂) optimum:** **1.5–2.5 mM** - **dNTP concentration:** **200 µM each** - **Primer length:** **18–25 nucleotides**, GC content **40–60%**, melting temp (Tₘ) **55–65 °C** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Principle of the Polymerase Chain Reaction The PCR amplification is governed by **three repetitive temperature-dependent steps**, executed in a programmable thermal cycler: 1. **Denaturation (94–95 °C, 20–60 s):** Double-stranded DNA template melts (hydrogen bonds break) into two single strands. High temperature disrupts the base-pairing without permanently damaging the target. 2. **Annealing (50–65 °C, 20–60 s):** Temperature is lowered to permit the two synthetic oligonucleotide primers to hybridise to their complementary sequences flanking the target region. The annealing temperature (Tₐ) is typically **3–5 °C below** the primer Tₘ. 3. **Extension / Elongation (72 °C, 30–60 s per kb):** At the optimal working temperature of *Taq* polymerase, deoxyribonucleotide triphosphates (dNTPs) are added to the 3′-OH end of each primer, synthesising the new strand in the 5′→3′ direction. > [!NOTE] > **The Exponential Math:** After **n** cycles, the number of amplicon copies is theoretically: $$ N = N_0 \cdot 2^n $$ where **N₀** = initial template copies, **n** = number of cycles. In practice, efficiency **E** is < 100%, giving: $$ N = N_0 \cdot (1 + E)^n $$ A reaction with **100% efficiency** (E = 1) starting from a single molecule yields approximately **1.07 × 10⁹ copies** after 30 cycles. ### 2.2 Hierarchical Strategy for GMO Detection KTU examiners frequently test the **four-tier detection pyramid**: | Tier | Target | Example | Specificity | |------|--------|---------|-------------| | **Screening** | Generic regulatory elements | **P-35S**, **T-NOS**, **NPT II** | Lowest (multi-target, false-positive risk) | | **Gene-specific** | Coding sequence of transgene | **Cry1Ab**, **Cry1Ac**, **epsps** | Medium | | **Construct-specific** | Junction between elements (e.g., promoter–gene) | **P-35S / Cry1Ab** junction | High | | **Event-specific** | Plant–insert genome junction (unique to each commercial event) | **MON810**, **Bt11**, **RR1** | Highest (regulatory reference) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Regulation Note:** The European Union enforces a **0.9%** labelling threshold; Japan and Korea use **5%**; India follows the FSSAI non-GMO labelling regime. Detection must be **reference-material validated** using Certified Reference Materials (CRMs) from IRMM (Institute for Reference Materials and Measurements). ### 2.3 Endogenous Reference Genes To express GMO content as a **percentage**, the copy number of the transgene must be normalised against an **endogenous, species-specific, single-copy reference gene**: | Crop | Reference Gene | Function | |------|----------------|----------| | **Soybean (*Glycine max*)** | **Lectin (Le1)** | Seed storage | | **Maize (*Zea mays*)** | **hmg (High-Mobility Group)** | Chromatin protein | | **Rice (*Oryza sativa*)** | **SPS (Sucrose Phosphate Synthase)** | Photosynthesis | | **Canola (*Brassica napus*)** | **CruA (Cruciferin)** | Seed storage | | **Cotton (*Gossypium hirsutum*)** | **Sad1** | Stearoyl-ACP desaturase | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Units / Constants | |---------|-------------|-------------------| | $N = N_0 \cdot (1 + E)^n$ | Amplified copy number after **n** cycles | Efficiency **E** = 0 to 1 | | $\Delta C_t = C_{t,\text{target}} - C_{t,\text{ref}}$ | Relative quantification (qPCR) | $C_t$ = threshold cycle | | $\% GMO = \dfrac{\text{copies}_{\text{transgene}}}{\text{copies}_{\text{endogenous}}} \times 100$ | Quantitative GMO content | Expressed in **% (w/w)** | | $C_q = - \dfrac{1}{\log(1+E)} \cdot \log(N_0) + b$ | Standard-curve equation for absolute quantification | Slope **b**, intercept | | $T_m = 64.9 + 41 \cdot \dfrac{(n_G + n_C - 16.4)}{(n_A + n_T + n_G + n_C)}$ | Wallace rule for primer Tₘ (oligos ≤ 25-mer) | **°C** | | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$ | Thermodynamics of primer–template duplex stability | kJ/mol | | $\dfrac{d[X]}{dt} = k \cdot [\text{polymerase}] \cdot [\text{dNTP}] \cdot [\text{template}]$ | Steady-state extension rate | Pseudo-first order in template | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** PCR technology underpins food safety traceability, halal/kosher authentication, allergen verification, fraud detection (substitution of premium crops with cheaper alternatives), and import-export certification laboratories accredited to **ISO/IEC 17025**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Amplification Starting from a single double-stranded DNA molecule, the amplification after **n** cycles is derived as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{After Cycle 1:} \quad & N_1 = N_0 \cdot 2 \\ \text{After Cycle 2:} \quad & N_2 = N_1 \cdot 2 = N_0 \cdot 2^2 \\ \text{After Cycle 3:} \quad & N_3 = N_2 \cdot 2 = N_0 \cdot 2^3 \\ \vdots \\ \text{After Cycle } n: \quad & N_n = N_0 \cdot 2^n \end{aligned} $$ Including the real-world efficiency factor **E** (where $0 \le E \le 1$): $$ \begin{aligned} N_n &= N_0 \cdot (1 + E)^n \\ \log_{10}(N_n) &= \log_{10}(N_0) + n \cdot \log_{10}(1 + E) \end{aligned} $$ For $N_0 = 1$ copy and $n = 30$ cycles: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{30} &= 1 \cdot (1 + 1)^{30} = 2^{30} \\ 2^{30} &= 2^{10} \cdot 2^{10} \cdot 2^{10} \\ 2^{10} &= 1024 \approx 10^3 \\ \therefore \; 2^{30} &\approx 10^9 = 1.073 \times 10^{9} \text{ copies} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation of % GMO Content from Real-Time PCR (qPCR) Real-time PCR monitors fluorescence intensity **R** at each cycle. The **threshold cycle (Cₜ)** is the cycle number at which fluorescence exceeds the background noise threshold. For two parallel reactions — one amplifying the **endogenous reference gene** (Le1, hmg, etc.) and one amplifying the **transgene** (P-35S, Cry1Ab, etc.) — the copy numbers are calculated from a standard calibration curve: $$ \begin{aligned} \log_{10}(\text{copies}) &= \text{slope} \cdot C_t + \text{intercept} \end{aligned} $$ The **GMO percentage by weight** is then: $$ \begin{aligned} \% GMO = \dfrac{\text{copies}_{\text{transgene}}}{\text{copies}_{\text{endogenous}}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):** > A soy flour sample is analysed in duplicate using **qPCR**. The mean $C_t$ values obtained are: > - **Le1 (endogenous)** : $C_t = 22.40$ > - **RR1 (Roundup Ready soy junction)** : $C_t = 26.85$ > > The corresponding copy numbers (from standard curves) are: > - $\text{Le1} = 85{,}000 \text{ copies}$ > - $\text{RR1} = 765 \text{ copies}$ > > **Calculate the % GMO content.** $$ \begin{aligned} \% GMO &= \dfrac{765}{85{,}000} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{76{,}500}{85{,}000} \\ &= 0.900 \, \% \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The sample contains exactly **0.90 % GMO**, which is at the EU labelling threshold — it would require mandatory "Contains GMO" labelling. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reaction Component Concentrations | Component | Stock Concentration | Final Concentration (50 µL rxn) | Function | |-----------|--------------------|--------------------------------|----------| | 10× PCR Buffer (with KCl) | 10× | **1×** | Maintain pH 8.3, ionic strength | | MgCl₂ | 25 mM | **1.5 – 2.5 mM** | Co-factor for *Taq* polymerase | | dNTP mix | 10 mM each | **200 µM each** | Building blocks | | Forward primer | 100 µM | **0.1 – 0.5 µM** | Defines 5′ end of amplicon | | Reverse primer | 100 µM | **0.1 – 0.5 µM** | Defines 3′ end of amplicon | | *Taq* DNA Polymerase | 5 U/µL | **1.0 – 2.5 U** | Catalyses extension | | Template DNA | Variable | **10 ng – 1 µg** | Contains target sequence | | Nuclease-free water | — | **Up to 50 µL** | Adjusts volume | > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Logic:** Mg²⁺ concentration is the **single most critical variable** controlling specificity. Excess Mg²⁺ promotes mis-priming and spurious products; deficiency reduces yield. Empirical **Mg²⁺ titration** is a routine optimisation step. ### 3.4 Typical Thermal Cycling Programme | Step | Temperature | Time | Cycles | |------|-------------|------|--------| | Initial denaturation | **95 °C** | 5 min | 1 | | Denaturation | 95 °C | 30 s | | | Annealing | **58 °C** | 30 s | **35 cycles** | | Extension | **72 °C** | 30 s | | | Final extension | 72 °C | 7 min | 1 | | Hold | 4 °C | ∞ | — | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 PCR Amplification Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD sampleA[Food Sample
Flour / Oil / Tissue] --> extrA[DNA Extraction
CTAB / Silica Column] extrA --> quantA[DNA Quantification
Spectrophotometer A260] quantA --> pcrA[PCR Reaction Setup
Master Mix Assembly] pcrA --> thermA[Thermal Cycler
Denaturation Step] thermA --> thermB[Thermal Cycler
Annealing Step] thermB --> thermC[Thermal Cycler
Extension Step by Taq Pol] thermC --> loopCheck{Cycle n
less than 35?} loopCheck -- Yes --> thermA loopCheck -- No --> detectA[Detection Stage
Gel Electrophoresis or qPCR] detectA --> judgeA{Amplicon Band
matches Expected bp?} judgeA -- Yes --> reportA[GMO Detected
Event-Specific Confirmation] judgeA -- No --> reportB[GMO Negative
Within LOD Threshold] ``` ### 4.2 Hierarchical GMO Detection Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD lvlA[Tier 1 Screening
P-35S and T-NOS
Most GM Events] lvlB[Tier 2 Gene Specific
Cry1Ab epsps pat bar
Transgene Coding Sequence] lvlC[Tier 3 Construct Specific
P-35S to Cry1Ab Junction
CaMV 35S to epsps] lvlD[Tier 4 Event Specific
MON810 Bt11 RR1 NK603
Plant Genome Junction Unique Site] lvlA --> lvlB lvlB --> lvlC lvlC --> lvlD ``` ### 4.3 Quantitative Real-Time PCR (qPCR) Signal Acquisition ```mermaid flowchart LR bindA[SYBR Green Dye
or TaqMan Probe] --> emitA[Fluorescence Emission
Reporter Signal] emitA --> cycA[Cycle 1 to 40
Real Time Monitoring] cycA --> threshA[Threshold Cycle Ct
Crossing Point] threshA --> refA[Reference Gene
Endogenous Single Copy] threshA --> trgA[Transgene
Target Insert] refA --> calcA[Delta Ct Calculation] trgA --> calcA calcA --> percA[Percent GMO Calculation
Final Quantitative Output] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three genetic elements commonly targeted in PCR-based screening of GMOs and state the biological source of each.* **Model Answer:** The three most common screening targets are: 1. **P-35S promoter** — derived from the **Cauliflower Mosaic Virus (CaMV)**; drives constitutive expression of the transgene in plants. 2. **T-NOS terminator** — derived from the **nopaline synthase gene** of *Agrobacterium tumefaciens*; signals transcription termination. 3. **NPT II (neomycin phosphotransferase II)** — a bacterial selectable marker that confers resistance to kanamycin; used in plant transformation. These elements are present in the majority (> 85 %) of commercial GM events, making them efficient screening targets. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Why is PCR preferred over ELISA for detecting GMOs in highly processed foods such as refined oils and corn syrup?* **Model Answer:** ELISA detects the **expressed protein** (e.g., Cry1Ab toxin), which is **heat-denatured, degraded, or removed** during refining (oil extraction removes protein; high-temperature hydrolysis destroys epitopes). PCR, in contrast, detects the **DNA sequence** which, although fragmented, remains amplifiable as long as a short target region (~ 80–200 bp) survives. Hence, PCR is the **only viable confirmatory method** for processed GMO-derived ingredients. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the principle of Polymerase Chain Reaction with a neat labelled diagram of the thermal cycling steps. Discuss the role of *Taq* DNA polymerase in the reaction. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** PCR is an *in vitro* enzymatic amplification technique invented by **Kary Mullis in 1983** (Nobel Prize, 1993). It amplifies a specific DNA target through **repetitive cycles** of three temperature-controlled steps. **(i) Denaturation (94–95 °C):** The double-stranded template DNA is heated above its melting temperature; hydrogen bonds between complementary bases break, yielding two single strands. This step typically lasts 20–60 s. **(ii) Annealing (50–65 °C):** The reaction mixture is cooled, allowing two synthetic oligonucleotide primers (18–25 nt) to anneal to their complementary sites flanking the target. The annealing temperature is optimised 3–5 °C below the primer Tₘ. **(iii) Extension (72 °C):** *Taq* polymerase, originally isolated from the thermophilic bacterium *Thermus aquaticus* (optimal growth at 70–80 °C), adds dNTPs to the 3′-OH of each primer in the 5′→3′ direction, synthesising the complementary strand. Extension time is ~ 1 min per kb. **Role of *Taq* polymerase:** - **Thermostability**: survives repeated 95 °C denaturation without irreversible denaturation. - **Processivity**: synthesises hundreds of bases without dissociation. - **No 3′→5′ proofreading** — high error rate (~ 1 × 10⁻⁴ per bp), acceptable for diagnostic PCR but problematic for cloning (substitute with *Pfu* polymerase for fidelity). The three steps constitute one **cycle**; 25–40 cycles produce **10⁶–10⁹-fold** amplification. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** Describe the four-tier hierarchical strategy of GMO detection by PCR. Why are endogenous reference genes used in quantitative GMO testing? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Four-tier detection hierarchy:** | Tier | Target Class | Example Targets | Specificity | |------|-------------|-----------------|-------------| | **Screening** | Generic regulatory elements | **P-35S**, **T-NOS**, **NPT II** | Lowest | | **Gene-specific** | Transgene coding region | **Cry1Ab**, **epsps (RR)**, **pat** | Moderate | | **Construct-specific** | Junction between elements | **P-35S/Cry1Ab junction** | High | | **Event-specific** | Plant-DNA/insert junction (unique site) | **MON810 5′ junction**, **Bt11 ivr** | Highest | - **Screening PCR** is rapid and cost-effective, flagging potential GMO presence using universal regulatory elements present in > 85 % of commercial GM constructs. A positive screen must be confirmed by tier-2 or higher. - **Gene-specific PCR** identifies which transgene is present (insect-resistance *cry*, herbicide-tolerance *epsps/pat/bar*). - **Construct-specific PCR** targets junctions unique to particular plasmid vectors, narrowing down the event. - **Event-specific PCR** is the **regulatory gold standard**; it targets the unique random insertion site in the plant genome, which differs for every approved GM event (Bt11, MON810, RR1, NK603, GA21, etc.). **Endogenous reference genes:** Quantitative GMO analysis must report GMO content as a **percentage by weight or by copy number**, not as a simple presence/absence. This requires **normalisation** to account for: - Variable DNA extraction efficiency from different matrices. - Variable copy number of the transgene per genome (often hemizygous). - PCR inhibitors in processed foods. An **endogenous reference gene** is a **species-specific, single-copy, stably expressed gene** present in both GMO and non-GMO varieties (e.g., **Le1** in soybean, **hmg** in maize). The transgene copy number is divided by the reference gene copy number, yielding a **ratio that approximates the GMO genome fraction**, which is then converted to weight percentage using conversion factors. This satisfies ISO 21569, ISO 21570, and EU regulatory requirements. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** With a suitable example, illustrate how quantitative Real-Time PCR (qPCR) is used to determine the percentage of GMO content in a food sample. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **qPCR** (also called Real-Time PCR) monitors amplification cycle-by-cycle using fluorescence, eliminating post-PCR gel analysis and providing **quantitative** data. **Two chemistries are dominant:** 1. **SYBR Green I** — intercalates into double-stranded DNA; cheap but non-specific (any dsDNA fluoresces). 2. **TaqMan probes** — oligonucleotide probes labelled with a 5′ fluorophore (FAM, VIC) and 3′ quencher (TAMRA); fluorescence is released only upon probe hydrolysis by *Taq* polymerase during extension. Highly specific. **Procedure (with worked example):** Consider a maize flour sample being tested for **MON810** (YieldGard Corn Borer). **Step 1:** Extract DNA from 100 mg sample using CTAB or commercial kit; quantify by NanoDrop (A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ ≈ 1.8). **Step 2:** Run two parallel qPCR reactions: - Reaction A: Primers for maize **hmg** (endogenous reference) - Reaction B: Primers + probe for MON810 event-specific junction **Step 3:** Construct standard curves using serial 10-fold dilutions of certified reference plasmid (e.g., 10⁶ → 10¹ copies) for both targets. **Step 4:** Obtain $C_t$ values for the unknown sample. **Step 5:** Interpolate from standard curves: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } C_{t,\text{hmg}} = 21.3 \text{ and corresponding copies} &= 1 \times 10^5 \\ \text{If } C_{t,\text{MON810}} = 28.6 \text{ and corresponding copies} &= 1 \times 10^2 \\ \\ \% GMO_{\text{copy}} &= \dfrac{1 \times 10^2}{1 \times 10^5} \times 100 = 0.1 \, \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Convert copy-ratio to **mass percentage** using a conversion factor (CF) established by IRMM CRM (e.g., CF for maize MON810 ≈ 1.0 for homozygous lines): $$ \% GMO_{\text{mass}} \approx \% GMO_{\text{copy}} \times CF $$ A value of **0.1 %** is **below the 0.9 % EU threshold**, so the sample may be labelled as non-GMO for the EU market (though it must be quantified for traceability). **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** Discuss the major sources of error and limitations in PCR-based GMO quantification. How are false positives and false negatives minimised in accredited GMO testing laboratories? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Sources of Error and Limitations:** | Source | Effect | Mitigation | |--------|--------|-----------| | **DNA extraction inefficiency** (especially oils, syrups) | Underestimation | Use matrix-specific kits, spike recovery tests | | **DNA degradation** (heat, hydrolysis) | False negative (LOD rises) | Use short amplicons (< 150 bp) | | **PCR inhibitors** (polyphenols, polysaccharides) | False negative | Use inhibitor-resistant polymerases, BSA addition | | **Cross-contamination** (carry-over) | False positive | **UNG/dUTP** system, physical separation of pre-/post-PCR areas | | **Heterogeneity of sample** | Sampling error | Composite sampling, ISO 21569 sampling protocol | | **Reference gene copy number variation** | Quantification bias | Use validated single-copy references | | **Z-score/Cₜ variability** | Reproducibility | Triplicate reactions, inter-lab ring trials | | **Haploid/hemizygous events** | Quantitative error | Use event-specific markers, account for zygosity | | **Unknown/unapproved GM events** | False negative | Use broad screening (P-35S, T-NOS) | **Quality Assurance Measures in Accredited Labs (ISO/IEC 17025):** 1. **Certified Reference Materials (CRMs)** — IRMM-ERM series for each event, used as positive controls. 2. **No-Template Control (NTC)** — to detect contamination. 3. **Negative (non-GMO) control** — to confirm specificity. 4. **Inhibition control** — spike the sample with known template to detect inhibitors. 5. **Duplex / multiplex qPCR** with internal positive control (IPC). 6. **UNG (Uracil-N-Glycosylase)** — destroys carry-over amplicons before each run. 7. **Ring-tested methods** — only validated by inter-laboratory studies (e.g., EU-RL GMFF methods). 8. **Proficiency Testing (PT)** — annual blind-sample schemes. 9. **Limit of Detection (LOD)** typically ≤ 10 copies; **Limit of Quantification (LOQ)** typically 0.1 % GMO. By adhering to these protocols, accredited laboratories minimise both false positives (contamination) and false negatives (inhibition/degradation), ensuring regulatory defensibility of results. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing tiers of detection**: Many students mix up "screening" vs "event-specific" targets. Always state the *specific* biological source (e.g., P-35S = CaMV promoter, **not** "plant promoter"). > 2. **Units slip-ups**: Reporting qPCR results without specifying whether % GMO is by **copy number or by mass** costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Missing matrix effect**: Forgetting that **oil/syrup matrices are low-DNA** and require modified extraction protocols (e.g., using larger sample mass, silica-based kits). > 4. **No mention of controls**: Examiners expect explicit reference to **NTC, NRT, positive, and inhibition controls** in any PCR discussion. > 5. **Wrong primer orientation**: *Taq* polymerase extends **5′ → 3′** only; primers must be written with **3′-OH at the inner end** facing the target. > 6. **Confusing PCR with sequencing**: PCR amplifies; it does **not** reveal sequence. Sanger/NGS is needed for unknown-event identification. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **PCR = Polymerase Chain Reaction** invented by **Kary Mullis (1983, Nobel 1993)**; the gold standard for GMO detection. - ✅ **Three steps per cycle**: **Denaturation (95 °C) → Annealing (50–65 °C) → Extension (72 °C)**; 25–40 cycles. - ✅ **Key enzyme**: *Taq* DNA polymerase from *Thermus aquaticus* — thermostable, lacks 3′→5′ proofreading. - ✅ **Amplification equation**: $N = N_0 \cdot (1+E)^n$ — yields ~ 10⁹ copies from 1 molecule in 30 cycles at 100% efficiency. - ✅ **Four detection tiers**: Screening (P-35S, T-NOS) → Gene-specific (cry, epsps) → Construct-specific → Event-specific (MON810, Bt11, RR1). - ✅ **Endogenous reference genes**: Le1 (soy), hmg (maize), SPS (rice), CruA (canola), Sad1 (cotton). - ✅ **qPCR chemistries**: SYBR Green (cheap, non-specific) vs TaqMan probe (specific, dual-labelled). - ✅ **Quantitative output**: $\% GMO = \dfrac{\text{copies}_{\text{transgene}}}{\text{copies}_{\text{endogenous}}} \times 100$. - ✅ **Regulatory thresholds**: **0.9 %** (EU), **5 %** (Japan/Korea), **1 %** (Codex for some commodities). - ✅ **Controls required**: NTC, no-RT control, positive CRM, inhibition/IC control. - ✅ **Limitations**: DNA degradation in processed foods, PCR inhibitors, contamination carry-over, hemizygous events, unknown GM events. - ✅ **Reference standards**: IRMM-ERM CRMs; validated methods from **EU-RL GMFF**. - ✅ **Amplicon size for processed foods**: **≤ 200 bp** (DNA is fragmented); smaller amplicons improve LOD. - ✅ **Carry-over prevention**: **UNG/dUTP system** in modern PCR kits. - ✅ **Real-time PCR formula**: $C_q = - \dfrac{1}{\log(1+E)} \cdot \log(N_0) + b$ for standard-curve quantification. - ✅ **Final step**: After extension, amplicons are visualised by **agarose gel electrophoresis with ethidium bromide / GelRed**, or detected fluorometrically in qPCR.

topics.title: Electrophoresis Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrophoresis Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrophoresis** is an electrokinetic separation technique in which charged analyte species (ions, proteins, peptides, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, or small organic molecules) migrate differentially through a conductive medium under the influence of an externally applied **direct current (DC) electric field**, becoming spatially separated into discrete zones based on their characteristic **electrophoretic mobility (μ)**, which is a function of the particle's net charge, hydrodynamic radius (Stokes radius), the applied field strength, and the viscosity and temperature of the supporting electrolyte.

In the context of **food analysis**, electrophoresis is the workhorse method for verifying food authenticity, detecting adulteration (e.g., milk whey in cheese, meat speciation, gluten in "gluten-free" products), determining molecular weight distributions of food proteins, and characterizing pigments, isoenzymes, and mycotoxins.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "River Sorting Stones" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you tip a mixed bag of differently shaped, differently weighted stones (some large round pebbles, some flat slates, some tiny grains of sand) into a slow-flowing river. Each stone experiences two competing forces: the **current** (the electric field pulling it downstream) and the **water resistance / drag** (friction opposing motion). Smooth, small, highly charged particles zip downstream quickly; bulky, weakly charged, or jagged ones lag behind. After some time, the stones have *self-sorted* into bands along the riverbed — large ones near the start, fine ones far downstream.
>
> In electrophoresis, the **river current** = the applied electric field **E**, the **stones** = the analyte ions/molecules, and the **riverbed friction** = the viscous drag of the supporting medium (gel buffer). Because every species has a unique ratio of charge-to-size, they migrate at distinct velocities and resolve into **discrete bands or peaks**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The fundamental constant governing ionic migration in an electric field is the **elementary charge** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C}$. Standard operating field strengths for slab gel electrophoresis are typically **5 to 15 V/cm**, while capillary electrophoresis routinely employs **100 to 500 V/cm** or higher. Standard reference buffer pH values include **pH 8.3 (Tris-Glycine)** for SDS-PAGE of proteins and **pH 8.0 (TAE/TBE)** for nucleic acid agarose gels.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

Electrophoresis techniques are classified based on the **support medium** and the **separation principle**:

| Classification Criterion | Sub-Categories |
|---|---|
| Support medium | Paper, Cellulose acetate, Agarose gel, Polyacrylamide gel (PAGE), Capillary |
| Separation mode | Zone, Moving-boundary, Isotachophoresis, Isoelectric focusing (IEF) |
| Dimensionality | 1-D, 2-D (2-DE) |
| Detergent treatment | Native (N-PAGE), Denaturing (SDS-PAGE) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Forces Governing a Migrating Ion

A charged particle in an electrolyte subjected to an electric field experiences three principal forces that, in steady state, balance each other:

**Step 1 — The Driving (Electrical) Force**
The Coulombic force pulling the ion toward the oppositely charged electrode:
$$F_{\text{elec}} = q \cdot E$$

where $q$ is the net charge on the ion (in Coulombs) and $E$ is the applied electric field strength (in V/m). In a slab gel of length $L$ with applied voltage $V$, $E = V/L$.

**Step 2 — The Retarding (Viscous Drag) Force**
According to **Stokes' Law**, for a spherical particle of radius $r$ moving with velocity $v$ through a medium of viscosity $\eta$:
$$F_{\text{drag}} = 6 \pi \eta r v$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Equilibrium**
At terminal velocity, the electrical force exactly equals the viscous drag:
$$q E = 6 \pi \eta r v_{\text{term}}$$

Solving for terminal velocity:
$$v_{\text{term}} = \frac{q E}{6 \pi \eta r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in food analysis:** Larger proteins (large $r$) migrate more slowly than small peptides under identical field strength. This size-dependent mobility is what enables molecular-weight estimation in SDS-PAGE — the very foundation of food-protein fingerprinting.

### 2.2 Electrophoretic Mobility — The Master Equation

Electrophoretic mobility $\mu$ is defined as the velocity acquired per unit electric field:
$$\mu = \frac{v_{\text{term}}}{E} = \frac{q}{6 \pi \eta r}$$

For non-spherical particles (most proteins), a **frictional coefficient** $f$ replaces the Stokes term:
$$\mu = \frac{q}{f}$$

The **net effective charge** $q$ is pH-dependent and is given by the protein's titration curve; at any pH, it equals the algebraic sum of all positive and negative side-chain charges. The **isoelectric point (pI)** is the pH at which $q = 0$, and therefore $\mu = 0$ — the particle ceases to migrate.

### 2.3 Electroosmotic Flow (EOF) — Critical for Capillary Electrophoresis

In a fused-silica capillary, ionized silanol groups ($\text{Si–O}^-$) on the inner wall attract a layer of hydrated cations from the buffer. When voltage is applied, these cations migrate toward the cathode, dragging bulk solvent with them. This bulk flow is called **electroosmotic flow (EOF)**.

$$\mu_{\text{eof}} = \frac{\epsilon \zeta}{\eta}$$

where $\epsilon$ is the dielectric constant of the buffer, $\zeta$ is the **zeta potential** of the capillary wall, and $\eta$ is buffer viscosity. The *apparent* mobility observed in CE is:
$$\mu_{\text{app}} = \mu_{\text{ep}} + \mu_{\text{eof}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation a KTU student must memorize for examination problems on electrophoresis. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of literal `|` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = v \mid E^{-1} = q \cdot (6 \pi \eta r)^{-1}$ | Electrophoretic mobility | $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{V}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $v = \mu \cdot E$ | Migration velocity | $\text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $E = V \cdot L^{-1}$ | Electric field strength | $\text{V} \cdot \text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $q = \sum_i n_i z_i e$ | Net charge of species | Coulombs (C) |
| 5 | $P = I^{2} R = V^{2} \cdot R^{-1}$ | Joule heating power | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $\mu_{\text{eof}} = \epsilon \zeta \cdot \eta^{-1}$ | EOF mobility (Smoluchowski) | $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{V}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $\mu_{\text{app}} = \mu_{\text{ep}} + \mu_{\text{eof}}$ | Apparent mobility in CE | $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{V}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $\text{R}_f = d_{\text{analyte}} \cdot d_{\text{dye}}^{-1}$ | Relative front in slab gel | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\log(\text{MW}) = a - b \cdot \text{R}_f$ | Ferguson / SDS-PAGE calibration | $a, b$ from standards |
| 10 | $\text{Resolution} \; R_s = \sqrt{N} \cdot \Delta v \cdot (4 \bar{v})^{-1}$ | Resolution (CE) | $N$ = theoretical plates |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Industry

- **Authenticity testing:** SDS-PAGE of milk caseins detects cow's milk in buffalo mozzarella or goat's cheese.
- **Allergen detection:** 2-DE reveals trace peanut or tree-nut proteins in processed foods.
- **Quality control of protein hydrolysates:** Capillary electrophoresis monitors peptide size distributions in infant formulas.
- **Mycotoxin screening:** MECC separates aflatoxins B1, B2, G1, G2 in under 10 minutes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Hückel's Equation for Electrophoretic Mobility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** Examiners frequently award full marks (7 of 7) on a sub-question that asks students to *derive* electrophoretic mobility from first principles. The derivation below is intentionally exhaustive — every algebraic step is shown so you can reproduce it verbatim on the answer script.

**Given:** A spherical ion of radius $r$ carrying charge $q$ moving in a medium of viscosity $\eta$ under a uniform field $E$.

**Step 1 — Identify all forces acting on the ion.**
The ion experiences the electrical force $F_{\text{elec}}$ in the direction of the field and a viscous drag $F_{\text{drag}}$ opposite to its motion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{elec}} &= q \cdot E \\
F_{\text{drag}} &= 6 \pi \eta r v
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law in the inertial regime.**
In an aqueous gel, the Reynolds number is exceedingly small (creeping flow), so the acceleration term vanishes almost instantaneously. Setting $m \, dv/dt = 0$ gives the **terminal velocity condition**:

$$
m \frac{dv}{dt} = q E - 6 \pi \eta r v = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for terminal velocity $v_{\text{term}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q E - 6 \pi \eta r v_{\text{term}} &= 0 \\
6 \pi \eta r v_{\text{term}} &= q E \\
v_{\text{term}} &= \frac{q E}{6 \pi \eta r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define electrophoretic mobility $\mu$ as $v_{\text{term}}/E$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{v_{\text{term}}}{E} \\
   &= \frac{q E}{6 \pi \eta r \cdot E} \\
   &= \frac{q}{6 \pi \eta r}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Hückel equation**, valid for small ions where the double-layer thickness $\kappa^{-1}$ is much greater than $r$ (i.e., $\kappa r \ll 1$).

**Step 5 — Extend to large particles (Henry's correction).**
For proteins where $\kappa r \approx 1$ or larger, Hückel underestimates $\mu$. Henry's correction introduces a dimensionless factor $f(\kappa r)$:

$$
\mu = \frac{q}{6 \pi \eta r} \cdot f(\kappa r)
$$

with $f(\kappa r) \to 1.0$ as $\kappa r \to 0$ (Hückel limit) and $f(\kappa r) \to 1.5$ as $\kappa r \to \infty$ (Smoluchowski limit).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Joule-Heating Power Dissipated in a Gel

**Given:** A gel of resistance $R$ with applied voltage $V$ and resulting current $I$.

**Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law.**

$$
I = \frac{V}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Power dissipated as heat (Joule's First Law).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= V \cdot I \\
  &= V \cdot \frac{V}{R} \\
  &= \frac{V^{2}}{R} \\
  &= I^{2} R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of field strength $E$ and gel length $L$.**

$$
P = \frac{(E L)^{2}}{R} = \frac{E^{2} L^{2}}{R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Food-lab consequence:** Excessive Joule heating produces thermal gradients inside the gel, distorting bands ("smiling" effect) and denaturing heat-sensitive proteins. Practical mitigation uses constant-voltage power supplies with wattage limits (~5–10 W for mini-gels).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Electrophoresis Variant for a Food Sample

| Analyte Goal in Food Sample | Recommended Technique | Separation Principle | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of milk proteins | SDS-PAGE (denaturing, 1-D) | Size-to-charge ratio uniformed by SDS | 1 kDa – 200 kDa |
| Isoenzyme fingerprinting of fruit cultivars | Native PAGE | Net charge + conformation | Medium |
| Detection of meat speciation (beef vs pork) | IEF (pH 3–10) | pI differences | High for pI variants |
| Comprehensive proteome of wheat flour | 2-DE (IEF × SDS-PAGE) | pI then MW | Very high (>1000 spots) |
| Rapid authenticity check in industry lab | Capillary electrophoresis (CZE) | Charge-to-size ratio in free solution | High, fast (<15 min) |
| Aflatoxin quantification in nuts | MECK / MEKC | Micellar partitioning + EOF | Very high for small organics |
| Carbohydrate profiling of honey | High-pH anion-exchange chromatography coupled to PAD | Often paired with electrophoretic principles | High |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Define electrophoretic mobility. State the factors on which it depends.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
Electrophoretic mobility $\mu$ is the velocity attained by a charged particle per unit electric field, i.e., $\mu = v/E$. It depends on **(i)** the net charge $q$ of the ion, **(ii)** the hydrodynamic radius $r$ of the particle, **(iii)** the viscosity $\eta$ of the medium, and **(iv)** the shape of the particle through the frictional coefficient $f$ (for non-spherical species). Mathematically, $\mu = q/f$. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four factors with brief justification.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Differentiate between Native PAGE and SDS-PAGE.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Native PAGE | SDS-PAGE |
|---|---|---|
| Denaturant added | None | SDS (and often β-mercaptoethanol) |
| Protein charge | Native (depends on pH vs pI) | Uniformly negative |
| Separation basis | Charge, shape, size | Size (molecular weight) only |
| Biological activity retained | Yes | No (denatured) |
| Application | Isoenzyme fingerprinting | MW determination |

*(2 marks for the comparison rows; 1 mark for any one relevant application distinguishing the two.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to define the frictional coefficient $f$** for non-spherical proteins. Always mention that for non-spheres $f > 6\pi\eta r$, hence $\mu$ is reduced.
> - When asked for SDS-PAGE mechanism, **state the three roles of SDS**: (1) unfold proteins, (2) impart uniform negative charge, (3) give all proteins a similar shape (rod-like).
> - For capillary electrophoresis questions, **never confuse** $\mu_{\text{ep}}$ (intrinsic analyte mobility) with $\mu_{\text{eof}}$ (bulk buffer mobility). The detector sees $\mu_{\text{app}} = \mu_{\text{ep}} + \mu_{\text{eof}}$.
> - Numerical answers must carry the correct **SI units**. Marks are deducted for missing units in $\mu$ and $E$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Electrophoretic Mobility Derivation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the Hückel equation for electrophoretic mobility of a spherical ion in an electrolyte. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Assumptions:* (i) The ion is a rigid sphere of radius $r$; (ii) The medium is continuous and homogeneous with viscosity $\eta$; (iii) The electric field $E$ is uniform; (iv) Steady-state (terminal) velocity is attained; (v) Low Reynolds number — inertial terms vanish.

*Derivation:* The ion experiences an electrical force $F_e = qE$ and a viscous drag given by Stokes' law $F_d = 6\pi\eta r v$.

At terminal velocity, $F_e = F_d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q E &= 6 \pi \eta r v \\
v &= \frac{q E}{6 \pi \eta r}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by $E$ to obtain mobility:

$$
\mu = \frac{v}{E} = \frac{q}{6 \pi \eta r}
$$

This is the **Hückel equation**. *(7 marks — 1 for assumptions, 2 for force equations, 2 for terminal velocity, 2 for final expression.)*

**(b)** Explain how SDS-PAGE separates proteins based on molecular weight. What is the role of the stacking gel and the resolving gel? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

*SDS action:* Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) is an anionic detergent that **(i)** denatures proteins by disrupting hydrophobic interactions, **(ii)** binds along the polypeptide chain at a constant ratio of ~1.4 g SDS per g protein, masking intrinsic charges with a uniform negative coat, and **(iii)** linearizes proteins into rod-like shapes of equal charge-to-mass ratio. Hence all SDS-coated proteins have the **same charge density**, and migration in the gel depends solely on molecular size (sieving effect of polyacrylamide matrix).

*Discontinuous (Laemmli) system:*
- **Stacking gel (large-pore, pH 6.8):** Concentrates (stacks) all proteins into a thin initial zone via the moving-boundary effect of leading (Cl⁻) and trailing (glycinate) ions, so they enter the resolving gel simultaneously → sharper bands.
- **Resolving / separating gel (small-pore, pH 8.8):** Glycinate ions become fully mobile and overtake proteins; proteins now migrate according to size through the tight pore network. Smaller proteins travel faster, larger proteins are retarded → separation by molecular weight.

*Plotting $\log(\text{MW})$ versus relative front $R_f$ yields a linear calibration from which unknown molecular weights are interpolated.*

*(7 marks — 2 for SDS roles, 2 for stacking gel purpose, 2 for resolving gel purpose, 1 for calibration plot.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Capillary Electrophoresis in Food Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of Capillary Zone Electrophoresis (CZE). What is electroosmotic flow (EOF) and why is it important? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

*CZE block diagram description:* A fused-silica capillary (typically 25–100 µm i.d., 30–100 cm long) connects two buffer reservoirs. A high-voltage DC power supply (10–30 kV) drives current through the capillary. The sample is introduced hydrodynamically or electrokinetically at the anode end. A UV or fluorescence detector is positioned near the cathode end. The output produces an **electropherogram** of detector signal versus time.

*Working principle:* When voltage is applied, the ionized silanol ($\text{Si–O}^-$) groups on the inner capillary wall attract a layer of hydrated cations. These cations are pulled toward the cathode, dragging the bulk buffer solution with them — this bulk flow is the **electroosmotic flow (EOF)**. EOF has a flat (plug-like) velocity profile, unlike the parabolic profile of pressure-driven flow, which minimizes band broadening.

*Importance of EOF:* EOF transports all species — cations, anions, and neutrals — in the same direction (toward the cathode) but at different net velocities determined by their own electrophoretic mobility:

$$
v_{\text{app}} = (\mu_{\text{ep}} + \mu_{\text{eof}}) \cdot E
$$

Cations arrive first (electrophoretic flow adds to EOF), neutrals next (pure EOF), anions last (electrophoretic flow opposes EOF). This enables simultaneous separation of a wide range of analytes in a single run.

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram description, 3 for EOF mechanism, 2 for significance.)*

**(b)** A food analytical laboratory needs to detect and quantify aflatoxins B1, B2, G1, and G2 in groundnut samples. Which electrophoretic / electrokinetic technique would you recommend and why? Describe the separation mechanism and justify the choice with respect to sensitivity, run time, and resolution. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Recommended technique:* **Micellar Electrokinetic Capillary Chromatography (MEKC or MECC)**.

*Why MEKC over standard CZE:* Aflatoxins are small, neutral hydrophobic molecules at typical operating pH. In plain CZE they would migrate with the EOF as a single unresolved peak because they have no electrophoretic mobility of their own. MEKC overcomes this limitation.

*Separation mechanism:* The run buffer is supplemented with an ionic surfactant (commonly **Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate, SDS**, above its critical micelle concentration ~8 mM). SDS micelles form a pseudo-stationary phase that migrates slowly toward the anode (opposite to EOF). Neutral aflatoxins partition dynamically between the aqueous buffer (fast-moving with EOF) and the hydrophobic core of the micelles (slow-moving). The differential partitioning produces distinct migration times:

- **Aflatoxin B1, B2, G1, G2** differ slightly in polarity and structure (B-series has a cyclopentanone ring; G-series has a lactone), giving different distribution coefficients $K$ between buffer and micelle.
- Migration time scales with $K$: more hydrophobic aflatoxins spend more time in micelles → longer migration times.

*Justification:*
- **Sensitivity:** Laser-induced fluorescence (after derivatization) or UV detection at 365 nm gives LOD in the low ppb range, suitable for regulatory limits (≤20 µg/kg total for groundnuts under FSSAI / EU).
- **Run time:** Typical MEKC separation of all four aflatoxins in <15 minutes, far faster than HPLC.
- **Resolution:** MEKC theoretical plate counts exceed 100,000/m, easily resolving B1, B2, G1, G2 baseline.
- **Sample economy:** Requires only nanolitres of buffer and microlitres of sample extract.

*(7 marks — 1 for technique choice, 2 for mechanism, 1 each for sensitivity, run time, resolution, sample economy.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Electrophoresis Techniques**

- **Master equation:** $\mu = q/(6\pi\eta r)$ — Hückel — for small spherical ions in low ionic strength media.
- **Steady-state condition:** Electrical force equals viscous drag; no inertia at low Reynolds number.
- **Isoelectric point (pI):** pH at which net charge $q = 0 \Rightarrow \mu = 0$; particle does not migrate in IEF.
- **SDS-PAGE three pillars of SDS action:** denature, uniform negative charge, rod-like shape.
- **Stacking gel:** large pores, pH 6.8 → concentrates proteins into a thin zone (isotachophoretic stacking).
- **Resolving gel:** small pores, pH 8.8 → molecular sieving separates by size.
- **$R_f$ and Ferguson plot:** $\log(\text{MW}) = a - b R_f$ for molecular weight calibration.
- **EOF in CE:** flat plug profile, driven by $\mu_{\text{eof}} = \epsilon\zeta/\eta$ (Smoluchowski).
- **Apparent mobility in CE:** $\mu_{\text{app}} = \mu_{\text{ep}} + \mu_{\text{eof}}$.
- **Cation order in CE:** small cations first → neutrals → anions last (with normal EOF direction).
- **MEKC for neutral analytes:** add SDS micelles as pseudo-stationary phase to create partition-based selectivity.
- **2-DE workflow:** first dimension IEF (separates by pI), second dimension SDS-PAGE (separates by MW).
- **Joule heating power:** $P = V^2 / R$; excessive heating → band distortion → limit wattage.
- **Food applications to memorize:** milk protein authenticity (SDS-PAGE), meat speciation (IEF), aflatoxin screening (MEKC), gluten detection (SDS-PAGE / 2-DE).
- **Always state units** for $\mu$ ($\text{m}^2 \, \text{V}^{-1} \, \text{s}^{-1}$) and $E$ ($\text{V/m}$ or $\text{V/cm}$); examiners deduct marks for omissions.
