PACKAGING"] --> tech8["Ag-NP OR
CHITOSAN FILM"] tech1 --> food1["PACKAGED FOOD
FRUITS MEAT"] tech2 --> food1 tech3 --> food1 tech4 --> food1 tech5 --> food1 tech6 --> food1 tech7 --> food1 tech8 --> food1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern (As per University Order):** > - **Part A** — 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (short answer) > - **Part B** — Internal choice; student answers ONE of two 14-mark questions > - **Module-wise weightage** — Question 1 from Modules 1–2, Question 2 from Modules 3–4, Question 3 from Modules 5 (with full internal choice) --- ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style] **"Define biosensor. List its four essential components with one example for each."** (3 Marks) ### Model Answer A **biosensor** is an analytical device that integrates a biological recognition element (bioreceptor) with a physicochemical transducer to detect and quantify a specific analyte. **Four essential components:** 1. **Bioreceptor** — *e.g., Glucose oxidase (GOx) enzyme* — selectively binds glucose. 2. **Transducer** — *e.g., Pt electrode* — converts H₂O₂ produced into current. 3. **Electronics** — *e.g., Potentiostat* — amplifies and conditions the signal. 4. **Display** — *e.g., LCD screen* — shows concentration in mM. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **"Differentiate between top-down and bottom-up approaches of nanomaterial synthesis. Give one example of each."** (3 Marks) ### Model Answer | Feature | Top-Down | Bottom-Up | |---|---|---| | **Direction** | Bulk → Nano | Atomic/Molecular → Nano | | **Mechanism** | Physical size reduction | Chemical self-assembly | | **Energy** | High (mechanical) | Moderate (chemical/thermal) | | **Example** | Ball milling of silver foil to Ag-NP | Chemical reduction of AgNO₃ to Ag-NP using citrate | | **Defect Density** | Higher (surface defects) | Lower (uniform) | --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice) --- ### **Question Choice A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern] **(a) Describe the principle, components, and working of an electrochemical biosensor with a suitable food-industry example. Add a labeled schematic.** (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **Definition (1 mark):** An electrochemical biosensor is an analytical device that uses an electrochemical transducer (electrode) coupled with a biological recognition element to produce a current, voltage, or impedance signal proportional to the analyte concentration. **Components (2 marks):** 1. **Reference electrode** — Ag/AgCl (provides stable potential). 2. **Working electrode** — Pt, Au, or Glassy Carbon (GCE), often modified with nanomaterials (graphene, Au-NP) to enhance electron transfer. 3. **Counter electrode** — Pt wire (completes the circuit). 4. **Bioreceptor** — Enzyme (GOx, AChE, Tyrosinase) or antibody, immobilized via glutaraldehyde/NHS-EDC chemistry. 5. **Potentiostat** — Three-electrode system controller. 6. **Display unit** — Digital readout. **Working Principle (3 marks):** Take the **organophosphate pesticide biosensor** as the food example. - **Bioreceptor:** Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) enzyme immobilized on a nanostructured Au-NP/chitosan-modified GCE. - **Reaction in absence of pesticide:** AChE hydrolyzes acetylthiocholine → thiocholine + acetic acid. Thiocholine is oxidized at the electrode, giving a measurable anodic current (I₀). - **Reaction in presence of pesticide (e.g., chlorpyrifos):** The pesticide *inhibits* AChE activity. Less thiocholine is produced → current *decreases* (I). - **% Inhibition = ((I₀ − I)/I₀) × 100.** This is inversely proportional to pesticide concentration. **Schematic (1 mark):** [Use the diagram from Section 4.3 — Electrochemical Biosensor schematic.] **(b) Derive the Michaelis-Menten equation and explain how the Lineweaver-Burk plot is used to determine $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ for an enzyme biosensor.** (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **Derivation (4 marks):** Follow the 6-step derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes. **Lineweaver-Burk Application (3 marks):** - Take the reciprocal: $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ - Plot $\dfrac{1}{v}$ (y-axis) vs. $\dfrac{1}{[S]}$ (x-axis) — gives a straight line. - **y-intercept** = $\dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ → compute $V_{max}$. - **x-intercept** = $-\dfrac{1}{K_m}$ → compute $K_m$ (magnitude). - **Physical meaning:** Low $K_m$ = high enzyme–substrate affinity = sensitive biosensor. - For pesticide detection, $K_m$ *increases* in presence of inhibitor (competitive inhibition) — confirming pesticide presence. --- ### **Question Choice B — 14 Marks** [Alternative Internal Choice] **(a) Explain the architecture and working of a Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) biosensor. How is it used for allergen detection in food?** (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **Architecture (2 marks):** - A thin (50 nm) gold film is evaporated onto a glass prism (Kretschmann configuration). - An anti-allergen antibody (e.g., anti-peanut Ara h 1) is immobilized on the gold surface. - Polarized light is directed through the prism at varying angles. - At the **resonance angle (θ_SPR)**, photons excite surface plasmons in the gold film, producing a sharp dip in reflected intensity. **Working Principle (3 marks):** - When the analyte (e.g., peanut allergen Ara h 1) binds the antibody, the **refractive index** near the gold surface increases. - This shifts the SPR angle by $\Delta\theta$ (typically measured in millidegrees). - The shift is **time-resolved** and **label-free** — provides binding kinetics ($k_a$, $k_d$) and affinity ($K_D$). **Food Application — Allergen Detection (2 marks):** - Antibodies for gluten (anti-gliadin), peanut (anti-Ara h 1), milk casein, soy, and crustacean tropomyosin are commercially available. - Limit of Detection: ~1 ng/mL (ppb level) — meets regulatory thresholds. - Advantages over ELISA: real-time, reusable, no fluorescent label. **(b) Discuss in detail the applications of nanotechnology in food industry, covering nano-encapsulation, nano-emulsions, nano-sensors, and antimicrobial nano-packaging. Provide at least one example for each.** (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **1. Nano-Encapsulation (1.5 marks):** - Definition: Trapping bioactive compounds (vitamins, flavors, probiotics) inside nanometer-sized carriers (liposomes, nanocapsules, cyclodextrins). - Example: **β-carotene in PLGA nanoparticles** for fortified fruit juices — protects from light/oxidation, improves bioavailability by 3×. **2. Nano-Emulsions (1.5 marks):** - Definition: Kinetically stable colloidal dispersions of two immiscible liquids with droplet size 20–200 nm. - Example: **Curcumin nano-emulsion** in functional beverages — increases water-solubility 1500-fold, enhances absorption. **3. Nano-Sensors (2 marks):** - Definition: Sensors employing nanomaterials (Au-NP, graphene, CNTs) for enhanced sensitivity. - Example: **Gold-nanoparticle based colorimetric biosensor** for melamine detection in milk — LOD = 0.1 ppb (visual pink-to-blue color change). **4. Antimicrobial Nano-Packaging (2 marks):** - Definition: Food packaging films embedded with antimicrobial nanoparticles. - Example: **Silver-nanoparticle chitosan film** for fresh meat packaging — reduces *E. coli* and *Salmonella* counts by 99 % over 7 days; extends shelf life. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Top Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on Biosensor Questions:** > 1. **Confusing transducer with bioreceptor** — the *transducer* is the physical converter, NOT the biological part. Writing "antibody is the transducer" guarantees -2 marks. > 2. **Omitting units in formulas** — the LOD formula $\dfrac{3\sigma}{m}$ MUST show units: $\sigma$ in µA, $m$ in µA·ppb⁻¹, result in ppb. > 3. **Mixing "nanotechnology" with "biosensor"** — they are related but distinct. Nanotechnology is the *tool*, biosensor is the *application*. Examiners expect you to define both separately. > 4. **Forgetting the Nernst factor** — at 25 °C, the slope is **0.05916/n V**, not "RT/nF" (always substitute numerical value). > 5. **In Lineweaver-Burk, writing y-intercept = $K_m$ instead of $1/K_m$** — this is a classic trap. y-intercept = $1/V_{max}$, x-intercept = $-1/K_m$. > 6. **No schematic in 7-mark questions** — KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a labeled diagram for full marks. Always draw. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **30-Second KTU Board Recall — Memorize Before Exam:** - ☐ **Biosensor** = Bioreceptor + Transducer + Electronics + Display. - ☐ **LOD** = $\dfrac{3\sigma}{m}$ · **LOQ** = $\dfrac{10\sigma}{m}$ · **SNR** = $\dfrac{I_{signal}}{I_{noise}}$. - ☐ **Michaelis-Menten:** $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$; invert to get **Lineweaver-Burk** linear plot. - ☐ **Nernst** at 25 °C: $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.05916}{n}\log\dfrac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$. - ☐ **Sauerbrey:** $\Delta f = -\dfrac{2f_0^{2}}{A\sqrt{\rho_q\mu_q}}\cdot\Delta m$ — negative shift = mass added. - ☐ **Beer-Lambert:** $A = \varepsilon c l$ — for colorimetric/optical sensors. - ☐ **Nanoscale** = 1–100 nm. **Top-down** = milling; **Bottom-up** = chemical reduction. - ☐ **AChE inhibition biosensor** = pesticide detection. **GOx biosensor** = glucose detection. **Anti-Ara h 1 SPR** = peanut allergen. - ☐ **Nano-Ag** = antimicrobial packaging. **Nano-emulsion** = curcumin delivery. **PLGA nanocapsule** = β-carotene fortification. - ☐ **Always draw a labeled block diagram** in 7-mark answers — KTU examiners reward visual clarity. - ☐ **Cite 2 specific food examples** per question — generic answers score ≤ 50 %. - ☐ **Mention units** in every formula — silent units = -1 mark per formula.

topics.title: Instrumental Measurement of Texture of Food
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instrumental Measurement of Texture of Food

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Texture (KTU 2024 Definition):** Texture is a sensory attribute resulting from the *structural and mechanical* (geometrical, surface, and deformational) characteristics of a food product, perceived through kinesthetic, tactile, and visual feedback during handling and consumption, and quantified instrumentally as the **resistance of a food material to an applied mechanical force**.

In instrumental analysis, **texture is treated as a multi-parameter mechanical property**, expressed through a force–distance (or force–time) response curve generated when a precisely defined probe deforms a standardized food specimen under controlled kinematic conditions (speed, distance, geometry).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensory Texture vs. Instrumental Texture:** Sensory evaluation depends on the human panel; instrumental measurement depends on physics (force, displacement, energy). The two correlate but are not identical — instrumental methods provide *objective, reproducible, quantitative* data for QA/QC, R&D, and shelf-life studies.

## 1.2 Key Texture Attributes (Sensory Correlates)

| Sensory Attribute | Instrumental Correlate | Common Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Hardness | Peak force on first compression | N (Newton) |
| Cohesiveness | Ratio of work of 2nd compression to 1st | Dimensionless |
| Springiness | Recovery distance after compression | mm |
| Adhesiveness | Negative work on probe withdrawal | N·s |
| Gumminess | Hardness × Cohesiveness | N |
| Chewiness | Gumminess × Springiness | N·mm |
| Fracturability | Force at first significant break | N |
| Resilience | Ratio of up-stroke to down-stroke energies | Dimensionless |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Bite Test":**
> Imagine biting into three foods — a raw carrot, a marshmallow, and a piece of cheese.
> - The **carrot** resists cracking → it measures **high hardness** and **fracturability**.
> - The **marshmallow** compresses easily, bounces back slowly, and sticks to teeth → it shows **high springiness** and **adhesiveness**.
> - The **cheese** shears smoothly, is cohesive, and melts on the tongue → it scores high on **cohesiveness** and **gumminess**.
> A **texture analyzer** is essentially a *robotized mechanical "tongue and teeth"* that quantifies exactly these resistance responses using calibrated probes instead of human bite.

## 1.4 Why Instrumental Measurement of Texture?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Objective & Reproducible:** Removes human panel bias.
> - **Rapid & High-throughput:** Suitable for industrial QA/QC.
> - **Quantifiable:** Generates numerical data (force, distance, energy).
> - **Correlatable with Sensory Data:** Statistical mapping (e.g., Pearson's r) bridges panel and machine scores.
> - **Standardized:** Compatible with **ASTM, ISO, and AACC International** standard test methods.

## 1.5 Classification of Instrumental Texture Tests

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Categories (KTU Module 4 Emphasis):**
> 1. **Imitative Tests (TPA):** Simulate biting/chewing → *most common*.
> 2. **Fundamental Tests:** Measure single mechanical property (compression, tension, shear, bending).
> 3. **Empirical Tests:** Use specialized fixtures (Kramer Shear, Warner-Bratzler, Ottawa Cell).
> 4. **Acoustic & Vibration Methods:** Coupled with mechanical tests.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Instrumental Texture Test Principles

Every instrumental texture test is governed by **three control variables** and measures **one or more response variables**:

| Control Variable | Typical Range | Response Measured |
|---|---|---|
| **Force (F)** | 0.1 N – 5000 N | Deformation / strain |
| **Distance / Strain (D)** | 0.1 mm – 100 mm | Reaction force |
| **Velocity / Strain Rate (v)** | 0.1 mm/s – 100 mm/s | Time-dependent modulus |
| **Time (t)** | 0.1 s – 600 s | Stress relaxation, creep |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — The Three Test Geometries:**
> 1. **Uniaxial Compression** → Bulk solid foods (cheese, gels, fruits).
> 2. **Shear / Cutting** → Fibrous foods (meat, vegetables).
> 3. **Penetration / Puncture** → Soft to semi-solid foods (yogurt, gels, fruit skins).

## 2.2 Fundamental Mechanical Parameters

### 2.2.1 Stress (σ)
$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}
$$

Where:
- $F$ = applied force (N)
- $A_0$ = original cross-sectional area (m²)
- $\sigma$ = engineering stress, **units: Pascal (Pa) = N·m⁻²**

### 2.2.2 Strain (ε)
$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}
$$

Where:
- $L_0$ = original sample length
- $L$ = instantaneous length after deformation

### 2.2.3 Young's Modulus (E) — for elastic foods
$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}
$$

**Units: Pascal (Pa)**, often reported as **kPa or MPa** for food systems.

### 2.2.4 Shear Stress (τ) & Shear Modulus (G)
$$
\tau = \frac{F}{A}, \qquad G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}
$$

Where $\gamma$ is shear strain (radians).

### 2.2.5 True Stress vs. Engineering Stress
$$
\sigma_{true} = \sigma \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)
$$

### 2.2.6 Power-Law Fluid Model (for viscous foods like sauces)
$$
\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}
$$

Where:
- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)
- $n$ = flow behavior index (dimensionless)

## 2.3 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) — The Core KTU Topic

> [!NOTE]
> **TPA** (developed by **Friedman, Whitney & Szczesniak, 1963**; refined by **Bourne, 1978**) is the **most examination-relevant instrumental texture method** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 2.3.1 TPA Operational Sequence

1. **Trigger load** → detects contact (e.g., 0.05 N).
2. **First Compression (Bite 1):** Probe descends to a preset distance (typically 50–75% of original sample height) at constant speed.
3. **Hold / Withdrawal:** Probe retracts.
4. **Wait Period:** Typically 5 s (allows elastic recovery and adhesive force measurement).
5. **Second Compression (Bite 2):** Probe descends again to same distance.
6. **Final Withdrawal.**

### 2.3.2 The Eight TPA Parameters — Master Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — TPA Parameter Definitions and Mathematical Expressions**

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Mathematical Expression | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | H | Peak force during first compression | $H = F_{peak,1}$ | N |
| **Fracturability** | Fr | Force at first significant peak/brittle break | $Fr = F_{first,peak}$ | N |
| **Cohesiveness** | Co | How well sample withstands 2nd deformation relative to 1st | $Co = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | dimensionless |
| **Springiness** | Sp | Elastic recovery between end of 1st bite and start of 2nd bite | $Sp = \dfrac{t_2}{t_1}$ (time) or $\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ (distance) | mm or s |
| **Adhesiveness** | Ad | Negative work area during probe withdrawal after 1st bite | $Ad = A_3$ (negative region) | N·s |
| **Gumminess** | Gu | Energy required to disintegrate a semi-solid food | $Gu = H \cdot Co$ | N |
| **Chewiness** | Ch | Energy to chew a solid food to swallowable state | $Ch = Gu \cdot Sp = H \cdot Co \cdot Sp$ | N·mm |
| **Resilience** | Re | How well sample recovers instantly from 1st compression | $Re = \dfrac{A_4}{A_3}$ (up-stroke/down-stroke areas) | dimensionless |

### 2.3.3 Graphical Interpretation of TPA Curve

> [!NOTE]
> The TPA force–time curve is divided into **integrated regions (areas)** and **characteristic points**:
> - **Area $A_1$** = Work of 1st compression (down-stroke)
> - **Area $A_2$** = Work of 2nd compression (down-stroke)
> - **Area $A_3$** = Negative work during 1st withdrawal (Adhesiveness)
> - **Area $A_4$** = Up-stroke of 1st compression
> - **Area $A_5$** = Down-stroke of 1st compression (used for Resilience)
> - **$t_1$** = Time at peak of 1st compression
> - **$t_2$** = Time at start of 2nd compression

## 2.4 Force–Deformation Curves — Three Behavioral Classes

| Food Type | Curve Shape | Behavior | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Elastic solid** | Linear, reversible | Hookean | Fresh apple, crisp vegetables |
| **Viscoelastic solid** | Curved, partial recovery | Time-dependent | Cheese, bread |
| **Viscous liquid** | No recovery, continuous flow | Newtonian / non-Newtonian | Honey, sauces |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Quality Control:** Detect batch-to-batch variation in cheese firmness.
> - **Shelf-life Studies:** Monitor bread staling (increased hardness).
> - **Process Optimization:** Tune extrusion parameters for snack crispness.
> - **Sensory Mapping:** Correlate instrumental TPA data with trained panel scores via Principal Component Analysis (PCA).
> - **Texture Modification:** Engineer reduced-fat foods by tuning Gumminess/Chewiness via hydrocolloids.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Young's Modulus from Compression Test Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A cylindrical gel sample of diameter $d = 20\,\text{mm}$, original height $L_0 = 25\,\text{mm}$, subjected to uniaxial compression by a flat probe on a texture analyzer.
> Recorded data: peak force $F = 18\,\text{N}$ at deformation $\Delta L = 2.5\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the original cross-sectional area:**
$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (20 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = \pi \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the engineering stress at peak:**
$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{18}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma \approx 57{,}295.8\,\text{Pa} \approx 57.30\,\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the engineering strain at peak:**
$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{2.5}{25} = 0.10 \;\; (\text{or } 10\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law to determine Young's Modulus (assuming linear elastic regime):**
$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{57{,}295.8}{0.10} = 572{,}958\,\text{Pa} \approx 573\,\text{kPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $E \approx 573\,\text{kPa}$. For soft gels (e.g., agar, gelatin), typical $E$ ranges from **1 kPa to 1000 kPa**; for crackers/cookies, $E$ may exceed **100 MPa**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Computing All Eight TPA Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Force–Time TPA Data (Stylized — typical food values):**
> - Peak force 1st compression $F_{peak,1} = 24\,\text{N}$
> - Force at fracture (first peak before yield) $F_{fr} = 20\,\text{N}$
> - Area under 1st compression curve $A_1 = 120\,\text{N·s}$
> - Area under 2nd compression curve $A_2 = 84\,\text{N·s}$
> - Negative area during withdrawal (adhesion) $A_3 = -6\,\text{N·s}$
> - Time at peak 1st compression $t_1 = 4.0\,\text{s}$
> - Time at start of 2nd compression $t_2 = 3.0\,\text{s}$
> - Up-stroke area of 1st compression $A_4 = 28\,\text{N·s}$
> - Down-stroke area of 1st compression $A_5 = 40\,\text{N·s}$

**Step 1 — Hardness (H):**
$$
H = F_{peak,1} = 24\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Fracturability (Fr):**
$$
Fr = F_{first,peak} = 20\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Cohesiveness (Co):**
$$
Co = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{84}{120} = 0.70
$$

**Step 4 — Springiness (Sp):**
$$
Sp = \frac{t_2}{t_1} = \frac{3.0}{4.0} = 0.75
$$

**Step 5 — Adhesiveness (Ad):**
$$
Ad = \vert A_3 \vert = 6\,\text{N·s}
$$

**Step 6 — Gumminess (Gu):**
$$
Gu = H \times Co = 24 \times 0.70 = 16.8\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 7 — Chewiness (Ch):**
$$
Ch = Gu \times Sp = 16.8 \times 0.75 = 12.6\,\text{N·mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Resilience (Re):**
$$
Re = \frac{A_4}{A_5} = \frac{28}{40} = 0.70
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretive Note:** The sample is *moderately hard (24 N)*, *somewhat cohesive (0.70)*, *moderately springy (0.75)*, *slightly adhesive (6 N·s)*, and *medium chewiness (12.6 N·mm)* — characteristic of **semi-hard cheese or processed meat**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: True Stress Correction for Highly Compressed Foods

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A food specimen undergoes 50% strain ($\varepsilon = 0.50$). Compare engineering vs. true stress.

**Engineering Stress:**
$$
\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0}
$$

**True Stress** (assuming constant volume — incompressible food):
$$
\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon) = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + 0.50) = 1.5 \cdot \sigma_{eng}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For highly compressible foods (bread, sponge cake), true stress may exceed engineering stress by **up to 100%** at high strains. KTU questions commonly test this distinction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Texture Test

| Food Type | Recommended Test | Probe/Fixture | Typical Parameter Reported |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bread, cake** | TPA / Compression | Flat cylindrical, 25–75 mm dia | Hardness, Cohesiveness, Chewiness |
| **Meat (whole muscle)** | Warner-Bratzler shear | V-notch blade | Shear force (N) |
| **Minced/ground meat** | Kramer Shear | Multi-blade cell | Shear force (N/g) |
| **Fruits (whole)** | Magness-Taylor / Penetration | Cylindrical probe | Firmness (N) |
| **Cheese** | TPA, Uniaxial Compression | Flat probe | Hardness, Cohesiveness |
| **Yogurt, pudding** | Back extrusion / Penetration | Disc probe | Firmness, Consistency |
| **Cereals, snacks** | Three-point bend | Support + knife | Break force, Fracturability |
| **Sauces, dressings** | Viscometry / Penetration | Cone / Disc | Viscosity, Yield stress |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Warner-Bratzler Shear Test on Meat

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A cooked beef muscle sample of cross-section $A = 100\,\text{mm}^2$ is sheared using a Warner-Bratzler blade. Maximum recorded force $F_{max} = 45\,\text{N}$.

**Shear Stress at Failure:**
$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{F_{max}}{A} = \frac{45\,\text{N}}{100 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = 450{,}000\,\text{Pa} = 450\,\text{kPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptable tenderness threshold for cooked beef:** $\tau_{max} \leq 80\,\text{N}$ shear force (ASTM F2925 standard) → this sample is **tough** and would benefit from enzymatic tenderization (papain, bromelain) or aging.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Universal Texture Analyzer Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.1: Functional Block Architecture of a Bench-Top Universal Texture Analyzer**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply and Controller Unit] --> B[Microprocessor with Test Profile Memory]
    B --> C[Motor Drive System Stepper or Servo]
    C --> D[Lead Screw and Linear Crosshead Actuator]
    D --> E[Probe or Fixture Mounting Plate]
    F[Load Cell Force Sensor 0.1 N to 5000 N] --> E
    G[Position Encoder LVDT or Optical] --> D
    E --> H[Food Specimen on Lower Fixed Platform]
    F --> I[Signal Amplifier and ADC]
    G --> J[Displacement Digitizer]
    I --> K[Data Acquisition and Software Module]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Force Distance Time Curve Generator]
    L --> M[Computer Interface for TPA Calculation]
    K --> N[Plotter and Report Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Logic Flow:**
> 1. Controller commands motor to drive crosshead at preset velocity.
> 2. Probe contacts specimen → load cell registers force (F).
> 3. Position encoder logs displacement (D) synchronously.
> 4. (F, D, t) data stream to PC → software computes stress, strain, energy, and TPA parameters.
> 5. Real-time curve plotted; report exported.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — TPA Operational Sequence

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.2: Texture Profile Analysis Sequential Logic**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Load Sample on Platform] --> P2[Lower Probe Until Trigger Load Detected 0.05 N]
    P2 --> P3[Compress Sample to Preset Distance 50 to 75 percent of Height]
    P3 --> P4[Withdraw Probe Upward at Same Speed]
    P4 --> P5[Hold for Wait Period 5 seconds Typical]
    P5 --> P6[Second Compression Identical Distance and Speed]
    P6 --> P7[Final Withdrawal]
    P7 --> P8[Acquire F vs t Data for Both Bites]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Areas A1 A2 A3 A4 A5]
    P9 --> P10[Calculate Eight TPA Parameters]
    P10 --> P11[Output Report Hardness Cohesiveness Springiness etc]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — TPA Force–Time Curve (Labelled Regions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.3: Labelled Force-Time Curve for a Two-Bite TPA Test**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Region A1: Work of First Bite] --> B[Peak Force Equals Hardness H]
    B --> C[Region Negative A3: Adhesiveness]
    C --> D[Region A4: Up Stroke Recovery]
    D --> E[Wait Period]
    E --> F[Region A2: Work of Second Bite]
    F --> G[Final Probe Withdrawal]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Logic — Selecting Probe Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.4: KTU Board-Style Decision Flow for Probe Selection**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the food physical state?] --> Q2{Solid Bulk}
    Q1 --> Q3{Semi Solid or Gel}
    Q1 --> Q4{Liquid or Viscous}
    Q2 --> Q5[Use Flat Cylindrical Probe TPA or Compression]
    Q3 --> Q6[Use Cylindrical Penetration Probe or Back Extrusion Disc]
    Q4 --> Q7[Use Cone and Plate Viscometer or Capillary Rheometer]
    Q5 --> Q8[Report Hardness, Cohesiveness, Chewiness]
    Q6 --> Q9[Report Firmness and Consistency]
    Q7 --> Q10[Report Viscosity and Yield Stress]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Texture Profile Analysis. List any four TPA parameters with their mathematical expressions.**

`[KTU University Exam - April 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) is an instrumental double-compression test that simulates two successive bites of a food material by a texture analyzer equipped with a calibrated probe, generating a force–time curve from which multiple textural parameters are computed.

**Four TPA Parameters:**

1. **Hardness:** $H = F_{peak,1}$ — peak force during first compression (N).
2. **Cohesiveness:** $Co = A_2/A_1$ — ratio of work of second bite to first bite (dimensionless).
3. **Springiness:** $Sp = t_2/t_1$ — ratio of recovery time to compression time (dimensionless).
4. **Chewiness:** $Ch = H \cdot Co \cdot Sp$ — energy to chew solid food to swallowable state (N·mm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark definition + 0.5 mark × 4 parameters + 0.5 mark × 4 units = 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Sensory and Instrumental Evaluation of Texture.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensory Evaluation | Instrumental Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Evaluator** | Trained / untrained human panel | Texture analyzer (machine) |
| **Subjectivity** | High (panel variability) | Objective and reproducible |
| **Scale** | Hedonic / descriptive (verbal, numerical) | Force (N), distance (mm), energy (N·s) |
| **Speed** | Slow (training, panel sessions) | Rapid (seconds per test) |
| **Cost** | High long-term (panel maintenance) | One-time equipment cost |
| **Correlation** | Direct human perception | Correlated statistically with sensory data |
| **Standards** | ISO 8586 (panel selection) | ASTM, AACC, ISO methods |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark for correct comparison basis + 1 mark tabulated comparison + 0.5 mark for concluding remark = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each. Internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Elective Subject Food Tech]`

**(a) Explain the principle, instrumentation, and procedure of Texture Profile Analysis. Draw a typical TPA force–time curve and label all key regions. (7 marks)**

**(b) Define the following TPA parameters and give the formulae: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness. A bread sample tested by TPA shows peak force 18 N, A₁ = 90 N·s, A₂ = 60 N·s, recovery time t₂ = 3.6 s, compression time t₁ = 4.5 s. Compute all five parameters and interpret the texture. (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Principle, Instrumentation, Procedure & Curve (7 marks)**

**Principle (2 marks):**
TPA imitates the two-bite chewing action of the human mouth. A food specimen of defined geometry is subjected to **two successive uniaxial compressions** by a probe mounted on a motorized crosshead, while a load cell records force and an encoder records displacement/time. The resulting **force–time curve** is integrated to extract mechanical energy terms corresponding to human-perceived textural attributes.

**Instrumentation (2 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Load cell (typically 5 kg, 50 kg) | Measures force in real time |
| Stepper/servo motor + lead screw | Drives crosshead at constant velocity |
| Position encoder (LVDT) | Records probe displacement |
| Probe/fixture (flat, cylindrical, conical) | Deforms specimen |
| Software (e.g., TexturePro, Exponent) | Calculates TPA parameters |

**Procedure (1.5 marks):**
1. Calibrate load cell and displacement.
2. Place specimen (cube/cylinder, standardized dimensions) on lower platform.
3. Lower probe until trigger force (≈ 0.05 N) detects contact.
4. Compress to 50–75% of original height at constant speed (1–10 mm/s).
5. Withdraw; wait 5 s.
6. Compress again identically.
7. Withdraw and save data.

**Curve (1.5 marks):** See **Figure 4.3** in Section 4 — labels $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, H, Sp$.

---

**(b) TPA Parameters — Definitions and Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Definitions and Formulae (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Hardness (H) | Peak force in first compression | $H = F_{peak,1}$ |
| Cohesiveness (Co) | Internal bonding strength | $Co = A_2/A_1$ |
| Springiness (Sp) | Elastic recovery | $Sp = t_2/t_1$ |
| Gummininess (Gu) | Energy for semi-solid disintegration | $Gu = H \cdot Co$ |
| Chewiness (Ch) | Energy for solid disintegration | $Ch = Gu \cdot Sp$ |

**Numerical Computation (2.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hardness:**
$$
H = 18\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Cohesiveness:**
$$
Co = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{60}{90} = 0.667
$$

**Step 3 — Springiness:**
$$
Sp = \frac{t_2}{t_1} = \frac{3.6}{4.5} = 0.80
$$

**Step 4 — Gumminess:**
$$
Gu = H \times Co = 18 \times 0.667 = 12.0\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Chewiness:**
$$
Ch = Gu \times Sp = 12.0 \times 0.80 = 9.6\,\text{N·mm}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
The bread is **moderately soft (18 N)** with **good internal cohesion (0.667)** and **high springiness (0.80)** → it has a **soft, spongy, fresh crumb**. If aged, hardness and chewiness would rise while springiness drops — a classic staling indicator.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternate Set]`

**(a) Describe in detail the Warner-Bratzler shear test and Kramer Shear Cell test. Compare their working principles, sample preparation, and reporting parameters. (7 marks)**

**(b) (i) Define the following terms: stress, strain, Young's Modulus. (3 marks)**
**(ii) A cylindrical cheese sample of diameter 30 mm and height 20 mm is compressed to 12% strain at peak load of 35 N. Calculate engineering stress, strain, and Young's Modulus. (4 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Warner-Bratzler vs. Kramer Shear Test (7 marks)**

**Warner-Bratzler (WB) Shear Test — 3 marks:**
- **Principle:** A rectangular meat core (typically 1 × 1 × 3 cm) is sheared perpendicular to fiber direction by a V-shaped blade.
- **Procedure:** Blade descends at constant speed (e.g., 200 mm/min) through the sample until complete cut.
- **Reported parameter:** Peak shear force (N) or shear stress (kPa).

**Kramer Shear Cell Test — 3 marks:**
- **Principle:** A multi-blade fixture shears a bulk sample (typically 200 g of ground/minced food) confined in a slotted cell.
- **Procedure:** Blades pass through slots in the cell bottom, shearing the sample extrudate.
- **Reported parameter:** Peak shear force per unit mass (N/g).

**Comparative Table — 1 mark:**

| Feature | Warner-Bratzler | Kramer Shear Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Sample form | Whole muscle core | Ground / bulk |
| Cutting geometry | Single V-blade | Multi-blade array |
| Parameter | Force (N) | Force per gram (N/g) |
| Application | Tenderness of intact meat | Processed meat, vegetables |

---

**(b) Stress, Strain, Modulus — Definitions and Calculation (7 marks)**

**(i) Definitions (3 marks):**

- **Stress (σ):** Internal force per unit original cross-sectional area; $\sigma = F/A_0$, unit: Pa.
- **Strain (ε):** Relative deformation; $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$, dimensionless.
- **Young's Modulus (E):** Ratio of stress to strain in elastic regime; $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$, unit: Pa.

**(ii) Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 30\,\text{mm}$, $L_0 = 20\,\text{mm}$, $\varepsilon = 0.12$, $F = 35\,\text{N}$

**Step 1 — Original Area:**
$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (30 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 9 \times 10^{-4}}{4} \approx 7.069 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Engineering Stress:**
$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{35}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 49{,}512\,\text{Pa} \approx 49.51\,\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Strain (given):**
$$
\varepsilon = 0.12 = 12\%
$$

**Step 4 — Young's Modulus:**
$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{49{,}512}{0.12} \approx 412{,}600\,\text{Pa} \approx 412.6\,\text{kPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The cheese has a moderate elastic modulus (~413 kPa), typical of **semi-hard cheese like Gouda or Cheddar**. Soft cheeses (Mozzarella) ≈ 50–100 kPa; hard cheeses (Parmesan) ≈ 1–10 MPa.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Texture TPA Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Reporting Gumminess in N·mm or Chewiness in N — examiners strictly enforce units. Gumminess = N; Chewiness = N·mm.
> 2. **Reversing Springiness formula:** $Sp = t_2/t_1$, NOT $t_1/t_2$. Springiness > 1 indicates overshoot (rare); typically $0.5 < Sp < 1$.
> 3. **Ignoring the 5-second wait period** between bites — without it, adhesiveness cannot be measured correctly.
> 4. **Confusing Engineering Stress and True Stress** — at strains > 20%, the difference exceeds 25%.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert mm² → m²** in stress calculations — a classic 1-mark loss in numerical problems.
> 6. **Confusing Adhesiveness (negative area) with Cohesiveness (positive area ratio)** — both relate to internal/external binding but are *not* the same.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Instrumental Texture Measurement**

- ☐ **Texture** = mechanical + structural property of food, measurable as **resistance to force**.
- ☐ **Three fundamental mechanical quantities:** Stress (Pa), Strain (dimensionless), Modulus (Pa).
- ☐ **Young's Modulus:** $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ for elastic foods.
- ☐ **TPA = double-bite compression test**, foundation of instrumental texture analysis.
- ☐ **Eight TPA parameters:** Hardness, Fracturability, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Adhesiveness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience.
- ☐ **Gumminess** = Hardness × Cohesiveness (for semi-solids).
- ☐ **Chewiness** = Gumminess × Springiness (for solids).
- ☐ **Cohesiveness** = $A_2/A_1$ — ratio of areas under two bites.
- ☐ **Probe speed** must be **constant** (typically 1–10 mm/s).
- ☐ **Standard specimen geometry** (cube/cylinder) is essential for reproducibility.
- ☐ **Warner-Bratzler** → single V-blade, for whole muscle meat tenderness.
- ☐ **Kramer Shear Cell** → multi-blade, for ground/processed foods.
- ☐ **Back-extrusion** → for viscous/liquid foods (yogurt, sauces).
- ☐ **Three-point bend test** → for brittle solids (cereals, crackers).
- ☐ **Instrumental data correlates with sensory data** via statistical methods (PCA, regression).
- ☐ **Standards to know:** ASTM F2925 (meat), AACC 56-36 (bread), ISO 11036 (sensory texture).
- ☐ **Always report:** sample dimensions, probe geometry, test speed, deformation %, replicate count (≥ 5), and temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Viscometer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Viscometer** is a precision analytical instrument used to measure the **viscosity** of a fluid — that is, its internal resistance to flow or deformation under an applied shear stress. In the context of food analysis, a viscometer quantifies the "thickness" or "flow behaviour" of liquid, semi-solid, and paste-like food products such as syrups, sauces, dairy products, oils, purees, and polymer solutions. > [!NOTE] > **Viscosity (η or μ):** A measure of a fluid's resistance to gradual deformation by shear stress or tensile stress. For liquids, it corresponds to the informal concept of "thickness." The SI unit is **Pascal-second (Pa·s)**, while the CGS unit is the **poise (P)**, where **1 Pa·s = 10 P**. ## 1.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Governing Principle The fundamental theoretical foundation of viscometry is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, which states that the shear stress (τ) between adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (du/dy) perpendicular to the flow direction. Mathematically: $$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ Where: - **τ** = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa) - **η** = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s) - **du/dy** = Velocity gradient or **shear rate** (s⁻¹), denoted as **γ̇** ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Analogy — The "Honey vs. Water" Test:** > > Imagine pouring **water** and **honey** through the same funnel at the same temperature. Water flows out almost instantly in a thin, fast stream, while honey creeps out slowly in a thick, sluggish ribbon. This difference is **viscosity**. Water has a low viscosity (~1 mPa·s at 25 °C), while honey has a viscosity roughly **10,000 to 20,000 times higher**. > > A viscometer is essentially a controlled, scientific version of this "funnel test" — but with precise measurement of force, time, geometry, and temperature, allowing exact numerical comparison between samples. ## 1.4 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids — A Food-Critical Distinction | Property | Newtonian Fluids | Non-Newtonian Fluids (Most Foods!) | |---|---|---| | Viscosity behaviour | Constant at constant temperature | Viscosity changes with shear rate | | Equation | τ = η·γ̇ | τ = f(γ̇) — non-linear relationship | | Food examples | Water, milk, refined oils, dilute sugar solutions | Ketchup, honey, chocolate, yogurt, mayonnaise, tomato paste, cream | | Shear stress response | Linear | Curved (shear-thinning/thickening, yield stress) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about food viscometry, always clarify whether the food is Newtonian or non-Newtonian. Most real food products (ketchup, honey, cream, chocolate) are **non-Newtonian**, and this drastically affects the choice of viscometer and interpretation of results. ## 1.5 Kinematic Viscosity In addition to dynamic viscosity, food analysts often use **kinematic viscosity (ν)**, which is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density: $$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$ Where **ρ** = density of the fluid (kg/m³). The unit of kinematic viscosity is **m²/s** or **Stokes (St)**, where 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Viscometers — Operational Taxonomy Viscometers are classified based on the physical principle used to induce and measure flow. Each type has specific advantages in food analysis depending on the sample state, viscosity range, and required accuracy. ### 2.1.1 Capillary (Tube) Viscometers - **Principle:** Measure the time required for a fixed volume of liquid to flow through a calibrated capillary tube under gravity. - **Best for:** Newtonian fluids of low-to-moderate viscosity (oils, syrups, sugar solutions). - **Examples:** Ostwald viscometer, Ubbelohde viscometer, Cannon-Fenske viscometer. - **Governing equation:** **Hagen–Poiseuille Law.** ### 2.1.2 Rotational Viscometers - **Principle:** A spindle, bob, or cone rotates at a defined speed inside the sample, and the torque required to maintain that rotation is measured. - **Best for:** Both Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids (sauces, pastes, gels, creams, ketchup). - **Examples:** Brookfield viscometer, Haake Rotovisco, Cone-and-plate viscometer. - **Advantage:** Can apply controlled shear rates — ideal for characterizing non-Newtonian behaviour. ### 2.1.3 Falling Ball / Falling Piston Viscometers - **Principle:** A ball (or piston) falls through the fluid under gravity; the time taken is inversely related to viscosity. - **Best for:** Opaque, viscous Newtonian fluids (used in quality control of oils and lubricants). ### 2.1.4 Vibrational Viscometers - **Principle:** A vibrating probe immersed in the fluid experiences damping; the degree of damping correlates with viscosity. - **Best for:** Inline/online process control (e.g., continuous monitoring of syrup viscosity in a production line). ### 2.1.5 Cup-Type Viscometers (Efflux Cups) - **Principle:** Measure the time for a fixed volume of fluid to drain through a calibrated orifice. - **Examples:** Ford cup, Zahn cup, Saybolt viscometer. - **Use:** Quick industrial checks (paint, oil, food coatings). ## 2.2 Working Principle — Capillary Viscometer (Ostwald) > [!NOTE] > **Detailed Step-by-Step Operation:** > > 1. The viscometer is cleaned, dried, and thermostatted in a water bath at a precise temperature (typically 25 °C or 40 °C). > 2. A known volume of the food sample is introduced into the lower reservoir via the filling tube. > 3. The sample is drawn up into the upper bulb (timing bulb) using suction, past the upper timing mark. > 4. The sample is then allowed to flow down under gravity. > 5. The **time (t)** taken for the meniscus to pass between the upper and lower timing marks is recorded using a stopwatch. > 6. The procedure is repeated for a reference fluid (usually distilled water) of known viscosity. ## 2.3 Working Principle — Brookfield Rotational Viscometer 1. The sample container is placed on the temperature-controlled stage. 2. An appropriate spindle (e.g., #1 to #7) is selected and immersed. 3. The spindle rotates at a set RPM (e.g., 10, 20, 50, 100 RPM). 4. A spring inside the viscometer deflects in proportion to the viscous drag (torque) on the spindle. 5. The dial reading (0–100 scale) is converted to viscosity in centipoise (cP) using a multiplication factor that depends on the spindle and speed. 6. By varying the RPM, a **flow curve** (viscosity vs. shear rate) can be generated — critical for non-Newtonian foods. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description / Variables | Units | Applicable Viscometer | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | τ: Pa, η: Pa·s, γ̇: s⁻¹ | All (theoretical) | | 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\eta}{\rho}$ | Kinematic Viscosity | m²/s | All (theoretical) | | 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4}}{8 \cdot \eta \cdot L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille Volumetric Flow Rate | m³/s | Capillary | | 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r^{4} \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$ | Absolute Viscosity from Capillary | Pa·s | Capillary (Ostwald) | | 5 | $\eta_{1} = \eta_{2} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{1} \cdot t_{1}}{\rho_{2} \cdot t_{2}}$ | Relative Viscosity (Calibration Method) | Pa·s | Capillary (Ostwald) | | 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{M \cdot (r_{b}^{2} - r_{c}^{2})}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot r_{c}^{3}}$ | Viscosity from Falling Ball (Höppler) | Pa·s | Falling Ball | | 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{2 \pi r_{c}^{2} L}$ | Shear Stress in Rotational Viscometer | Pa | Rotational | | 8 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60 \cdot t_{c}}$ | Shear Rate (Cone-and-Plate) | s⁻¹ | Cone-and-Plate | | 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent Viscosity | Pa·s | Rotational | | 10 | $\text{Multiplier Factor} = \dfrac{\eta_{cP}}{\text{Dial Reading}}$ | Brookfield Conversion | cP | Brookfield | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Aid:** Always remember that in the Hagen–Poiseuille equation, viscosity is **inversely proportional** to the fourth power of the radius (η ∝ 1/r⁴). A small change in capillary bore causes a huge change in measured flow time — which is why precise bore calibration is essential. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Food-Industry Utility - **Quality Control (QC):** Ensures batch-to-batch consistency of ketchup, sauces, mayonnaise, baby foods. - **Process Engineering:** Determines pumping, mixing, and piping requirements in food plants. - **Sensory Correlation:** Viscosity strongly correlates with "mouthfeel," a critical sensory attribute. - **Shelf-Life & Stability:** Viscosity changes over time reveal aggregation, sedimentation, or fermentation in products. - **R&D and Formulation:** Designing new textures (e.g., low-fat spreads mimicking full-fat cream). - **Regulatory Compliance:** Standards by **BIS, ISO, ASTM, and Codex Alimentarius** for specific food products. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Capillary Viscometer) This is the central derivation of capillary viscometry. It relates the **volumetric flow rate (Q)** of a viscous fluid through a cylindrical tube to the pressure drop, tube geometry, and viscosity. ### Step 1 — Setup and Assumptions Consider a fluid flowing steadily through a horizontal cylindrical capillary tube of: - Radius = **r** - Length = **L** - Pressure difference between ends = **ΔP = P₁ − P₂** **Assumptions:** - The fluid is **Newtonian** and **incompressible**. - Flow is **laminar** (Re < 2100), **steady**, and **isothermal**. - There is **no-slip** at the wall (fluid velocity at the wall is zero). - The flow is fully developed (parabolic velocity profile). ### Step 2 — Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element Consider a coaxial fluid cylinder of radius **x** (where x ≤ r) inside the tube. Two forces act on it along the axis: **(a) Pressure force (driving):** $$F_{pressure} = \Delta P \cdot \pi x^{2}$$ **(b) Viscous shear force (resisting):** $$F_{viscous} = \tau \cdot 2 \pi x L$$ Where τ is the shear stress acting on the lateral surface area 2πxL. ### Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition (Steady State) For steady, fully developed flow, the net force on the fluid cylinder is zero: $$\Delta P \cdot \pi x^{2} = \tau \cdot 2 \pi x L$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for Shear Stress Rearranging the equilibrium equation: $$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot x}{2 L}$$ This shows that the shear stress varies **linearly** from zero at the centre (x = 0) to a maximum at the wall (x = r): $$\tau_{wall} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$ ### Step 5 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity By definition: $$\tau = -\eta \cdot \frac{du}{dx}$$ Note the negative sign because velocity **decreases** as we move **away** from the centre. Substituting the expression for τ: $$-\eta \cdot \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot x}{2 L}$$ ### Step 6 — Separate Variables and Integrate Rearranging: $$du = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot x}{2 \eta L} \cdot dx$$ Integrating between the centre (where x = 0, velocity = u_max) and any radius x (velocity = u): $$\int_{u_{max}}^{u} du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} \int_{0}^{x} x \, dx$$ $$u - u_{max} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2}$$ $$u = u_{max} - \frac{\Delta P \cdot x^{2}}{4 \eta L}$$ ### Step 7 — Apply the No-Slip Boundary Condition At the wall (x = r), velocity u = 0. Substituting: $$0 = u_{max} - \frac{\Delta P \cdot r^{2}}{4 \eta L}$$ $$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r^{2}}{4 \eta L}$$ ### Step 8 — Velocity Profile (Parabolic) The fully developed velocity distribution is therefore: $$u(x) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} \left( r^{2} - x^{2} \right)$$ This is the classic **parabolic (Poiseuille) flow profile**. ### Step 9 — Volumetric Flow Rate The volumetric flow rate Q is obtained by integrating the velocity over the circular cross-section: $$Q = \int_{0}^{r} u(x) \cdot 2 \pi x \, dx$$ Substituting u(x): $$Q = \int_{0}^{r} \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} \left( r^{2} - x^{2} \right) \cdot 2 \pi x \, dx$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \int_{0}^{r} \left( r^{2} x - x^{3} \right) dx$$ ### Step 10 — Evaluate the Integral $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \left[ \frac{r^{2} x^{2}}{2} - \frac{x^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{r}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \left( \frac{r^{4}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right)$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \cdot \frac{r^{4}}{4}$$ ### Step 11 — Final Hagen–Poiseuille Equation $$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4}}{8 \cdot \eta \cdot L}}$$ Solving for η (the absolute dynamic viscosity): $$\eta = \frac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4} \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$$ where t is the time for volume V to flow through the capillary. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Viscometers in Food Analysis | Food Sample | Approximate Viscosity (mPa·s) | Rheology Type | Recommended Viscometer | Why? | |---|---|---|---|---| | Water, milk | 1 – 3 | Newtonian | Capillary (Ostwald / Ubbelohde) | High accuracy for low viscosity; simple & cheap | | Refined edible oil | 30 – 100 | Newtonian | Capillary / Brookfield LV | Temperature-sensitive; needs precise thermostatting | | Honey | 2,000 – 20,000 | Non-Newtonian (shear-thinning) | Brookfield RV / Falling Ball | Wide range, robust for sticky samples | | Ketchup | 5,000 – 50,000 | Non-Newtonian (yield stress + shear-thinning) | Brookfield with T-bar spindle | Standard test for ketchup (e.g., Bostwick vs. Brookfield) | | Yogurt, cream | 100 – 5,000 | Non-Newtonian (thixotropic) | Brookfield, Cone-and-Plate | Need controlled shear rate to study structure | | Chocolate (molten) | 500 – 5,000 | Non-Newtonian (Casson fluid) | Rotational with vane spindle | Yield stress is critical to moulding behaviour | | Tomato paste / puree | 1,000 – 100,000 | Non-Newtonian (shear-thinning) | Brookfield Helipath | Helipath allows measurement as the level falls | | Fruit juice (concentrated) | 50 – 5,000 | Newtonian / slightly non-Newtonian | Capillary or Brookfield LV | Depends on solids content and pulp | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A capillary viscometer of radius **0.5 mm** and length **10 cm** is used to measure the viscosity of a honey sample. The pressure drop driving the flow is **100 Pa**, and the time taken for **1 cm³** of honey to flow through the capillary is **80 seconds**. Calculate the dynamic viscosity of the honey. **Given:** - r = 0.5 mm = 0.5 × 10⁻³ m - L = 10 cm = 0.10 m - ΔP = 100 Pa - t = 80 s - V = 1 cm³ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ m³ **Solution:** $$\eta = \frac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4} \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$$ Substituting values: $$\eta = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{4} \times 80}{8 \times 0.10 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}$$ Computing r⁴: $$(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{4} = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \, m^{4}$$ Substituting: $$\eta = \frac{3.1416 \times 100 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 80}{8 \times 0.10 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\eta = \frac{1.5708 \times 10^{-8}}{8 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$\eta \approx 0.1963 \, Pa \cdot s \approx 196.3 \, mPa \cdot s$$ > [!NOTE] > This low value is consistent with a **diluted or low-grade honey**. Pure honey typically shows 2,000–20,000 mPa·s. The result here suggests either thin honey or measurement at a high temperature (e.g., 40 °C) where viscosity decreases. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Viscometer Operation & Decision Flow The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical rotational viscometer system, mapping how user inputs, mechanical subsystems, sensors, and signal-processing interact to yield a viscosity reading. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Food Sample
