State Newton's Law of Viscosity. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Model Answer:** **Viscosity** is the property of a fluid that resists its flow or deformation under an applied shear stress. It arises from internal friction between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities. Mathematically, it is the ratio of shear stress to shear rate. **Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the shear stress (τ) between two adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (du/dy) — also called the shear rate (γ̇) — perpendicular to the direction of flow: $$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ The proportionality constant η is the **dynamic viscosity** of the fluid, measured in Pa·s (SI) or poise (CGS). The law is valid for **Newtonian fluids**; for non-Newtonian fluids (e.g., most foods), the relationship becomes non-linear. > [!NOTE] > Examiner's Note: Students often forget to define the **velocity gradient** or to specify the units of η. Always mention the proportionality constant explicitly. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with two food examples of each. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Newtonian Fluids | Non-Newtonian Fluids | |---|---|---| | Definition | Viscosity is **constant** at a given temperature, independent of shear rate | Viscosity **changes** with applied shear rate | | Relationship | Linear: τ = η·γ̇ | Non-linear: τ = f(γ̇) | | Food examples | Water, milk, refined sunflower oil, dilute sugar syrup | Ketchup, honey, mayonnaise, chocolate, yogurt, tomato paste | | Viscosity measurement | A single viscosity value is sufficient | Multiple shear rates needed; flow curve required | > [!IMPORTANT] > Most natural and processed food products are **non-Newtonian**, which is why a rotational viscometer capable of varying shear rate is preferred over a capillary viscometer for most food applications. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Option 1): Viscometer Types and Capillary Viscometer Principle **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Classify the different types of viscometers used in food analysis. Explain the working principle of a **Brookfield Rotational Viscometer** with a neat labelled diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for a capillary viscometer. State the assumptions made during derivation. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Classification of Viscometers in Food Analysis:** Viscometers used in food analysis can be broadly classified as: 1. **Capillary (Tube) Viscometers** — Ostwald, Ubbelohde, Cannon-Fenske 2. **Rotational Viscometers** — Brookfield, Cone-and-Plate, Coaxial Cylinder 3. **Falling Ball Viscometers** — Höppler-type 4. **Vibrational Viscometers** — Tuning-fork probe type (for online use) 5. **Efflux (Cup) Viscometers** — Ford cup, Zahn cup, Saybolt **Working Principle of Brookfield Rotational Viscometer:** The Brookfield viscometer is a torque-measuring rotational instrument, ideal for non-Newtonian food systems. Its operation is as follows: 1. The food sample is placed in a **thermostatted container** on the viscometer stage. 2. An appropriate **spindle** (numbered #1 to #7, with #1 being smallest diameter) is selected and lowered into the sample until the immersion mark is reached. 3. The **motor** drives the spindle to rotate at a user-defined speed (e.g., 10, 50, 100 RPM). 4. The viscous drag of the sample exerts a **torque** on the spindle axis. 5. This torque is balanced by the **deflection of a calibrated spring** inside the instrument. 6. The deflection is read on a **dial (0–100 scale)** or digitally. 7. The **dial reading × multiplier factor** (a function of spindle + RPM) gives the viscosity in centipoise (cP). 8. By repeating at multiple RPMs, a **flow curve** of viscosity versus shear rate is obtained, essential for characterising non-Newtonian foods. **Key Advantages for Food Analysis:** - Wide viscosity range (1 cP to 8 × 10⁶ cP). - Suitable for opaque, heterogeneous samples. - Can measure thixotropy, yield stress, and shear-thinning behaviour. - Sample volume requirement is small (typically 50–500 mL). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation:** *(See Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation. The model answer should reproduce the following key steps:)* 1. State the **assumptions**: Newtonian fluid, laminar flow, steady state, isothermal, no-slip at the wall, fully developed flow. 2. **Force balance** on a coaxial fluid cylinder of radius x: $$\Delta P \cdot \pi x^{2} = \tau \cdot 2 \pi x L$$ 3. Solve for shear stress: $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot x}{2 L}$ 4. Apply **Newton's Law of Viscosity**: $\tau = -\eta \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx}$ 5. Combine and integrate using the no-slip condition at the wall: $$u(x) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} (r^{2} - x^{2})$$ 6. Integrate velocity over the cross-section to get volumetric flow rate: $$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4}}{8 \cdot \eta \cdot L}}$$ 7. Solve for η: $$\eta = \frac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4} \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **miss stating the assumptions** — this loses 1–2 marks. > - The **negative sign** in Newton's law (velocity decreases away from centre) is frequently omitted; examiners specifically check this. > - The **order of integration limits** (centre to radius) is often reversed — keep it consistent. > - Final answer must be in terms of **measurable quantities** (P, r, L, V, t) — not just η as a symbol. > - Do NOT confuse the formula with the Fanning or Darcy equation — they have different constants. --- ### Question B (Option 2): Viscosity of Non-Newtonian Foods and Brookfield Application **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **kinematic viscosity** and **dynamic viscosity**. A capillary viscometer is used to determine the viscosity of a sugar syrup at 25 °C. The flow time of the syrup is 240 seconds, and the flow time of pure water is 60 seconds. The density of the syrup is **1.30 g/cm³** and that of water is **0.997 g/cm³**. The viscosity of water at 25 °C is **0.89 mPa·s**. Calculate the absolute (dynamic) viscosity of the syrup. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the importance of viscosity measurement in the food industry. Discuss why a **Brookfield viscometer** is preferred over a **capillary viscometer** for analysing ketchup, honey, and chocolate. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity:** - **Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (η):** The internal resistance of a fluid to flow under applied shear stress. SI unit: Pa·s; CGS unit: poise (P) or centipoise (cP). **1 cP = 1 mPa·s**. - **Kinematic viscosity (ν):** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under the action of gravity. $$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$ SI unit: m²/s; CGS unit: Stokes (St) or centistokes (cSt). **1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s**. **Numerical Calculation:** **Given:** - t₁ (syrup) = 240 s - t₂ (water) = 60 s - ρ₁ (syrup) = 1.30 g/cm³ = 1300 kg/m³ - ρ₂ (water) = 0.997 g/cm³ = 997 kg/m³ - η₂ (water) = 0.89 mPa·s **Formula (relative method, same capillary and volume):** $$\eta_{1} = \eta_{2} \cdot \frac{\rho_{1} \cdot t_{1}}{\rho_{2} \cdot t_{2}}$$ **Substituting:** $$\eta_{1} = 0.89 \times \frac{1.30 \times 240}{0.997 \times 60}$$ $$\eta_{1} = 0.89 \times \frac{312}{59.82}$$ $$\eta_{1} = 0.89 \times 5.216$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{1} \approx 4.64 \, mPa \cdot s}$$ This viscosity is consistent with a **light sugar syrup** (e.g., simple syrup used in confectionery). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Importance of Viscosity in the Food Industry:** Viscosity is a **critical quality and process parameter** in food manufacturing for the following reasons: 1. **Quality Control (QC):** Viscosity determines the **texture, mouthfeel, and consumer acceptance** of products such as sauces, yogurts, syrups, and beverages. Batch-to-batch consistency is verified by viscosity measurement. 2. **Process Engineering:** Viscosity data is essential for sizing **pumps, pipes, mixers, heat exchangers, and filling machines**. Higher viscosity fluids need larger pumps and more energy. 3. **Sensory Science:** Viscosity directly correlates with sensory attributes like **thickness, smoothness, and creaminess** perceived by consumers. 4. **Shelf-Life and Stability:** Changes in viscosity over time reveal **aggregation, syneresis, fermentation, or degradation** in products like sauces, dairy, and emulsions. 5. **Formulation and R&D:** When developing **low-fat or reduced-sugar products**, viscosity must be matched to the original product using hydrocolloids (xanthan, guar, carrageenan). 6. **Regulatory Standards:** Many food standards (BIS, Codex, ISO) include viscosity specifications for products like honey, milk products, and edible oils. **Why Brookfield is Preferred for Ketchup, Honey, and Chocolate:** | Sample | Behaviour | Why Capillary is Inadequate | Why Brookfield is Ideal | |---|---|---|---| | **Ketchup** | Non-Newtonian with yield stress; shear-thinning | Capillary measures a single point under low shear; cannot capture yield stress | Spinning at multiple RPMs captures full flow curve; standard industry method | | **Honey** | Highly viscous, non-Newtonian, sticky | Difficult to load and clean in a capillary; long flow times | Spindle rotation is robust; broad viscosity range (with T-bar / Helipath) | | **Chocolate (molten)** | Casson fluid with yield stress; tempering-dependent | Temperature control is critical and difficult in capillaries | Cone-and-plate or vane spindle provides precise shear control at controlled T | In summary, **Brookfield viscometers allow multiple shear rates**, **accommodate heterogeneous and opaque samples**, and **are easier to clean** between food samples — making them the **industry gold standard** for non-Newtonian food viscometry. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - When explaining the Brookfield advantage, students often just say "it is more accurate." This is **too vague** — explain **why** (shear rate control, flow curve generation, suitability for non-Newtonian fluids). > - In the numerical, **unit conversions** (g/cm³ → kg/m³; cP → Pa·s) are commonly missed. Always show conversions. > - Do NOT forget to mention the **instrument constant / multiplier factor** when explaining the Brookfield method. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Viscosity (η):** Resistance to flow; SI unit = **Pa·s**; CGS = **poise (P)** or **centipoise (cP)**. - **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \eta \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — valid only for **Newtonian fluids**. - **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \dfrac{\eta}{\rho}$ — unit **m²/s** or **Stokes (St)**. - **Hagen–Poiseuille Equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P r^{4}}{8 \eta L}$ — capillary flow foundation. - **Capillary Viscometer** (Ostwald / Ubbelohde) → best for **Newtonian, low-viscosity** fluids; uses relative method with water. - **Brookfield Viscometer** → industry standard for **non-Newtonian foods**; measures torque over a range of RPMs; uses **multiplier factor**. - **Cone-and-Plate Viscometer** → uniform shear rate; best for **small samples and high accuracy**. - **Falling Ball (Höppler)** → useful for **opaque Newtonian fluids** like oils. - **Vibrational Viscometer** → ideal for **inline / online process control**. - **Assumptions in Hagen–Poiseuille derivation:** Newtonian, laminar, steady, isothermal, no-slip, fully developed flow. - **Most foods are non-Newtonian** → ketchup (shear-thinning + yield stress), chocolate (Casson), yogurt (thixotropic), honey (shear-thinning). - **η ∝ 1/r⁴** → capillary radius is **extremely critical**; small bore change causes huge flow time change. - **Viscosity decreases with temperature** → always **thermostat** food samples during measurement. - **Applications:** QC, sensory science, R&D, process design, shelf-life, regulatory compliance. - **Common KTU marks-losing mistakes:** missing assumptions, sign errors in Newton's law, wrong unit conversions, omitting the multiplier factor, not classifying foods as Newtonian vs. non-Newtonian.

topics.title: Viscoanalysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Viscoanalysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Viscoanalysis **Viscoanalysis** is the branch of instrumental food analysis that deals with the quantitative measurement and characterization of the **viscoelastic** and **viscous flow behaviour** of food materials under applied mechanical stress or deformation. It is fundamentally a sub-discipline of **Rheology** — the science of flow and deformation of matter. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, viscoanalysis in food science specifically concerns: - The measurement of **viscosity (η)** — the internal friction resisting flow. - The characterization of **viscoelastic moduli** — namely, the storage modulus **G′** (elastic component) and the loss modulus **G″** (viscous component). - The determination of flow behaviour indices for **non-Newtonian food systems**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Viscoanalysis is the instrumental evaluation of a food material's combined viscous and elastic response when subjected to a defined shear stress or shear strain, providing critical textural, structural, and quality parameters.* The two fundamental measurable parameters are: $$ \tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{(Newton's Law of Viscosity)} $$ Where: - **τ** (tau) = Shear stress (**Pa** or **N·m⁻²**) - **η** (eta) = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (**Pa·s** or **P**oise) - **γ̇** (gamma-dot) = Shear rate (**s⁻¹**) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "Pouring Test"** > Imagine three kitchen items: **water, honey, and tomato ketchup.** > - **Water** flows immediately when you tilt the glass. It obeys Newton's law perfectly (Newtonian). > - **Honey** flows, but slowly — it resists flow, showing high viscosity. The faster you push it, the more it resists (shear-thickening tendency in some honeys). > - **Ketchup** behaves strangely — it refuses to come out of the bottle when gently tilted, but once shaken (high shear), it gushes out. This is **shear-thinning** — a hallmark non-Newtonian behaviour common in food. > > **Viscoanalysis is the science that puts numbers on these intuitive observations.** It tells the food scientist *exactly* how much the ketchup will resist flow, how it will behave in pipes during processing, and how it will feel in the consumer's mouth (texture). The prefix **"visco-"** is crucial — most foods are **not purely viscous** (like water) nor **purely elastic** (like rubber). They exhibit **viscoelasticity**, meaning: - Some of the energy input is **stored elastically** (recoverable deformation — think of a cheese slice bending). - Some is **dissipated as heat** (viscous flow — think of cream spreading on bread). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Viscoanalysis | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Food Example | |---|---|---|---| | Dynamic Viscosity | η | **Pa·s** | Honey ≈ 2–10 Pa·s | | Kinematic Viscosity | ν | **m²/s** (or **cSt**) | Edible oils | | Shear Stress | τ | **Pa** | Process design | | Shear Rate | γ̇ | **s⁻¹** | Pipe flow | | Storage Modulus | G′ | **Pa** | Gel strength | | Loss Modulus | G″ | **Pa** | Liquid-like behaviour | | Complex Viscosity | η* | **Pa·s** | Oscillatory testing | | Loss Tangent | tan δ | dimensionless | Viscoelastic balance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion constant to memorize:** 1 **Pa·s = 10 Poise (P) = 1000 centipoise (cP)**. Water at 20 °C has η ≈ 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: From Newton's Law to Complex Fluids ### 2.1.1 Ideal (Newtonian) Fluid Behaviour The fundamental relationship was proposed by **Isaac Newton (1687)** and forms the bedrock of rheology. For a fluid confined between two parallel plates of area **A** separated by a distance **dy**, with the top plate moving at velocity **dv**: **Step 1 — Define Shear Stress:** The force **F** applied parallel to the surface per unit area gives the shear stress. $$ \tau = \frac{F}{A} \quad [\text{Pa}] $$ **Step 2 — Define Shear Rate:** The velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction is the shear rate. $$ \dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} \quad [\text{s}^{-1}] $$ **Step 3 — Newton's Law of Viscosity:** The proportionality constant between stress and rate of strain is the **dynamic viscosity**. $$ \tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} $$ For such a fluid, η is **constant** and **independent of shear rate**. Examples: water, milk, sucrose solutions, most edible oils (at moderate temperatures). ### 2.1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluid Behaviour Most foods (sauces, gels, doughs, batters, purees) **do not** obey Newton's law. Their apparent viscosity **η_a** varies with applied shear. These are classified by their **flow curve** (τ vs. γ̇ plot). **The Ostwald–de Waele Power Law (Herschel–Bulkley generalization):** $$ \tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n} $$ - **τ₀** = Yield stress (**Pa**) — minimum stress to initiate flow - **K** = Consistency index (**Pa·sⁿ**) - **n** = Flow behaviour index (dimensionless) The four major non-Newtonian categories are: | Behaviour Type | n value | τ₀ value | Food Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** | n < 1 | τ₀ = 0 | Ketchup, fruit juice concentrates, molten chocolate | | **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** | n > 1 | τ₀ = 0 | Corn starch suspensions, wet sand | | **Bingham Plastic** | n = 1 | τ₀ > 0 | Toothpaste, margarine | | **Casson Fluid** | specialized | τ₀ > 0 | Chocolate melt (above melting point) | > [!NOTE] > **Time-dependent fluids:** > - **Thixotropic** — viscosity **decreases** with time at constant shear (yogurt, paint). > - **Rheopectic** — viscosity **increases** with time at constant shear (rare; some gypsum suspensions). ### 2.1.3 Viscoelasticity — The Heart of Modern Food Rheology Real foods display both viscous and elastic responses simultaneously. The **Maxwell model** represents this by combining a dashpot (viscous) and spring (elastic) in **series**. For oscillatory shear at angular frequency **ω**: $$ G^{*}(\omega) = G'(\omega) + i \cdot G''(\omega) $$ Where **G\*** = complex modulus. The **storage modulus G′** represents elastic energy stored; the **loss modulus G″** represents viscous energy dissipated. **The two critical viscoelastic parameters:** $$ \tan \delta = \frac{G''}{G'} \quad \text{and} \quad \eta^{*} = \frac{\sqrt{(G')^{2} + (G'')^{2}}}{\omega} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule for Viscoelastic State:** > - If **G′ > G″** (tan δ < 1) → material is **gel-like / solid-like** (e.g., set yogurt, jelly). > - If **G″ > G′** (tan δ > 1) → material is **liquid-like** (e.g., melted chocolate, milk). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **τ = η · γ̇** | Newton's law; Newtonian fluids | Pa = Pa·s · s⁻¹ | | 2 | **ν = η / ρ** | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s | | 3 | **τ = K · γ̇ⁿ** | Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) | Pa = Pa·sⁿ · s⁻ⁿ | | 4 | **τ = τ₀ + K · γ̇ⁿ** | Herschel–Bulkley model | Pa | | 5 | **η_a = τ / γ̇** | Apparent viscosity definition | Pa·s | | 6 | **G\* = G′ + iG″** | Complex modulus (oscillatory) | Pa | | 7 | **tan δ = G″ / G′** | Loss tangent (solid vs. liquid) | dimensionless | | 8 | **η\* = G\* / ω** | Complex viscosity | Pa·s | | 9 | **G′ = (G\*)² · cos δ** | Storage modulus from complex modulus | Pa | | 10 | **G″ = (G\*)² · sin δ** | Loss modulus from complex modulus | Pa | | 11 | **η = (π · r⁴ · ΔP · t) / (8 · L · V)** | Hagen–Poiseuille (capillary) | Pa·s | | 12 | **η = (2 · r² · g · (ρ_s − ρ_l) · t) / (9 · L)** | Stokes' Law (falling ball) | Pa·s | | 13 | **η = (M · ω) / (k · γ̇)** | Rotational viscometer constant | Pa·s | | 14 | **δ_c = (ρ_s − ρ_l) / ρ_l** | Density correction (falling ball) | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **dynamic viscosity η (Pa·s)** with **kinematic viscosity ν (m²/s)**. The numerical values can differ by **three orders of magnitude** for the same fluid because ν = η/ρ. Always check which one the question asks for. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Industries Viscoanalysis is **not academic** — it drives industrial decisions: 1. **Process Engineering** — Pipe sizing, pump selection, and energy requirements for transporting tomato paste depend on **apparent viscosity at process shear rates** (typically 10–1000 s⁻¹). 2. **Quality Control** — Batch-to-batch consistency of mayonnaise or chocolate is monitored via Brookfield viscometer readings. 3. **Sensory Correlation** — Oral shear rates during chewing are ~10–100 s⁻¹; instrumental measurements at these shear rates correlate with perceived **mouthfeel** and **thickness**. 4. **Shelf-life Prediction** — Thixotropic recovery of a sauce after shearing predicts its **in-bottle stability** and **squeezability**. 5. **Formulation R&D** — Emulsion stability, gel setting kinetics (e.g., pectin), and starch retrogradation are all monitored viscoelastically. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hagen–Poiseuille Equation for Capillary Viscometry This derivation gives the **dynamic viscosity** from a capillary viscometer (Ostwald / Ubbelohde type), which is the most common glass viscometer used in dairy and oil labs. **Setup:** A fluid flows steadily through a vertical capillary of length **L** and internal radius **r**, driven by a hydrostatic pressure head **ΔP = ρgH**, where **H** is the mean liquid height. **Step 1 — Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element** of radius **x** (0 < x < r), length **L**: The pressure force pushing the fluid forward is balanced by the viscous drag on the cylindrical surface of area **2πxL**. $$ \Delta P \cdot \pi x^{2} = -\eta \cdot 2\pi x L \cdot \frac{dv}{dx} $$ **Step 2 — Simplify and separate variables:** $$ x \cdot \Delta P = -2\eta L \cdot \frac{dv}{dx} $$ $$ dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \cdot x \cdot dx $$ **Step 3 — Integrate from x = r (where v = 0, no-slip) to a general x:** $$ \int_{0}^{v} dv = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \int_{r}^{x} x \, dx $$ $$ v = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \left[ \frac{x^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{2}}{2} \right] = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} (r^{2} - x^{2}) $$ **Step 4 — Obtain volumetric flow rate Q** by integrating the velocity profile over the cross-section: $$ Q = \int_{0}^{r} v(x) \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = \frac{2\pi \Delta P}{4\eta L} \int_{0}^{r} (r^{2} - x^{2}) x \, dx $$ $$ Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L} \left[ \frac{r^{2} x^{2}}{2} - \frac{x^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{r} = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L} \left( \frac{r^{4}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right) $$ $$ Q = \frac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L} $$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of volume V and time t (Q = V/t):** $$ \eta = \frac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P \cdot t}{8 L \cdot V} $$ > [!NOTE] > **For a glass capillary viscometer**, the instrument constant **C** is pre-calibrated so that η = C · t · ρ (for the test fluid), where **t** is efflux time and **ρ** is fluid density. The factor ρ appears because ΔP ∝ ρgH. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stokes' Law for Falling-Ball Viscometer A sphere of radius **r_s** and density **ρ_s** falls through a Newtonian liquid of density **ρ_l** and viscosity **η** under gravity **g**. At terminal velocity, three forces balance. **Step 1 — Identify the three forces:** $$ F_{\text{gravity}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{s}^{3} \rho_{s} g $$ $$ F_{\text{buoyancy}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{s}^{3} \rho_{l} g $$ $$ F_{\text{drag}} = 6 \pi \eta r_{s} v_{t} \quad \text{(Stokes' drag)} $$ **Step 2 — Equilibrium condition (net force = 0 at terminal velocity):** $$ F_{\text{gravity}} = F_{\text{buoyancy}} + F_{\text{drag}} $$ $$ \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{s}^{3} \rho_{s} g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{s}^{3} \rho_{l} g + 6 \pi \eta r_{s} v_{t} $$ **Step 3 — Isolate η:** $$ 6 \pi \eta r_{s} v_{t} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{s}^{3} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{l}) $$ $$ \eta = \frac{2 r_{s}^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{l})}{9 v_{t}} $$ **Step 4 — Express v_t = L/t** (ball traverses distance L in time t): $$ \boxed{\eta = \frac{2 r_{s}^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{l}) t}{9 L}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rotational Viscometer — Torque–Shear Rate Relationship A **concentric cylinder (Couette) viscometer** has an inner bob of radius **R_i** rotating at angular velocity **Ω** inside an outer cup of radius **R_o**. The gap is small. **Step 1 — Shear rate at the bob surface:** $$ \dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{2 \Omega R_{i}^{2}}{R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2}} \approx \frac{2 \Omega R_{i}}{R_{o} - R_{i}} \quad \text{(for thin gap)} $$ **Step 2 — Shear stress on the cylindrical bob surface** of area A = 2πR_iL: $$ \tau = \frac{T}{2 \pi R_{i}^{2} L} $$ where T is the measured torque. **Step 3 — Dynamic viscosity:** $$ \eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{T (R_{o} - R_{i})}{4 \pi \Omega R_{i}^{3} L} $$ For a Brookfield-type viscometer, this is reduced to a **single instrument constant** k supplied by the manufacturer, giving: $$ \eta = k \cdot \frac{T}{\Omega} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maxwell Viscoelastic Model in Oscillatory Shear **Step 1 — Constitutive equation of the Maxwell element** (spring + dashpot in series): $$ \dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \frac{\tau}{\eta} $$ **Step 2 — Apply oscillatory strain** γ = γ₀ e^{iωt}. Then τ = G\* · γ. **Step 3 — Substituting and solving** (after differentiating the strain with respect to time): $$ G^{*}(\omega) = \frac{i \omega \eta}{1 + i \omega \tau_{\text{rel}}} $$ where **τ_rel = η/G** is the **relaxation time**. **Step 4 — Rationalize the complex expression:** $$ G'(\omega) = G \cdot \frac{(\omega \tau_{\text{rel}})^{2}}{1 + (\omega \tau_{\text{rel}})^{2}} $$ $$ G''(\omega) = G \cdot \frac{\omega \tau_{\text{rel}}}{1 + (\omega \tau_{\text{rel}})^{2}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **High-Frequency Limit** (ωτ >> 1): G′ → G (elastic), G″ → 0. Material behaves like a **solid**. > **Low-Frequency Limit** (ωτ << 1): G′ → 0, G″ → ωη. Material behaves like a **liquid**. > The **crossover point** where G′ = G″ is used to identify the gelation point in foods. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A ketchup sample is tested in a Brookfield viscometer at 25 °C using spindle No. 4 at 10 rpm. The dial reading is **48 units** and the instrument constant k for this combination is **2.0**. The density of ketchup is **1180 kg/m³**. Calculate the (a) dynamic viscosity and (b) kinematic viscosity. **Solution:** (a) The Brookfield viscometer reports viscosity directly using: $$ \eta = k \times \text{Dial Reading} = 2.0 \times 48 = 96 \text{ cP} $$ Convert to SI: $$ \eta = 96 \text{ cP} \times \frac{1 \text{ Pa·s}}{1000 \text{ cP}} = 0.096 \text{ Pa·s} $$ (b) Kinematic viscosity: $$ \nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho} = \frac{0.096}{1180} = 8.14 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s} = 81.4 \text{ cSt} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is consistent with literature values for tomato ketchup (typically 50–100 cP at 10 rpm, exhibiting pseudoplastic shear-thinning). ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Viscometer | Property Measured | Suitable Instrument | Typical Food Application | |---|---|---| | Single-point viscosity (Newtonian) | Capillary (Ostwald) | Milk, oils, sugar syrups | | Viscosity vs. shear rate | Rotational (Brookfield, cone–plate) | Sauces, batters, chocolate | | Time-dependent behaviour | Rotational with stepped shear | Thixotropy of yogurt | | Viscoelastic moduli G′, G″ | Oscillatory rheometer | Gel setting, dough development | | Very low viscosity | Falling-ball, U-tube | Beverages, fruit juices | | High-temperature, in-line | Vibrational viscometer | Cooking oil, molten chocolate | | Continuous monitoring | Process viscometer | CIP systems, fermentation broths | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification of Fluid Behaviour (Rheogram) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Fluid Behaviour]:::root --> nodeB[Newtonian]:::nwt nodeA --> nodeC[Non-Newtonian]:::nnt nodeB --> nodeD["Water, Milk, Edible Oils
tau = eta gamma"]:::leaf nodeC --> nodeE[Time-Independent]:::ti nodeC --> nodeF[Time-Dependent]:::td nodeC --> nodeG[Viscoelastic]:::ve nodeE --> nodeH[Pseudoplastic n less than 1]:::psd nodeE --> nodeI[Dilatant n greater than 1]:::dlt nodeE --> nodeJ[Bingham Plastic tau0 greater than 0 n equals 1]:::bnp nodeE --> nodeK[Yield-Pseudoplastic]:::ypp nodeF --> nodeL[Thixotropic - viscosity drops with time]:::thx nodeF --> nodeM[Rheopectic - viscosity rises with time]:::rhp nodeG --> nodeN[Maxwell Model]:::mxw nodeG --> nodeO[Kelvin-Voigt Model]:::kvg classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef nwt fill:#A8D8EA,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef nnt fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#660000,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef ti fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#1A4314,color:#000 classDef td fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000 classDef ve fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#4A235A,color:#000 classDef psd fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000 classDef dlt fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#660000,color:#000 classDef bnp fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef ypp fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#1A4314,color:#000 classDef thx fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000 classDef rhp fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef mxw fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#4A235A,color:#000 classDef kvg fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#4A235A,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Rotational Viscometer Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR nodePower[Power Supply and Motor Drive]:::pwr --> nodeSpeed[Speed Controller RPM Setpoint]:::spd nodeSpeed --> nodeSpindle[Rotating Spindle Bob]:::spnd nodeSpindle --> nodeSample[Food Sample in Cup]:::samp nodeSample --> nodeDrag[Viscous Drag Torque T]:::drag nodeDrag --> nodeSensor[Torque Sensor and Transducer]:::sns nodeSensor --> nodeAmp[Signal Amplifier]:::amp nodeAmp --> nodeADC[Analog to Digital Converter]:::adc nodeADC --> nodeMicro[Microprocessor and Calculation Unit]:::mcu nodeMicro --> nodeDisplay[Digital Display Viscosity cP]:::disp nodeMicro --> nodeInterface[USB Data Output for PC]:::int nodeMicro --> nodeFeedback[Feedback to Motor for Constant RPM]:::fb nodeFeedback --> nodeSpeed classDef pwr fill:#FFD966,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef spd fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef spnd fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef samp fill:#F9CB9C,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef drag fill:#EA9999,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef sns fill:#D5A6BD,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef amp fill:#B4A7D6,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef adc fill:#76A5AF,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef mcu fill:#A2C4C9,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef disp fill:#FFE599,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef int fill:#CCCCCC,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Flowchart: Viscoanalytical Decision Tree for a Food Sample ```mermaid graph TD nodeStart([Food Sample Arrives in QC Lab]):::start --> nodeQ1{Is it a clear liquid?}:::decision nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeCap[Use Capillary Viscometer
Ostwald or Ubbelohde]:::method nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeQ2{Does it contain particles
or is it opaque?}:::decision nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeQ3{Is viscosity changing with time?}:::decision nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeQ4{Is it gel-like or solid-like?}:::decision nodeQ3 -- Yes --> nodeThix[Test with stepped shear program
Hysteresis loop]:::method nodeQ3 -- No --> nodeRot[Rotational Viscometer
Brookfield or Cone-Plate]:::method nodeQ4 -- Yes --> nodeOsc[Oscillatory Rheometer
Measure G prime and G double prime]:::method nodeQ4 -- No --> nodeRot nodeCap --> nodeReport[Generate Report:
Viscosity value + temperature]:::report nodeThix --> nodeReport nodeRot --> nodeReport nodeOsc --> nodeReport classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFE599,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef method fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef report fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Oscillatory Viscoelastic Measurement Principle ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Drive["DRIVE SYSTEM"] nodeOsc[Function Generator Sinusoidal Signal]:::a nodeMotor[Servo Motor Applies Strain gamma zero sin omega t]:::a end subgraph Sample["SAMPLE HOLDER"] nodeGeom[Parallel Plate or Cone-Plate Geometry]:::b nodeFood[Food Specimen Held in Gap]:::b end subgraph Detect["DETECTION SYSTEM"] nodeForce[Force Transducer Measures Stress Response]:::c nodePhase[Phase Detector Extracts Phase Lag delta]:::c end subgraph Analysis["COMPUTATION"] nodeMath[Software Calculates G prime, G double prime, eta star, tan delta]:::d end nodeOsc --> nodeMotor nodeMotor --> nodeGeom nodeGeom --> nodeFood nodeFood --> nodeForce nodeForce --> nodePhase nodePhase --> nodeMath classDef a fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000 classDef b fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#1A4314,color:#000 classDef c fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef d fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#4A235A,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the term "viscosity" and explain Newton's law of viscosity. How is kinematic viscosity related to dynamic viscosity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Viscosity is the internal friction within a fluid that resists its flow. It is the proportionality constant between the applied shear stress and the resulting rate of shear strain. **Newton's Law (1 mark):** For a Newtonian fluid, $$ \tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} $$ where **τ** is shear stress (Pa), **η** is dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), and **γ̇** is shear rate (s⁻¹). The viscosity η is constant and independent of shear rate. **Relation to Kinematic Viscosity (1 mark):** Kinematic viscosity **ν** (m²/s) is dynamic viscosity divided by fluid density: $$ \nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Marking tip:** Examiners expect mention of **units (Pa·s for η, m²/s for ν)** along with the density relationship. A 3-mark answer should have a **definition + formula + units** structure. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids with two suitable food examples for each category.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Newtonian | Non-Newtonian | |---|---|---| | Viscosity behaviour | Constant, independent of shear rate | Viscosity varies with shear rate | | Flow curve | Straight line through origin | Curved or has yield stress | | **Examples** | Water, milk, refined sugar syrup, edible oils (1 mark) | Ketchup (pseudoplastic), toothpaste (Bingham plastic), cornstarch slurry (dilatant) (1 mark) | | Governing equation | τ = η · γ̇ (single parameter) | τ = K · γ̇ⁿ or τ = τ₀ + K · γ̇ⁿ (1 mark) | --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a **Brookfield rotational viscometer**. Discuss its advantages for food analysis. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** and explain how it is applied in a capillary viscometer to determine the viscosity of a Newtonian food liquid. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Brookfield Rotational Viscometer — 7 marks** **Construction (3 marks):** - A synchronous motor drives a vertical spindle (bob) at selectable, constant rotational speeds (0.3 to 100 rpm). - The spindle is immersed in the food sample held in a stationary cup. - A beryllium–copper spring inside the instrument measures the viscous drag (torque) on the spindle. - A pointer attached to the spring indicates the deflection on a calibrated dial (0–100 scale). - Modern Brookfield viscometers have digital torque sensors and microprocessor-based readout. **Working Principle (3 marks):** - When the spindle rotates in the sample, the viscous resistance generates a torque **T** on the spring. - The spring deflection is proportional to T and therefore to viscosity. - Viscosity is calculated as: $$ \eta = k \cdot T \cdot \frac{1}{\Omega} $$ where **k** is the geometry constant (spindle + cup), **T** is torque, and **Ω** is angular velocity. The instrument directly displays the value in **cP or mPa·s**. **Advantages for Food Analysis (1 mark):** - Wide range of viscosities can be measured (1 cP to 2000 P) by changing spindles/speeds. - Suitable for **opaque, particulate, and non-Newtonian** food samples (sauces, batters, purees). - Continuous readout possible; small sample volume (~500 mL). - Temperature control jacket enables temperature-dependent studies. **Diagram (must be drawn; no separate marks but expected):** Sketch showing motor → spindle → sample cup → spring deflection → dial with proper labelling. --- **(b) Hagen–Poiseuille Derivation & Capillary Application — 7 marks** **Derivation (5 marks):** The full derivation is in **Section 3.1** above. Key steps to be written in the answer: - Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element: ΔP·πx² = −η·2πxL·(dv/dx). - Separation of variables: dv = −(ΔP/2ηL)·x·dx. - Integration from r to x: v = (ΔP/4ηL)(r² − x²). - Volume flow rate: Q = (π·r⁴·ΔP)/(8ηL). - Final result: η = (π·r⁴·ΔP·t) / (8·L·V). **Application in Capillary Viscometer (2 marks):** - An **Ostwald viscometer** has a U-tube with a precisely-bored capillary section and two bulbs (upper and lower). - A fixed volume **V** of liquid is drawn into the upper bulb; the time **t** to flow through the capillary under gravity is measured. - For a calibrated viscometer of constant **C**, the viscosity is: $$ \eta = C \cdot t \cdot \rho $$ where **ρ** is the density of the sample at the test temperature. - The constant **C** is determined by calibration with a liquid of known viscosity (often water). - Suitable for **Newtonian** food liquids such as refined oils, milk, sugar syrups, and clarified juices. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Define **viscoelasticity**. With suitable diagrams, describe the **Maxwell model** and explain how it characterizes the viscoelastic behaviour of food materials using oscillatory testing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is a **rheogram**? Classify food fluids based on their rheological behaviour, and explain the **Herschel–Bulkley model** with its significance in food processing. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Viscoelasticity & Maxwell Model — 7 marks** **Definition (1 mark):** Viscoelasticity is the property of a material that exhibits both **viscous (liquid-like, energy-dissipating)** and **elastic (solid-like, energy-storing)** behaviour when subjected to deformation. Most structured foods (gels, dough, cheese, melted chocolate) are viscoelastic. **Maxwell Model Construction (2 marks):** - The Maxwell model is a **spring (elastic element of modulus G)** and a **dashpot (viscous element of viscosity η)** connected in **series**. - A schematic (to be drawn): `τ → [Spring G] — [Dashpot η] → γ_total` - Under constant stress, the spring stretches instantaneously; the dashpot then extends linearly with time — this is **stress relaxation**. **Constitutive Equation (1 mark):** $$ \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{1}{G}\frac{d\tau}{dt} + \frac{\tau}{\eta} \quad \text{or} \quad \tau + \tau_{\text{rel}}\frac{d\tau}{dt} = \eta\frac{d\gamma}{dt} $$ where the **relaxation time** τ_rel = η/G is a characteristic material time constant. **Oscillatory Testing (3 marks):** - A small sinusoidal strain γ(t) = γ₀ sin(ωt) is applied at angular frequency **ω**. - The stress response **leads** the strain by a phase angle **δ** (0° for purely elastic solid, 90° for purely viscous liquid). - The complex modulus is split into two components: $$ G'(\omega) = G \cdot \frac{\omega^{2}\tau_{\text{rel}}^{2}}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau_{\text{rel}}^{2}} \quad ; \quad G''(\omega) = G \cdot \frac{\omega \tau_{\text{rel}}}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau_{\text{rel}}^{2}} $$ - **G′** (storage modulus) → elastic energy stored; **G″** (loss modulus) → viscous energy dissipated. - **tan δ = G″/G′** indicates whether the food is solid-like (tan δ < 1) or liquid-like (tan δ > 1). - The **crossover point** (G′ = G″) marks the **gel point** during gelation of pectin, starch, or protein systems. --- **(b) Rheogram & Herschel–Bulkley Model — 7 marks** **Rheogram Definition (1 mark):** A rheogram is a **plot of shear stress (τ) versus shear rate (γ̇)** for a fluid. The shape of this curve classifies the fluid and provides its rheological constants (η, K, n, τ₀). **Classification of Food Fluids (3 marks):** - **Newtonian** (τ vs γ̇ is linear, passes through origin) — water, milk, dilute sugar solutions. - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** (curve concave to shear-rate axis, no yield stress) — ketchup, fruit juice concentrates, xanthan gum solutions. - **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** (curve convex to shear-rate axis) — concentrated starch suspensions, wet sand. - **Bingham Plastic** (linear with non-zero yield stress) — margarine, toothpaste, mayonnaise (approx). - **Yield-pseudoplastic / Herschel–Bulkley fluid** (curved, with yield stress) — tomato paste, molten chocolate at processing temperatures. - **Thixotropic** (hysteresis loop; viscosity decreases with time at constant shear) — yogurt, some salad dressings. **Herschel–Bulkley Model (2 marks):** $$ \tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n} $$ - **τ₀** (yield stress): minimum stress required to initiate flow — important for products that must hold their shape (ketchup in a bottle, chocolate coating). - **K** (consistency index): measure of viscosity magnitude. - **n** (flow behaviour index): n < 1 → shear-thinning; n = 1 → Bingham; n > 1 → shear-thickening. **Significance in Food Processing (1 mark):** - Used for **pipe-line design**: pressure drop = f(τ₀, K, n) in tomato paste, chocolate, and dairy concentrate transport. - Predicts **mixing and pumping energy**; critical for CIP (clean-in-place) operations. - Determines **sensory mouthfeel** through shear-rate-dependent viscosity in the oral cavity. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Mixing up G′ and G″** — Remember: G′ = elastic (storage); G″ = viscous (loss). Examiners deduct heavily for this swap. > 2. **Forgetting the density factor in capillary viscometer formula** — η = C·t·**ρ** (not just C·t). Capillary viscometer readings must be density-corrected. > 3. **Drawing Maxwell model with spring and dashpot in parallel** — that is the **Kelvin–Voigt model**, not Maxwell. They are not interchangeable. KTU questions explicitly distinguish them. > 4. **Stating viscosity without units** — Always write **Pa·s (or cP)**. Numerical answers without units carry zero credit. > 5. **Not specifying shear rate** in Brookfield readings — Viscosity of a non-Newtonian food is meaningless without the corresponding **rpm/shear rate** at which it was measured. > 6. **Confusing "thixotropy" with "pseudoplasticity"** — Pseudoplasticity is **shear-rate dependent (instantaneous)**, thixotropy is **time-dependent** (viscosity drops over time even at constant shear rate). This distinction is a favourite KTU 14-mark question hook. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Newton's law of viscosity**: τ = η · γ̇ defines **Newtonian** behaviour (water, milk, oils). - [ ] **Dynamic viscosity η (Pa·s)** vs **kinematic viscosity ν (m²/s)**: ν = η/ρ. - [ ] **1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP**; water at 20 °C ≈ 1 cP. - [ ] **Non-Newtonian fluids** are classified into time-independent (pseudoplastic, dilatant, Bingham), time-dependent (thixotropic, rheopectic), and **viscoelastic**. - [ ] **Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele):** τ = K · γ̇ⁿ. n < 1 → shear-thinning (ketchup); n > 1 → shear-thickening (starch). - [ ] **Herschel–Bulkley:** τ = τ₀ + K · γ̇ⁿ — most realistic model for **food pastes and chocolate**. - [ ] **Viscoelasticity** is characterized by **G′ (elastic)** and **G″ (viscous)** in **oscillatory testing**. - [ ] **tan δ = G″/G′**: < 1 → solid-like gel; > 1 → liquid-like. - [ ] **Maxwell model** = spring + dashpot in **series**; **Kelvin–Voigt** = in **parallel**. Do not confuse. - [ ] **Capillary viscometer** (Ostwald) uses Hagen–Poiseuille: η = πr⁴ΔPt / 8LV. Best for clear Newtonian liquids. - [ ] **Rotational viscometer** (Brookfield, cone–plate) measures torque on a rotating bob — best for opaque, non-Newtonian, particulate foods. - [ ] **Falling-ball viscometer** uses Stokes' law: η = 2r²g(ρ_s − ρ_l)t / 9L — best for low-viscosity beverages. - [ ] **Oscillatory rheometer** is the gold standard for modern viscoelastic food characterization (gels, doughs, biopolymers). - [ ] **Shear rate is not optional** — always report viscosity *with* the corresponding shear rate (rpm) for non-Newtonian foods. - [ ] **Industrial relevance**: pipe sizing, pump selection, sensory texture, shelf-life prediction, formulation R&D. - [ ] **Famous food examples**: ketchup (pseudoplastic), chocolate (Casson/Bingham), yogurt (thixotropic), set jelly (viscoelastic gel), cornstarch slurry (dilatant).

topics.title: Texture Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT613 - INSTRUMENTAL METHODS IN FOOD ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Texture Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Texture Analysis

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Texture** is formally defined by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO 11036:1994 / reaffirmed in KTU 2024 module framework) as: *“All the mechanical, geometrical, surface and body attributes of a product perceptible by means of mechanical, tactile, visual, and auditory receptors.”*

In the context of **Instrumental Methods in Food Analysis**, *texture analysis* refers to the quantitative measurement of the **structural and mechanical properties** of a food matrix using a precisely calibrated **Texture Analyzer** (e.g., TA.XT Plus, Instron Universal Testing Machine, Stevens LFRA). The output is expressed as a **Force–Time (F-t) curve** or **Force–Distance (F-d) curve** that is mathematically reduced to standardized rheological/textural parameters.

The five recognized primary textural attributes (per **Szczesniak classification, 1963** – still the KTU 2024 baseline) are:

1. **Hardness** – force required to deform/compress
2. **Cohesiveness** – strength of internal bonds
3. **Adhesiveness** – work required to overcome adhesive forces
4. **Springiness (Elasticity)** – rate of recovery after deformation
5. **Viscosity** – flow rate under force

Secondary characteristics (derived from primary) include **Fracturability, Gumminess, Chewiness**, and **Firmness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 highlight:** Texture is a **multi-parameter attribute** — it cannot be represented by a single number. A complete texture profile must always quote a **minimum of 5 TPA parameters** as defined by Bourne (1978, 1982).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of biting into a **crunchy roasted peanut** versus a **soft, chewy caramel**:

- The peanut **resists** your tooth strongly, makes a sharp **crack**, and shatters quickly.
- The caramel **yields** gradually, sticks to your tooth (pulls a string), and slowly returns to shape.

A **Texture Analyzer** simply *imitates* your teeth and jaw with a steel probe moving at a controlled speed. It records **how much force (Newtons) the food pushes back** as the probe bites into it, and **how far it travels (mm)**, and **how much energy** is spent in the process.

**Real-world analogy:**
> Imagine pressing your thumb firmly onto a soft marshmallow vs. a raw carrot. Your thumb is the **probe**, the force your thumb feels is the **resistance force (F)**, the depth your thumb sinks is the **distance (d)**, and the work (F × d) is the **area under the Force–Distance curve**. That is essentially what a texture analyzer does — only with millinewton precision, controlled speed, and reproducible geometry.

The analyzer then auto-computes textural parameters by integrating the area under the curve, identifying peak forces, and measuring recovery distances.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Their Units (KTU Board Significance)

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Engineering Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force | $F$ | **Newton (N)** | $\pm 0.001$ N |
| Distance / Deformation | $d$ | **Millimeter (mm)** | $\pm 0.01$ mm |
| Time | $t$ | **Second (s)** | $\pm 0.001$ s |
| Cross-head speed | $v$ | **mm/s** | $\pm 0.01$ mm/s |
| Work / Energy | $W$ | **Millijoule (mJ)** | $\pm 0.01$ mJ |
| Strain rate | $\dot{\varepsilon}$ | **s⁻¹** | $\pm 0.001$ s⁻¹ |
| Young’s Modulus (for solids) | $E$ | **Pascal (Pa)** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Always write **SI units** in the examination. Avoid CGS (dyne, erg) unless explicitly asked. Hardness is in **N**, not kgf (kilogram-force is a deprecated non-SI unit).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Texture Tests (Operational Categories)

| Test Type | Probe Geometry | Typical Food | Measured Attribute |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TPA (Texture Profile Analysis)** | Cylindrical, Flat, 50.8 mm | Bread, Cheese, Gel, Fruits | Hardness, Springiness, Cohesiveness |
| **Penetration Test** | Cylindrical (≤ 6 mm) | Fruits, Vegetables | Firmness, Pulp strength |
| **Puncture Test** | Sharp needle / conical | Skin of sausages, Tomato peel | Skin strength |
| **Cutting / Shear Test** | Wire shear, Kramer Shear Cell | Meat, Bakery, Vegetables | Shear force, Toughness |
| **Three-Point Bend Test** | 2 supports + 1 load | Biscuits, Extrudates | Flexural strength, Brittleness |
| **Extrusion / Back Extrusion** | Closed cell, Piston | Sauces, Gels, Yogurt | Consistency, Viscosity index |
| **Adhesion Test (Tack Test)** | Flat disc, pull-away | Honey, Cheese spread | Stickiness, Work of adhesion |
| **Tensile Test** | Tensile grips | Films, Chewing gum | Tensile strength, Elongation at break |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept of Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)

Texture Profile Analysis (TPA), developed by **Bourne (1978)**, is the most widely accepted KTU-evaluable instrumental method. It mimics the **two-bite mastication cycle** of human chewing using a **two-compression cycle** (down-up-down-up) on a stationary sample.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of TPA

1. **Sample Preparation**
   - Standardize sample geometry (cube, cylinder) with surface area $A \geq 1\,\text{cm}^2$ and height $h \geq 10\,\text{mm}$.
   - Equilibrate to a defined temperature (typically $20 \pm 2^{\circ}\text{C}$ for solid foods; $4 \pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ for dairy/ice cream).

2. **Probe Selection & Calibration**
   - Select probe diameter (commonly **50.8 mm cylindrical flat probe** for general TPA).
   - Calibrate load cell (typically 5 kg, 25 kg, or 50 kg range depending on product hardness).
   - Set **cross-head speed** = $1.0\,\text{mm/s}$ (most common for bread, cheese, gels).

3. **Pre-Test (Trigger Force)**
   - Apply a small initial contact force (e.g., **0.05 N**) to detect the sample surface (auto-zero the displacement).

4. **First Compression Stroke (Bite 1)**
   - Probe moves down at constant speed until it compresses the sample to a preset strain (commonly **25–75 %** of original height for TPA).

5. **Return Stroke**
   - Probe retracts to its original position at the same speed.

6. **Hold / Wait Time (Bite Separation)**
   - Pause typically **2–5 seconds** (varies by product) to allow **elastic recovery**.

7. **Second Compression Stroke (Bite 2)**
   - Probe descends again with the same parameters.

8. **Data Acquisition & Curve Generation**
   - Software plots **Force (N) vs. Time (s)** OR **Force (N) vs. Distance (mm)**.
   - All textural parameters are extracted from the curve geometry and integrals.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Reduction of the Force–Time Curve

The TPA parameters are computed as follows. The naming convention follows the **Bourne (1982) standardization**, which is the KTU 2024 reference.

Let:
- $F_1$ = Peak force during first compression
- $F_2$ = Peak force during second compression
- $A_1$ = Positive area under first compression curve
- $A_2$ = Positive area under second compression curve
- $A_3$ = Negative area during withdrawal (between compressions, the trough)
- $d_1$ = Distance to peak force in first compression
- $d_2$ = Distance traveled during second compression
- $d_{recovery}$ = Distance recovered after first compression
- $L_0$ = Original sample length/height

### 2.2.1 Primary TPA Parameters (with formulas)

| # | Parameter | Formula (Symbolic) | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hardness** | $H = F_1$ | Peak force on first compression |
| 2 | **Fracturability** | $F_{frac} = F_{first\text{ peak}}$ (if a distinct break occurs before $F_1$) | Force at first significant fracture |
| 3 | **Cohesiveness** | $C = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Ratio of second to first compression positive areas |
| 4 | **Adhesiveness** | $\text{Adh} = A_3$ (magnitude of negative area) | Work to pull probe off sample |
| 5 | **Springiness** | $S = \dfrac{d_{recovery}}{L_0}$ OR $S = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1}$ (instrument-dependent) | Elastic recovery ratio |
| 6 | **Gumminess** | $G = H \times C$ | Hardness × Cohesiveness (used for semi-solids) |
| 7 | **Chewiness** | $Ch = H \times C \times S$ | Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness (used for solids) |
| 8 | **Resilience** | $R = \dfrac{A_{1\text{-upstroke}}}{A_{1\text{-downstroke}}}$ | Instantaneous recoverability |

### 2.2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table — Pipe-Free Notation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markdown rendering safeguard:** All absolute-value / vertical bar notation has been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` to prevent table-format breakage in KTU 2024 PDF exports.

| Parameter | Engineering Formula | SI Unit | Boundary Condition / Range | Applicable Food Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness | $H = \max \vert F(t) \vert_{\text{1st bite}}$ | N | $0.05 \le H \le 50$ | All |
| Fracturability | $F_{frac} = F_{\text{first peak}}$ | N | $0 \le F_{frac} \le H$ | Crispy / Crumbly |
| Cohesiveness | $C = A_2 / A_1$ | dimensionless | $0 \le C \le 1$ | All |
| Adhesiveness | $\text{Adh} = \vert A_3 \vert$ | mJ | $0 \le \text{Adh} \le 5$ | Sticky foods |
| Springiness | $S = d_{recovery} / L_0$ | dimensionless | $0 \le S \le 1$ | Elastic solids |
| Gumminess | $G = H \cdot C$ | N | — | Semi-solids (cheese, jam) |
| Chewiness | $Ch = H \cdot C \cdot S$ | mJ | — | Solid foods (meat, bread) |
| Resilience | $R = A_{\text{up},1} / A_{\text{down},1}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le R \le 1$ | All |
| Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ | All |
| Stress | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Pa | — | All |
| Young’s Modulus | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (linear region) | Pa | $10^3$ (gel) – $10^7$ (hard biscuit) | Solid foods |
| Shear Stress | $\tau = F / A_{\text{shear}}$ | Pa | — | Meat, Vegetables |
| Work of Penetration | $W = \int_{0}^{d_{max}} F(d)\, dd$ | mJ | — | Fruits, Vegetables |
| Cross-head Speed | $v = d / t$ | mm/s | $0.1 \le v \le 10$ | All |
| Strain Rate | $\dot{\varepsilon} = v / L_0$ | s⁻¹ | $0.01 \le \dot{\varepsilon} \le 5$ | All |

---

## 2.3 The Force–Time Curve Anatomy (Engineering Interpretation)

The TPA force-time curve is divided into distinct mechanical phases:

1. **Loading Phase (1st Compression Down-stroke)**
   - Probe approaches sample → trigger force detected → curve begins at $t = 0$, $F = 0.05$ N.
   - As strain increases, force rises.
   - Peak $F_1$ = Hardness (or Fracturability if a yield occurs first).
   - Area under this section = $A_1$ (Work of 1st compression).

2. **Unloading Phase (1st Up-stroke)**
   - Probe retracts.
   - In sticky foods, the curve dips **below the zero-force baseline** → this trough area is $A_3$ = Adhesiveness.

3. **Recovery Phase (Hold Time)**
   - Time-based elastic recovery; sample rebounds partially.
   - The distance recovered vs. total distance = Springiness.

4. **Loading Phase (2nd Compression Down-stroke)**
   - Probe descends again.
   - New peak $F_2 \le F_1$ (always, in normal TPA).
   - Area under this section = $A_2$ → Cohesiveness $= A_2 / A_1$.

5. **Unloading Phase (2nd Up-stroke)**
   - Final retraction; curve returns to baseline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** If $F_2 > F_1$, it indicates **strain hardening** (e.g., dense crystalline gel, aged cheese) — this is an *advanced* TPA interpretation, beyond KTU 2024 Module 4 base syllabus but useful for higher-marks analytical answers.

---

## 2.4 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

**Why these particular parameters?**
Because they **map directly** to the human perception of texture as described by Szczesniak (1963). The mechanical, geometric, and surface attributes perceived by a human taster are each approximated by one of these derived quantities.

**How the curve yields them?**
- **Force peaks** → Resistance to deformation.
- **Area under curve** → Total work done (energy absorbed).
- **Curve shape (rounded vs. jagged)** → Fracturability, brittleness.
- **Negative area** → Adhesion (stickiness).
- **Recovery distance / total distance** → Elasticity (springiness).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

| Industry | TPA Application | Quality Control Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Bakery** | Bread crumb hardness, springiness | Predicts staling rate |
| **Dairy** | Cheese firmness, gumminess | Monitors ripening stage |
| **Meat** | Chewiness, shear force | Correlates with Warner-Bratzler shear |
| **Confectionery** | Jelly/gummy chewiness | Optimizes recipe pectin/gelatin |
| **Fruits & Vegetables** | Pulp firmness, skin puncture | Predicts harvest maturity & shelf life |
| **Snack Foods** | Crispness (acoustic + TPA) | Controls extrusion parameters |
| **Sauces / Dressings** | Back-extrusion consistency | Detects phase separation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board note:** When asked "Discuss the importance of texture analysis in food quality," mention at least **three specific industry examples** with the **parameter** and **benefit** linked together. Listing only "QA/QC" without specifics is worth partial credit only.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stress–Strain Relationship from a Typical Compression Curve

The fundamental textural engineering equation in compression testing is **Hooke's Law** in its generalized form. We derive the relationship between applied force and sample response.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions

Consider a cylindrical food sample of:
- Original height: $L_0$ (m)
- Cross-sectional area: $A_0$ (m²)
- Cross-head speed: $v$ (m/s)

When the probe compresses the sample by a distance $\Delta L$, the following definitions apply.

**Engineering Strain** ($\varepsilon$):

The ratio of deformation to original length — a dimensionless number that quantifies the relative change in shape.

**Engineering Stress** ($\sigma$):

The force distributed over the original cross-sectional area. We use original area $A_0$ (not instantaneous area) because food samples are not perfectly incompressible in compression.

**Cross-head displacement**:

Since the probe moves at constant speed $v$ and the test runs for time $t$, the distance moved equals speed multiplied by time.

### 3.1.2 Full LaTeX-Aligned Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given: cylindrical sample, original height } L_0,\ \text{area } A_0. \\[6pt]
&\text{Probe compresses the sample by distance } \Delta L. \\[6pt]
&\text{By definition of engineering strain (ratio of deformation to original length):} \\[6pt]
&\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad\quad \text{(dimensionless)} \\[10pt]
&\text{By definition of engineering stress (force per unit original area):} \\[6pt]
&\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} \quad\quad \text{(Pa)} \\[10pt]
&\text{For a constant cross-head speed } v \text{ and time } t: \\[6pt]
&\Delta L = v \cdot t \\[10pt]
&\text{Substituting into the strain equation:} \\[6pt]
&\varepsilon(t) = \frac{v \cdot t}{L_0} \\[10pt]
&\text{Therefore, the strain rate (rate of deformation) becomes:} \\[6pt]
&\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{v}{L_0} \quad\quad \text{(s}^{-1}\text{)} \\[10pt]
&\text{If the force response } F \text{ is measured at time } t, \text{ then stress is:} \\[6pt]
&\sigma(t) = \frac{F(t)}{A_0} \\[10pt]
&\text{Hence the constitutive stress–strain relation is:} \\[6pt]
&\sigma(\varepsilon) = \frac{F(\varepsilon)}{A_0} \quad \text{plotted vs. } \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the **Work of Penetration** (Area Under Force-Distance Curve)

The total mechanical work done by the probe on the food during compression is the **integral of force over distance**.

### 3.2.1 Why This Is Important

Work of penetration is the **fundamental energy measure** in texture analysis. It tells you how much energy a person would need to bite through the food — directly correlating with **perceived hardness/effort**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Elementary work done by force } F \text{ over infinitesimal distance } dd: \\[6pt]
&dW = F \cdot dd \\[10pt]
&\text{Total work from 0 to maximum penetration depth } d_{max}: \\[6pt]
&W = \int_{0}^{d_{max}} F(d)\, dd \\[10pt]
&\text{Discretizing for an N-point force-distance data set (numerical form):} \\[6pt]
&W \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \left[\frac{F_i + F_{i+1}}{2}\right] \cdot (d_{i+1} - d_i) \\[10pt]
&\text{This is the trapezoidal rule — exactly how the texture analyzer software calculates area.} \\[10pt]
&\text{For a linearly elastic sample where } F(d) = k \cdot d \text{ (Hookean behavior, } k = \text{ stiffness in N/m):} \\[6pt]
&W = \int_{0}^{d_{max}} (k \cdot d)\, dd = \frac{k \cdot d_{max}^{2}}{2} \\[10pt]
&\text{Since } k = \frac{F_{max}}{d_{max}}, \\[6pt]
&W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{F_{max}}{d_{max}} \cdot d_{max}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F_{max} \cdot d_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 exam tip:** The **½ × Force × Distance** formula is valid **only for purely linear elastic response**. For plastic/viscoelastic foods (chocolate, cheese), use the full **trapezoidal integration** over the actual curve.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of **Young's Modulus** (E) from the Linear Region of the Curve

In the **initial linear-elastic region** of the stress–strain curve (typically $\varepsilon \le 0.02$ for hard biscuits, up to $\varepsilon \le 0.10$ for soft gels), the slope gives Young's Modulus $E$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \bigg|_{\text{linear region}} \\[8pt]
&\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}, \quad \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \\[8pt]
&\Rightarrow E = \frac{F/A_0}{\Delta L/L_0} = \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example for KTU Board Practice:**

> A cylindrical bread sample has $L_0 = 30\,\text{mm}$, $A_0 = 19.63\,\text{cm}^2 = 19.63 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$. At $\Delta L = 0.5\,\text{mm}$, the force is $F = 1.2\,\text{N}$. Calculate Young's Modulus.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\varepsilon = \frac{0.5}{30} = 0.01667 \\[6pt]
&\sigma = \frac{1.2}{19.63 \times 10^{-4}} = 611.3\,\text{Pa} \\[6pt]
&E = \frac{611.3}{0.01667} = 36{,}682\,\text{Pa} \approx 36.7\,\text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a typical value for **soft white bread crumb** — full KTU 2024 exam standard numerical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — TPA Parameter Derivation from Curve Areas

The following matrix provides the complete mathematical reduction logic for KTU 2024 examinations. Each parameter is traceable to a measurable curve feature.

| TPA Parameter | Curve Region Used | Mathematical Operation | Typical Range (Food) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | First compression peak | $\max[F(t)]$ over $0 \le t \le t_1$ | $0.5$–$15$ N (bread) |
| **Cohesiveness** | Positive areas of two compressions | $A_2 \div A_1$ | $0.3$–$0.8$ |
| **Adhesiveness** | Negative area between cycles | $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vert F(t) \vert\, dt$ | $0$–$3$ mJ |
| **Springiness** | Distance recovered in hold time | $(d_{\text{recover}}) \div d_1$ | $0.4$–$0.95$ |
| **Gumminess** | Product of two | $H \times C$ | $0.2$–$10$ N (cheese) |
| **Chewiness** | Triple product | $H \times C \times S$ | $1$–$50$ mJ (meat) |
| **Resilience** | Up-stroke / Down-stroke of 1st cycle | $A_{\text{up},1} \div A_{\text{down},1}$ | $0.2$–$0.7$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Style, 14-Mark Format)

**Problem:**
> A TPA test on cheese is performed with a 50.8 mm flat probe at 1.0 mm/s cross-head speed, 50% strain. The original sample height is $L_0 = 20\,\text{mm}$. The following data are extracted from the force-time curve:
> - $F_1 = 12.5\,\text{N}$ (first peak)
> - $A_1 = 78.3\,\text{mJ}$
> - $A_2 = 56.4\,\text{mJ}$
> - $A_3 = -2.1\,\text{mJ}$ (negative area, adhesiveness)
> - $d_1 = 9.8\,\text{mm}$ (distance at first peak)
> - Distance recovered during the 2-second hold = $6.2\,\text{mm}$
> - $A_{\text{up},1} = 8.9\,\text{mJ}$ (area on upstroke of 1st compression)
>
> Calculate: **(a)** Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience. **(b)** Comment on the texture of this cheese.

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Parameter Calculations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Hardness:} \quad H = F_1 = 12.5\,\text{N} \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Cohesiveness:} \quad C = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{56.4}{78.3} = 0.720 \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Adhesiveness:} \quad \text{Adh} = \vert A_3 \vert = 2.1\,\text{mJ} \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Springiness:} \quad S = \frac{d_{\text{recover}}}{d_1} = \frac{6.2}{9.8} = 0.633 \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Gumminess:} \quad G = H \times C = 12.5 \times 0.720 = 9.0\,\text{N} \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Chewiness:} \quad Ch = H \times C \times S = 12.5 \times 0.720 \times 0.633 = 5.70\,\text{mJ} \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Resilience:} \quad R = \frac{A_{\text{up},1}}{A_1} = \frac{8.9}{78.3} = 0.114
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Interpretation**

> - The cheese has **high hardness (12.5 N)** → it is **firm** (e.g., aged Cheddar or Gouda class).
> - **Cohesiveness of 0.72** indicates strong internal bonds; cheese does not crumble easily.
> - **Moderate adhesiveness (2.1 mJ)** → slight stickiness to the probe, typical of semi-soft ripened cheese.
> - **Springiness of 0.63** indicates fair elastic recovery — cheese is not brittle.
> - **High gumminess (9.0 N)** classifies it as **semi-solid, dense, and chewy**.
> - **Low resilience (0.114)** suggests that most of the deformation energy is dissipated (plastic behavior dominates).

**Final verdict:** This is a **firm, cohesive, semi-elastic aged cheese** suitable for slicing/grating — not a fresh soft cheese.

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — TPA Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Preparation<br/>Standardize geometry and temperature] --> B[Probe Selection and Calibration<br/>Load cell verification]
    B --> C[Set Test Parameters<br/>Speed = 1 mm/s<br/>Strain = 25 to 75 percent]
    C --> D[Trigger Force Detection<br/>F trigger = 0.05 N]
    D --> E[First Compression Downstroke<br/>Bite 1 simulation]
    E --> F[Record Peak Force F1<br/>and Area A1]
    F --> G[Upstroke Begins<br/>Probe retraction]
    G --> H{Is Food Sticky?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Record Negative Area A3<br/>Adhesiveness measurement]
    H -- No --> J[No negative area recorded]
    I --> K[Hold Time 2 to 5 s<br/>Elastic recovery]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Record Recovery Distance d recover]
    L --> M[Second Compression Downstroke<br/>Bite 2 simulation]
    M --> N[Record Peak Force F2<br/>and Area A2]
    N --> O[Final Upstroke<br/>Probe returns to start]
    O --> P[Compute TPA Parameters<br/>Hardness, Cohesiveness,<br/>Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness]
    P --> Q[Generate Force versus Time Plot]
    Q --> R[End of Test]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Texture Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        CPU[Microcontroller and DAQ]
        LC[Load Cell 5 to 50 kg]
        ENC[Optical Encoder]
    end

    subgraph MECHANICAL[Motion Subsystem]
        MOT[Stepper or Servo Motor]
        DRV[Motor Drive Electronics]
        LEAD[Lead Screw or Ball Screw]
        PROBE[Probe Attachment]
    end

    subgraph SAMPLE[Sample Stage]
        STAGE[Fixed Sample Platform]
    end

    subgraph SOFTWARE[Software Subsystem]
        PC[Host PC with Texture Pro Software]
        DISP[Real Time Force vs Time Display]
        STORE[Data Storage and Export]
        ANA[Parameter Calculator Module]
    end

    LC -->|Force signal in mV| CPU
    ENC -->|Position pulses| CPU
    CPU -->|Control commands| DRV
    DRV -->|PWM or step pulses| MOT
    MOT -->|Rotational motion| LEAD
    LEAD -->|Linear translation| PROBE
    PROBE -->|Compresses| STAGE
    STAGE -->|Reaction force| LC
    CPU -->|Digital data stream| PC
    PC --> DISP
    PC --> STORE
    PC --> ANA
    ANA -->|Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Adhesiveness, Resilience| DISP

    style CPU fill:#FFE08A
    style PROBE fill:#A8D5BA
    style ANA fill:#C8E6C9
    style STAGE fill:#A8D5BA
```

> **Subsystem roles:**
> - **Input Subsystem:** Captures force (load cell) and position (encoder) signals.
> - **Motion Subsystem:** Drives the probe at constant cross-head speed.
> - **Sample Stage:** Holds the food stationary.
> - **Software Subsystem:** Plots curves, runs the trapezoidal integration, and outputs TPA parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[First Upstroke Begins<br/>F decreases]
```
