# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Texture Profile Analysis (TPA). List any four textural parameters determined by TPA.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** is an instrumental double-compression test, developed by Bourne (1978), that imitates the two-bite mastication cycle of human chewing to quantitatively evaluate the mechanical and geometrical attributes of solid and semi-solid foods. The test is conducted using a calibrated texture analyzer fitted with an appropriate probe (commonly a 50.8 mm flat cylindrical probe for general foods), operated at a defined cross-head speed (typically 1 mm/s) and a defined strain (25–75%).
>
> **Four textural parameters determined by TPA:**
> 1. **Hardness** – the peak force on the first compression cycle.
> 2. **Cohesiveness** – the ratio of the positive area under the second compression curve to that of the first ($A_2 / A_1$).
> 3. **Springiness** – the ratio of recovered distance to original deformation distance after the first bite.
> 4. **Chewiness** – the product of hardness, cohesiveness, and springiness ($H \times C \times S$), representing the energy required to masticate a solid food to a state ready for swallowing.

**Mark distribution (as per KTU 2024 valuation key):**
| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Definition of TPA | 1 |
| Mention of double-compression principle | 1 |
| Any 4 parameters with one-line definition | 1 |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Hardness and Fracturability in texture analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hardness** is defined as the **peak force** recorded during the first compression of a TPA test. It represents the maximum resistance offered by the food sample to the compressing probe. Mathematically, $H = F_1$ (in Newtons). Hardness is a **mandatory parameter** in every TPA run, irrespective of whether the food fractures or not.
>
> **Fracturability** (also called **brittleness**) is defined as the **force at the first significant break or crack** in the force–time curve, and is meaningful **only for foods that exhibit a clear fracture event** (e.g., biscuits, carrots, cheese with a rind, extruded snacks). It is identified as the **first peak in the curve that is preceded by an abrupt drop in force**. If $F_{frac} < F_1$, the food is considered to fracture *before* reaching its maximum hardness.
>
> **Key distinction:** Hardness is a **mandatory peak force**; Fracturability is an **optional break-point force** applicable only to brittle/fracturing foods. In elastic foods (e.g., gelatin, bread crumb), $F_{frac}$ may equal $F_1$ or may be undefined (no fracture), while hardness is always defined.

**Mark distribution:**
| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Definition of Hardness + units | 1 |
| Definition of Fracturability + condition for occurrence | 1 |
| Key distinction between the two | 1 |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **one** of the two internal-choice options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** method in detail. Explain the construction and interpretation of a typical **force–time curve** obtained in TPA, clearly marking all key parameters and areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TPA test on a **cheddar cheese sample** ($L_0 = 25\,\text{mm}$, $A_0 = 19.63 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$) gives the following data: $F_1 = 14.8\,\text{N}$, $A_1 = 92.4\,\text{mJ}$, $A_2 = 64.1\,\text{mJ}$, $A_3 = -3.2\,\text{mJ}$, distance to first peak $d_1 = 12.5\,\text{mm}$, recovery distance $d_{recovery} = 8.1\,\text{mm}$. Calculate **Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, and Chewiness**, and comment on the cheese quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a):

> **TPA — Detailed Description:**
> TPA is a **two-bite instrumental simulation** of human mastication, developed by **Malcolm C. Bourne (Cornell University, 1978)**. It uses a **texture analyzer** equipped with a flat cylindrical probe (commonly 50.8 mm diameter) and a load cell.
>
> **Procedure:**
> 1. The sample is **shaped into a regular geometry** (cube or cylinder) of at least 1 cm² cross-section and 10 mm height, and **equilibrated** to a defined test temperature (e.g., $20 \pm 2^{\circ}\text{C}$).
> 2. The probe is brought into contact with the sample surface at a **trigger force of 0.05 N** (to auto-zero the displacement).
> 3. The probe descends at a **constant cross-head speed of 1.0 mm/s** for the **first compression**, compressing the sample to a preset strain (typically 25–75 % of $L_0$).
> 4. The probe retracts at the same speed. For **sticky foods**, the curve dips below the baseline, recording the **adhesiveness area** ($A_3$).
> 5. A **2–5 second hold** allows the sample to recover elastically.
> 6. A **second compression** is performed under identical conditions.
> 7. The instrument records the **force (N) vs. time (s)** and **force vs. distance (mm)** data.
>
> **Force–Time Curve Interpretation:**
> - The **peak force on the first compression** is the **Hardness (H)**.
> - The **positive area under the first compression curve** is the **work of the first bite ($A_1$)**.
> - The **negative area** (between compressions) gives **Adhesiveness ($A_3$)**.
> - The **distance the sample recovers** during the hold time (relative to $d_1$) gives **Springiness (S)**.
> - The **positive area under the second compression curve** is the **work of the second bite ($A_2$)**.
> - **Cohesiveness** is computed as $C = A_2 / A_1$.
> - **Gumminess** $= H \times C$ (for semi-solids).
> - **Chewiness** $= H \times C \times S$ (for solids).
> - **Resilience** is the ratio of the upstroke area to the downstroke area of the first compression.

#### Model Solution (Part b):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Hardness:} \quad H = F_1 = 14.8\,\text{N} \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Cohesiveness:} \quad C = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{64.1}{92.4} = 0.694 \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Adhesiveness:} \quad \text{Adh} = \vert A_3 \vert = 3.2\,\text{mJ} \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Springiness:} \quad S = \frac{d_{\text{recover}}}{d_1} = \frac{8.1}{12.5} = 0.648 \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Gumminess:} \quad G = H \times C = 14.8 \times 0.694 = 10.27\,\text{N} \\[8pt]
&\textbf{Chewiness:} \quad Ch = H \times C \times S = 14.8 \times 0.694 \times 0.648 = 6.66\,\text{mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comment on cheese quality:**
> - The cheese is **firm (H = 14.8 N)** and **cohesive (C = 0.694)** — internal bonds are strong, indicating a **mature aged Cheddar**.
> - **Moderate stickiness (Adh = 3.2 mJ)** is consistent with fat-rich, semi-soft cheese.
> - **Springiness of 0.648** indicates good elastic recovery; cheese is not brittle.
> - **High gumminess (10.27 N)** and **moderate chewiness (6.66 mJ)** classify it as a **semi-solid, chewy cheese** suitable for slicing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **instrumentation and operating principle** of a **Texture Analyzer** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **load cell, cross-head, probe**, and **software** in texture measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Adhesiveness** in texture analysis? A jam sample is tested using a 12.7 mm flat probe at 0.5 mm/s. The negative area under the force–time curve between two compressions is found to be **$A_3 = 4.8\,\text{mJ}$**. If the sample was compressed by 8 mm in each cycle, calculate the **average adhesive force** exerted by the jam on the probe. Comment on the **practical significance** of this value in jam quality evaluation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a):

> **Instrumentation of a Texture Analyzer:**
> A modern texture analyzer (e.g., **TA.XT Plus, Stable Micro Systems; Instron 3343**) consists of the following functional subsystems:
>
> 1. **Load Cell** – The force-sensing element (commonly of capacity 5 kg, 25 kg, or 50 kg) that converts the mechanical reaction force from the sample into a proportional electrical signal (mV/V), which is digitized by the data acquisition system. Higher-capacity cells are used for hard foods (meat, biscuits), lower-capacity cells for soft gels.
>
> 2. **Cross-Head and Drive System** – A **stepper motor or servo motor** drives a **lead screw or ball screw** to translate the probe vertically at a precisely controlled speed (0.01 to 10 mm/s). The cross-head speed is the primary experimental variable.
>
> 3. **Probe** – The contact element whose geometry determines the type of test (flat cylinder for TPA, conical for puncture, knife for cutting). It is rigidly fixed to the moving cross-head.
>
> 4. **Sample Platform** – A stationary platform (often with a slotted base for back-extrusion) on which the sample rests.
>
> 5. **Data Acquisition System (DAQ)** – Samples the load-cell output at high frequency (typically 500 Hz) and synchronizes it with the encoder reading to produce the force-time/force-distance plot.
>
> 6. **Software** – Controls test parameters, plots curves in real time, and applies the **trapezoidal integration** algorithm to compute all TPA parameters automatically.
>
> **Operating Principle:**
> The probe descends at a programmed speed and compresses the sample. The reaction force is sensed by the load cell. The software plots the force curve and integrates areas to derive the seven TPA parameters. The system is **closed-loop** for force, position, and speed.

#### Model Solution (Part b):

> **Adhesiveness** in texture analysis is defined as the **work (energy) required to overcome the attractive forces between the food surface and the probe** during the upstroke phase of a TPA test, after the probe has been in contact with the food. It is represented by the **negative area** ($A_3$) of the force–time curve between two compression cycles. Mathematically, $\text{Adh} = \int_{t_{\text{upstroke}}} F(t)\, dt$ (in mJ). Adhesiveness is a key quality attribute for **sticky foods** such as jam, honey, cheese spreads, caramel, and taffy.
>
> **Average Adhesive Force Calculation:**
> The work done against adhesion is the negative area under the force-time curve over the distance traveled by the probe during the upstroke.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Work} = A_3 = 4.8\,\text{mJ} = 4.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{J} \\[6pt]
&\text{Distance traveled during upstroke} = d = 8\,\text{mm} = 8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \\[6pt]
&\text{Average Force} = \frac{\text{Work}}{\text{Distance}} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.6\,\text{N} \\[8pt]
&\boxed{F_{\text{avg,adh}} = 0.6\,\text{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Practical Significance:**
> - An average adhesive force of **0.6 N** indicates that the jam exhibits **moderate stickiness** to the probe, characteristic of a **well-set jam** with a balanced pectin–sugar–acid ratio.
> - **Higher values (> 1.0 N)** suggest the jam is **too sticky or under-set** (excess sugar, low pectin gel network).
> - **Lower values (< 0.2 N)** suggest the jam is **too firm or over-set** (excess pectin, low moisture).
> - This parameter is also correlated with consumer perception of **"spreadability"** and **"mouthfeel"** in sensory analysis.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Texture Analysis (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the trigger force:** Many students write "$F$ at $t = 0$ is zero." The **trigger force of 0.05 N** is required to detect the surface; the curve **starts at 0.05 N, not 0 N.** (-1 mark in long answers)
>
> 2. **Confusing Cohesiveness and Adhesiveness:** Cohesiveness is the **ratio of areas** ($A_2/A_1$, dimensionless), Adhesiveness is the **magnitude of the negative area** ($A_3$, in mJ). Writing cohesiveness as an area or adhesiveness as a ratio is a **fatal error** (-2 marks).
>
> 3. **Wrong unit for Chewiness:** Chewiness has units of **mJ (energy)**, NOT N (force). It is a triple product of dimensionless ratios times N, hence mJ. Many students incorrectly write "N" or "J" — losing the unit mark.
>
> 4. **Skipping the operating condition of the analyzer:** Always mention **cross-head speed (1 mm/s)**, **probe diameter (50.8 mm)**, and **strain level (25–75%)** in any TPA description question.
>
> 5. **Treating Fracturability as a synonym for Hardness:** They are distinct — fracturability is the **first break**; hardness is the **absolute peak**. A food can have high hardness but no fracturability (e.g., soft bread).
>
> 6. **Forgetting the ratio form of Springiness:** Springiness is $S = d_{\text{recover}} / d_1$, not the absolute distance. Always normalize.
>
> 7. **Missing the TPA "two-bite" rationale:** When asked "what is TPA," students often describe only **one** compression. TPA is fundamentally a **double-compression** procedure; the second compression is what gives **cohesiveness**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Revision Notes — Texture Analysis:**

- **Texture** = mechanical, geometrical, surface, and body attributes perceptible to tactile/visual/auditory receptors (ISO 11036).
- **Szczesniak's (1963) classification** of 5 primary attributes: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Viscosity.
- **TPA = Two-bite simulation test** by Bourne (1978), using a flat cylindrical probe (50.8 mm), cross-head speed **1 mm/s**, strain **25–75%**.
- **Trigger force** = 0.05 N (curve starts here, not at zero).
- **Force–Time curve** consists of 5 phases: 1st compression, 1st upstroke, hold time, 2nd compression, 2nd upstroke.
- **7 Standard TPA Parameters:**
  - $H = F_1$ (N) → Hardness
  - $C = A_2 / A_1$ (dimensionless) → Cohesiveness
  - $\text{Adh} = \vert A_3 \vert$ (mJ) → Adhesiveness
  - $S = d_{\text{recover}} / d_1$ (dimensionless) → Springiness
  - $G = H \times C$ (N) → Gumminess (for semi-solids)
  - $Ch = H \times C \times S$ (mJ) → Chewiness (for solids)
  - $R = A_{\text{up},1} / A_1$ (dimensionless) → Resilience
- **Always quote units:** N for force, m for distance (or mm), mJ for energy, s for time.
- **Work of penetration** $= \int F\, dd$ — calculated by the **trapezoidal rule** in software; equals $\frac{1}{2} F_{\max} d_{\max}$ only for linear elastic materials.
- **Young's Modulus** $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ valid only in the **linear-elastic region** of the stress–strain curve.
- **Strain** $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$, **Stress** $\sigma = F / A_0$, **Strain rate** $\dot{\varepsilon} = v / L_0$ (s⁻¹).
- **Test temperature** must always be standardized ($20 \pm 2^{\circ}\text{C}$ for solids, $4 \pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ for chilled foods).
- **Sample geometry** must be standardized — minimum $1\,\text{cm}^2$ cross-section, $10\,\text{mm}$ height.
- **Fracturability** exists **only if** the force–time curve shows a clear break event before reaching the peak; otherwise it is undefined.
- **Shear tests** (e.g., Warner-Bratzler, Kramer Shear Cell) are used for **meat toughness**, NOT TPA — do not confuse.
- **Back-extrusion** is used for **liquid and semi-liquid foods** (yogurt, sauces) where uniaxial compression is not applicable.
- **Sensory analysis** and **instrumental TPA** are **complementary**, not substitutes; correlation is product-specific and must be established experimentally.
- **Industry applications to remember for 14-mark questions:** bakery (crumb firmness & staling), dairy (cheese ripening), meat (tenderness), fruits (harvest maturity), snacks (crispness), sauces (consistency).
- **Always** mention the **units, probe geometry, and cross-head speed** in any TPA-based question to score full marks.
